### Post by poofyhairguy on 2005-03-01
[QUOTE=jeffsmith8]
**Nautilus, however, I don't think I'll ever warm too.  I just find it so clunky not being able to view the file trees like you can in Konquerer, Xfe, or (gulp!) win explorer**[/QUOTE]

I agree with you that Nautilus in its default spatial mode is one of the worst things about Gnome. I have read tons of explinations of why it is the default or why it is better, but most of them break down to "if you were to start all over with file managers (as in, you have no experiance), this would be a better way to do it." Unfortunatly most users have seen a feature packed file manager before, and the spatial gnome with its tons of open windows seems weak in comparison. Every Gnome box I touch I type 

gconftool-2 --type bool --set /apps/nautilus/preferences/always_use_browser true

to disable Spatial Nautilus and enable the multimillion dollar Nautilus that made me fall in love with Gnome in the first place. I know that Windows converts that I have set-up Ubuntu for like the normal mode a lot more than the Spatial mode. If I forget that line, I always hear something like "I like Ubuntu, but dislike its file window." One line later, and they are commenting how regular Nautilus looks like the best thing since sliced bread.

[QUOTE=jeffsmith8]
P.S:  But what I don't understand is why it is a problem.  Are there actually significant disadvantages to just installing KDE, or enlightenment, or Xfce, or whatever, and using that GUI instead of GNOME?  How fundamentally does this "streamlining" of Ubuntu and Gnome effect the alternative use of KDE?  Is it some coding thing or are the GUI menu defaults the only sore point?   :-s[/QUOTE]

There is not problem. I have installed KDE in Ubuntu. It works, but not as well as I would expect a KDE distro to work (such as USB drives appearing on the desktop- I love that!). I have installed XFCE in Ubuntu. The new version works great with Hoary, and my laptop remains like that every single day.

### Post by az on 2005-03-01
'You started out by saying that Qt was not free because Trolltech could somehow simply take the GPL'd Qt away."

I said that Qt was a commercial product that is currently under a free licence.


"Then you claimed (and still seem to claim) that Trolltech could somehow decide not to release future versions of Qt as free software. Now apart from the fact that this holds true for nearly any software under the GPL, this doesn't hold true for Qt. Again, Tolltech has signed a contract that makes sure that would Trolltech do such a thing any new version of Qt would also be released as free software. So in this sense you are on the safer side using Qt than you are using almost any other GPL'd software."

You are dreaming.  That "contract" only proves that they realize that people have concerns about their pilocies.


"Now as this situation seems to sink in you suggest that Trolltech could release a closed, reworked, renamed library, thereby circumventing the contract they signed. Now if constructing this scenario doesn't speak of desperation, what does?"

Why can't you just be nice?  Yes, this is what I said.

"But let's look at it anyway:
First of all, Trolltech would clearly break a contract, so this situation would be highly problematic for them, to say the least and of course highly unlikely. But even if they did pull this stunt of, there would still be the GPL'd version of Qt.

Ah, but now you claim that the closed, reworked, renamed library released by Trolltech breaking a contract would have some killer features the original GPL'd Qt has not, thereby making all the apps linked to Qt obsolete. This is of course a funny "argument", as this situation isn't different from any opensource software that has a closed source equivalent that is superior. So what exactly does make this problem so terrible with regard to Qt?"

Having an equivalent is completely different than licencing _the same software_ under two licences.


"And now here's your last argument. You seem to assume that the open source community is somehow able to develop a world class operating system, world class tools for it, great toolkits like gtk, great desktop environments like Gnome and Kde but somehow for some freakish reason wouldn't be able to sustain the development of Qt in case that Trolltech doesn't develop it anymore. (Remember, they broke a contract and went closed source  ). Now why this should be the case is beyond me, but I'm sure you'll enlighten me."

You said it yourself.  The open source community did not develop Qt.  A commercial enterprise did.  It is completely feasable.  MacOS X is based on FreeBSD.  They have an open-source project named Darwin.  Can Darwin run Safari?  Does it have all the features of OSX?

No.


[http://developer.apple.com/darwin/](http://developer.apple.com/darwin/)

### Post by TravisNewman on 2005-03-01
Note: part of this post references topics discussed here:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=17369&page=2&pp=40](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=17369&page=2&pp=40)

ralph, are you familiar with the BSD license? Nowhere near as much protection for the user as GPL. THAT is a serious worry for me. If CompanyA decides to grab the source and develop on it, closed source, and goes leaps and bounds, sets standards for QT variants, and obliterates the competition, then that's a major problem. You've said yourself that it could go to a BSD license.

I would like to mention that I think this is a non issue as of now. It may BECOME an issue.

You also ask how their licensing is wish-washy. Well, they have two different licensing methods, one of which being GPL, and if they ever stop development it goes BSD. That sounds wishy-washy to me.

Now lets talk about FUD as a concept.

FUD=Fear, Uncertainty, and Doubt.
If I say I don't like KDE because it's bloated, there are 15 different applications for each task, etc (note that I wouldn't, I don't agree with these points), is that FUD? No. It's an opinion. It may be misinformed, and it may be specific to the user, but it's not FUD. No opinion can be considered FUD, and no misinformation can be considered FUD, unless it's INTENTIONALLY trying to spread fear, uncertainty, and doubt in people. Microsoft using skewed tests to prove that they are cheaper for running servers is FUD. If I were to say "Windows sucks because it's slow, it crashes all the time, it's bloated, etc" most people on these forums would say "yep, you're right" but if I were to say that I hate KDE (which I don't) because it's bloated, slow, and I don't like the licensing, about half the people start screaming FUD. It CAN'T BE BOTH WAYS.

### Post by TravisNewman on 2005-03-03
OK I've gotten konstruct to work much better.  It's just a matter of figuring out what dependencies you need to install that it thinks you have but you don't. A lot of -dev packages that it doesn't resolve, and you don't know you need them until compiling fails. This is the newest error:
/usr/lib/libGL.a(glxcmds.o)(.text+0x2eea): In function `glXGetMscRateOML':
: undefined reference to `XF86VidModeQueryVersion'
/usr/lib/libGL.a(glxcmds.o)(.text+0x2f1a): In function `glXGetMscRateOML':
: undefined reference to `XF86VidModeGetModeLine'

anyone know what I could do to fix this one? I've been racking my brain on this one for a while. It's when it tries to compile kcontrol, part of kdebase.

### Post by Ironi on 2005-03-03
[QUOTE=panickedthumb]
anyone know what I could do to fix this one? I've been racking my brain on this one for a while. It's when it tries to compile kcontrol, part of kdebase.[/QUOTE]
apt-get install libxxf86vm-dev

... and you might have to rerun configure if it's not trying to link against -lXxf86vm -- this is why building from non-Debianized source sucks (no apt-get build-dep whatever). :p

### Post by regeya on 2005-03-03
Again, I leave for a while and find that this thread has degenerated into a flamewar between two sides. :-(

One amusing thing from this thread and others, if you boil down the information:  Qt is both Free and not Free.  GNOME is faster than KDE, while KDE is faster than GNOME.  GNOME is freer than KDE, while KDE is freer than GNOME.  KDE is threatened by the dual-licensing nature of Qt, and though that's not entirely true, it's verboten to discuss the legalities of Mono.

Both sides are right, both sides are wrong.  There is no middle ground, only cold hard facts that support notions that are complete opposites.  :D

The main thing I've gotten from this thread is that people still, STILL seem to think that the whole Qt-KDE thing isn't quite legal.  Well, it is.  Get over it. :D  The real kicker is that Qt is licensed under Troll Tech's own license AND the GPL, which means that non-proprietary software that wants to be Open Source has to comply with Free Software guidelines.  While the GNOME project can argue that it's more free because the base libs are mostly LGPL, it can also be argued that that very issue, the more liberal licensing of core libs, means that GNOME is more opposed to the spirit of Free Software than GNOME.

I don't believe that GNOME is less free than KDE for a minute.  All I believe is that if someone wants to write a proprietary app for KDE, there's a financial obligation, while with GNOME there is not.  That's the big difference in licensing issues for me, and anything else is just splitting hairs.

Now, can we stop arguing about which one's uglier and which one's prettier?  Can I as the instigator of the whole thing ask that we not talk about useless eyecandy?

---

### Post by landotter on 2005-03-08
So if you drive a Citroen with a shifter in the dashboard for many years and trade it in for a Ford you then complain that Ford's incorrectly put the shifter in the floor? :P

My cousins who begged me to install Linux were the same way: it was "wrong to not have just a "start button" on the bottom left. It as wrong to not have trash on the desktop. It was wrong to not have a bunch of launchers on the desktop and not a nicely organized menu...

Brainwashed is what that is.

It's still so much like windows it's rediculous--how hard can it really be to adapt--it's not like it's something more obscure like Enlightenment or a *box window manager.

There's a clicky place to find your programs. There's a toolbar to put launchers on. A window list, clock, and volume applet finish the deal. 

Oh, that's right, they're in different places. Oy.

Bloody sheep.

Do people really want their intelligence insulted by a crappy windows copy?

Yes?

Then Lycoris, or Xandros might be your bag. They're not bad distros--but I do find their UI to be insulting.

If you're too dumb to drag some toolbars around to your liking in Gnome, then stick with windows. Really. I don't mean to be insulting--but if you think that everybody needs to copy poor design to make it palatable to tha masses, then you're entitled to that opinion--and I'll feel entitled to laugh at it. ;)

Baaaah. 

:P

### Post by jdonnell on 2005-03-08

### Post by lgoss007 on 2005-03-16
I used to use KDE (in Mandrake) because it was more appealing to me. I thought Gnome looked ugly and it didn't seem to have as much cool eye candy and also seemed to function a little awkward. The default icons seemed bland and kinda dark, as opposed to KDE's brighter icons. It also seemed to handle transparency on things a lot better. But I was unhappy with the updates for Mandrake (and I was part of club), and rpm's were always giving me a hassle.

I did a major distro test where I tested out about 10-15 different distributions and I was down to Mandrake, Mepis, Fedora, and Ubuntu. Of course I stayed away from Ubuntu at first because it was Gnome based, but then I gave it another try and I really liked it... after I fixed up Gnome. :grin: 

> ... but I find konqueror much nicer than nautilus. However, I LOVE some of the kde apps like k3b and kate. The combination of kate and vim is the perfect development environment for me (I do php/python all day).

Nautilus I didn't like at first because of some of the default options (like spatial). I think it's nice Gnome supports different ways of browsing because different users have different preferences. Gedit seems to be just as good as kate to me and there has already been mention of an alternative to k3b.

One thing I really like about Ubuntu is the constant updates to things like nvidia drivers, xorg, gnome, etc. Of course I'm runny Hoary which I guess things will change when it's released and updates will slow down. So I think there needs to be an Ubuntu-Edge distribution, that always has the latest software. Oh and I do like that Gnome is cleaner...

### Post by dusu on 2005-03-22
I do not think having two separated versions of Ubuntu, namely Ubuntu and KUbuntu is a problem, for the following reasons:

  i) it's a fact that there are two main Desktop environments, Gnome and KDE, so that a distribution with the goals that Ubuntu has, should provide both.
It should also avoid to provide any Desktop, because the same kind of arguments would lead to providing any program whatsoever...
  ii) any one can take a decision of what Desktop to install, before he/she actually performs the install .
After performing the large step which is to come to the linux world, any one can make the small step to choose a Desktop !
There is thus no special need to provide both KDE *and* Gnome in one unified distribution
  iii) some people worry because in distrowatch, the "split" Ubuntu/KUbuntu could do some arm to the stats...
Of course distrowatch is a nice tool, but it just gives some infos.
And anyway, Ubuntu is not nice because it's written somewhere that it's nice !! Ubuntu is nice because *it is nice*  :mrgreen: 

As for the question Gnome/KDE, I'll stick to Gnome now that I've got it installed (and I anyway have KDE at my working place !)

---

### Post by ember on 2005-03-22

I don't mind having Kubuntu as an Ubuntu spinoff, but I don't think it should become more than that.

### Post by kassetra on 2005-03-22
[QUOTE=lao_V]Would you continue using Ubuntu or do you like Kubuntu better? Or do you think Ubutnu/Kubuntu should be a single distro?[/QUOTE]

One of the things that makes Ubuntu so great is the single Desktop Environment approach.  The last thing most users need is a bloated, 4-disc install that takes 2 hours and lots of mind-glazing questions.

Think of it this way.  I want to make a pitcher of Koolaid.  If I follow the instructions exactly, I get flavored sugar water.  If I don't add the whole two quarts of water, and maybe not an entire cup of sugar, I get a more intense flavor in my flavored sugar water.  If I add a gallon of water instead of the two quarts that the instructions say to add, I get watered-down, very little flavored sugar water.
 That is how distributions work. 

Debian = making Koolaid according to the instructions.  You get flavored sugar water.

Ubuntu = a more intensely flavored Debian.  heh.  

Most other distributions, (including the idea of combining Ubuntu/Kubuntu) = watered down Koolaid.

The more you add to a distribution, the less of a "flavor" you get.  Ubuntu and Kubuntu should stay separate distributions.

---

### Post by ember on 2005-03-22

Choice does not always mean additional selection. Choice can also mean a distro that focuses on one DE unapologetically. I _have_ a choice between several, single GUI focused distro's (Ubuntu, Linspire, etc.) and several multi-focused disto's (Fedora, MDK, SUSE). 

Having tried most of the main ones, I appreciate the choice of Ubuntu.  They've chosen what I want- simplicity.

Please, Canonical- let other companies develop KDE and multi-GUI based distro's. Stick to Gnome alone.

Hey- a movie about a kid who gets left at home with nothing but a computer! "Gnome Alone"! ;-)

---

### Post by poofyhairguy on 2005-03-22
[QUOTE=kassetra]The more you add to a distribution, the less of a "flavor" you get.  Ubuntu and Kubuntu should stay separate distributions.[/QUOTE]

Its not that simple. Kubuntu is a subset of Ubuntu. Kubuntu existed before the project officially started, in the sense that the universe KDE worked fairly well. As soon as Warty was released, I thought that the kubuntu project would be a logical next step. Kubuntu is not a separate distro. Kubuntu is an unofficial part of ubuntu that has been there from the begining. The project leaders of kubuntu are adding polish and hamming out an unofficial part of ubuntu. They are marketing the fact Ubuntu has KDE, saying "yes this is a gnome distro, but it is flexible enough to be a KDE distro too." We'll even support it separately. The main ubuntu devs continue to work on a very polished Gnome desktop to rule the world. All the work is found on the same server.

They are one. The effect (as in difference from warty's silent kubuntu) is a more polished, unsupported (by the ubuntu developers) desktop environment that many people happen to like. Kubuntu will make sure the main ubuntu devs don't to anything that keeps kde from not running, but they will work on the bugs inbetween.

No steam is lost in the Ubuntu Movement. In fact, more is added by knowledgable KDE users that are being attracted to Ubuntu. Do not forget that jdong, the kind soul that has eliminated many a complaint about Ubuntu is primarily a KDE user. 

I've tried kubuntu, and its my favorite KDE distro ever. I plan to run it on my slow laptop because KDE is lighter than Gnome. My desktop will stay Gnome, if only because the eyecandy is the only worthwhile thing my P4 does!

---

### Post by kassetra on 2005-03-22
[QUOTE=panickedthumb]Though, Kassetra was talking about adding it into one distribution (as in, having them both on the cd(s)). And I might add, that's a great analogy, Kass! I giggled.

The development model of Ubuntu/Kubuntu is a great one. The corporate employees focus on one DE, the volunteers focus on another, and they integrate their work. That way you don't have lunacy.[/QUOTE]

Right, I meant the single cd "distribution" of Ubuntu.

An example of what I mean is RedHat 9 - they worked very hard to combine KDE and Gnome into their install, but what many users were left with is a system with too much bloat (because of the "crossover" libraries) - and systems that had other conflicting issues.

### Post by fizgig on 2005-03-22
I'm afraid I'm addicted to the KDE apps like smb4k, k3b, kppp and konqueror - oh yeah, and a MENU EDITOR! :)  I used the Gnome desktop for awhile and it was ok but I had trouble getting smb4k and kppp working.  I'm sure I was doing something wrong but I didn't know what.  I had chosen Gnome since it was completely free but after rethinking the dual-license concept out, that argument doesn't hold as much power over me now...

I literally couldn't sleep for a couple hours last week when I was trying to make up my mind of what to do (I get like that when I'm in a middle of a project).  Should I revert to Mepis and resign myself to be 8 months back in time in the overall release cycle?  Should I try straight Debian out?  When I investigated kubuntu and tried it out, the smoke cleared.  All the apps above worked perfectly and I got a slick looking interface - all on top of a ubuntu system that I had already tailored (ndiswrapper, powernowd tweaks and so forth).

Gnome was great for awhile but the lack of polish affects people like me used to a slick looking OS like XP and who have the processing power and memory to support it.

Anyhow, if I change my mind again, I can simply select Gnome on my next boot so I'm very happy with the flexibility the Ubuntu family of software has provided.

Just my two cents.   =;

### Post by lao_V on 2005-03-24
[QUOTE=jwb]Choice does not always mean additional selection. Choice can also mean a distro that focuses on one DE unapologetically. I _have_ a choice between several, single GUI focused distro's (Ubuntu, Linspire, etc.) and several multi-focused disto's (Fedora, MDK, SUSE). 

Having tried most of the main ones, I appreciate the choice of Ubuntu.  They've chosen what I want- simplicity.

Please, Canonical- let other companies develop KDE and multi-GUI based distro's. Stick to Gnome alone.

Hey- a movie about a kid who gets left at home with nothing but a computer! "Gnome Alone"! ;-)[/QUOTE]

I suppose it depends how you interpret choice. As a distributor, do you make a choice of what you would like to distribute or do you want to relegate that choice to yours users and provide everything they would want?

In my interpretation of the open source/linux software, I feel that the user should be in the driving seat and they should be able to chose everything there is to chose rather then someone else making the descisions for you. If you let someone else chose all the time then there may come a point when you don't agree with their choice for whatever reason. What will you do then? Switch?

Why do you think they are called distributions? Cuz they are meant to distribute what Linux has to offer. If distributor 1 decides to include A-G, distributor 2 includes H-V and distributor 3 includes W-Z, then there will never be a complete UNIX system for people who wants to have A-Z. 

Lets have a freedom of choice. Not only between distributions but also within!

I love Ubuntu and I love KDE. I can have best of both worlds with Ubuntu packaged as Kubuntu. I have no problem with Kubuntu being a subset of Ubuntu. Like a flavour in Debian sense. But marketing Kubuntu as a seperate distro is something I feel is technically correct.

### Post by wk1989 on 2005-03-24
[QUOTE=kassetra]One of the things that makes Ubuntu so great is the single Desktop Environment approach.  The last thing most users need is a bloated, 4-disc install that takes 2 hours and lots of mind-glazing questions.

Think of it this way.  I want to make a pitcher of Koolaid.  If I follow the instructions exactly, I get flavored sugar water.  If I don't add the whole two quarts of water, and maybe not an entire cup of sugar, I get a more intense flavor in my flavored sugar water.  If I add a gallon of water instead of the two quarts that the instructions say to add, I get watered-down, very little flavored sugar water.
 That is how distributions work. 

Debian = making Koolaid according to the instructions.  You get flavored sugar water.

Ubuntu = a more intensely flavored Debian.  heh.  

Most other distributions, (including the idea of combining Ubuntu/Kubuntu) = watered down Koolaid.

The more you add to a distribution, the less of a "flavor" you get.  Ubuntu and Kubuntu should stay separate distributions.[/QUOTE]
 I personally prefer gnome.

### Post by LongTooth on 2005-03-25
The simplicity and elegance of Gnome is the reason I use it. I have to admit that when I first started with Linux, KDE was the desktop I used. But at that time I didn't know any better :). After quite a lot of time using KDE I decided to try Gnome and I haven't looked back since. Its appealing. Its functional. It does what you want cleanly and without fuss. And it does it without getting in the way..  

Choice is something Linux is well know for and with so many KDE centric distros out there surely we Gnomer (Gnomistas?) should have one of our own. This also keep the bloat down to a manageable level. 

Because Linux is about choice, KDE should be a DE one can download from the repositories as it is in Debian. But all focus should primarily be kept on Gnome.

### Post by bailout on 2005-03-25
Just added my vote for kde. The problem with such a poll on here is that most users are obviously using ubuntu and hence like gnome. I tried unbuntu last year and was put off exactly because it used gnome. I have only come back because of kubuntu.

I saw a kde vs gnome poll on the Opera linux board that had 60% kde and 20% gnome. As the opera board is completely independent of any distro I think it gives a much better indication of the popularity of the two desktops than this poll.

Having read more about the aims of ubuntu I am struggling to understand why they decided to use gnome in the first place. Kde is much better suited because of its user-friendliness and is associated k friendly applications. If ubuntu want to push one choice and build a set of applications tied into that rather than the traditional multiple choice distros then kde would have been a much better choice to start with.

### Post by kassetra on 2005-03-25
[QUOTE=bailout]
Having read more about the aims of ubuntu I am struggling to understand why they decided to use gnome in the first place. Kde is much better suited because of its user-friendliness and is associated k friendly applications. If ubuntu want to push one choice and build a set of applications tied into that rather than the traditional multiple choice distros then kde would have been a much better choice to start with.[/QUOTE]

Well, that is your opinion of KDE.  Many users do not share that opinion.  Ubuntu chose gnome, because *they* felt it was the best choice for their goals.  Obviously they have a different opinion about the desktop environments than you do.

The point being - if you don't like Gnome but want to use Ubuntu, either use Ubuntu and then install KDE yourself, or use Kubuntu.

Making comments as to which desktop environment is better and why it should have been chosen instead of X is an exercise in futility.

### Post by XDevHald on 2005-04-10

### Post by Brunellus on 2005-04-10
What, no love for Fluxbox users?  or IceWM and Enlightenment and Xfce, for that matter?

I'm happy to keep ubuntu and kubuntu as separate projects--fewer things to download, fewer choices in the install.  I agree with whomever posted that for newbies, no description of KDE or gnome is adequate to lead them to an informed preference one way or the other. 

My computer runs gnome 2.8 in warty fine;  but it's a bit sluggish.  I don't have much RAM to play with, and so I have found that running fluxbox is far more responsive than gnome.  Did ubuntu make this difficult?  No.   Installed , configured, and ran fluxbox without much trouble.  It was great.

Gnome's nice.  KDE's nice.  both work in ubuntu (apparently).  So do flux, icewm, and xfce.  I'd keep ubuntu as it is, but add lots of nice wikipages and howtos about setting up different windowmanagers and/or desktop environments (and, importantly, WHY you would want to do such a thing).

### Post by Klin'Targ on 2005-04-12

The main reason I have settled on KDE for the time being is that it is much harder to configure gnome to have some kde-isms than it is to make kde have some gnome-isms.

### Post by DougInKY on 2005-04-12

I take it this wasn't designed for former Windoze users to start using Linux for the first time.  I'm using synaptic to install KDE as I'm writing this, but I thought I'd just give my thoughts instead of breaking stuff around the room.[/QUOTE]

I have Ubuntu 4.10 installed on my laptop and left it there as it was (to date) the most stable, fastest linux I had ever used on that poor tired old machine. I really tried to like Gnome, really I did. But even after giving it the old college try for all this time it is with a relief that I am downloading Kubuntu right now. Yes, I know that I could have installed KDE on my older Ubuntu but that is beside the point as I really wanted to give Gnome a chance. I guess that I am just used to KDE and all the little things I can do in it. I also must admit a fondness for eye-candy.

I don't dislike Gnome after this, I just prefer KDE. It was good for me in any event as before this I had no idea if I had a desktop preference other than detesting FVWM (left over from my teething days with Red Hat 3 or 4, I don't remember for sure - that was a long time ago).

I expect that Ubuntu/Kubuntu will be replacing SuSE on my desktop now too. I guess I will get to see how it installs with a dual head machine, wish me luck.

Doug in Kentucky

---

### Post by mbuel76 on 2005-04-13
[QUOTE=joplass]I am glad to be running Ubuntu these days because to me Kde looks more like windows.  Running Linux is running Gnome, my personal opinion tho.  After I installed Ubuntu I also ran to Synaptic for a Kde installation without even giving a chance to Gnome.  Then I decided to go back and try Gnome.  Since then I am all Gnome.  I was even thinking about taking Kde out but Konqueror is too good for files and folders browsing.  Distros I used before Ubuntu did not make Gnome look or feel the way it is with Ubuntu.  But I guess we all feel comfortable with some things but not others.[/QUOTE]
 I don't get the whole problem with it "looking like windows" by default.  Do you think that the problem with windows is it's interface?  it's certainly not the best in the world for a GUI but it's better than alot of the crap out there.

it may shock you that some people actually like the windows interface but not microsoft's politics, or other portions of windows.  KDE "looks" like the windows interface at first glance but upon digging deeper, you'll see that it's MUCH MUCH improved and with the QT-GTK gidget in KDE 3.4 I'd say it's far more polished looking (esp in Kubuntu with the trash on the panel.)

here's my problems with gnome:

no centralized control center.  (the kde control center is AWESOME.)

the default icons are NOT pretty or elegant.  The crystal icon set (IMO) rocks.

compare GTK apps to QT apps.... QT apps LOOK and FEEL more polished.

compare gnucash to Kmymoney.
compare GAIM to Kopete.  (while I use GAIM in KDE it's not because it has a better interface, but because it has a better option set.)

as for some of the other comments in here that people should have to adapt to the computer?  the computer should adapt to the user, NEVER the other way around, and this is another problem I have with the "gnome" philosophy.

Look at the filemanager fiasco with it's default behaviour to open a new window for a folder click vs opening in the same window.  and then people complained about the lack of an option to change it back to the more intelligent way of doing it (win 95 and mac OS 7 way of opening a window for folder clicks a good thing?  what are the GNOME devs thinking?) the gnome devs told the community "use it that way and you'll like it"

I like the ubuntu philosophy.... dislike the GNOME philosophy.

dispell some other KDE myths:

[http://kdemyths.urbanlizard.com/](http://kdemyths.urbanlizard.com/)

---

### Post by Brunellus on 2005-04-13
[QUOTE=mbuel76]I don't get the whole problem with it "looking like windows" by default.  Do you think that the problem with windows is it's interface?  it's certainly not the best in the world for a GUI but it's better than alot of the crap out there.[/QUOTE]

Ding!  And a more familiar environment lowers the barrier to entry for the timid. 

(bear in mind, I'm a gnome/fluxbox user, so)
> compare GAIM to Kopete.  (while I use GAIM in KDE it's not because it has a better interface, but because it has a better option set.)

Friendly advice:  when you're making an argument, don't go shoot yourself in the foot like that.  If Kopete were all around better than Gaim, then it would have been worth mentioning.  But Kopete succeeded in doing nothing but annoying the living bejeezus out of me when I used it.  (It also largely turned my little brother off Linux, since he'd been used to AIM and couldn't get used to Kopete, either.  He's a bit more sanguine about Gaim now, having seen me use it lots, but Kopete greatly increased his irritation with the whole Linux experience.  It'll take a lot of coaxing to get him to try Linux again)

I actually prefer gnome to kde, but I don't hate kde users for it.  That Ubuntu and Kubuntu exist and co-exist makes me happy;  the nice thing about ubuntu is that it gave me a pretty good default DE, and when I decided that wasn't quite what I needed, it also made it very easy for me to change that (running fluxbox now).

### Post by mbuel76 on 2005-04-14
[QUOTE=Brunellus]Ding!  And a more familiar environment lowers the barrier to entry for the timid. 

(bear in mind, I'm a gnome/fluxbox user, so)


Friendly advice:  when you're making an argument, don't go shoot yourself in the foot like that.  If Kopete were all around better than Gaim, then it would have been worth mentioning.  But Kopete succeeded in doing nothing but annoying the living bejeezus out of me when I used it.  (It also largely turned my little brother off Linux, since he'd been used to AIM and couldn't get used to Kopete, either.  He's a bit more sanguine about Gaim now, having seen me use it lots, but Kopete greatly increased his irritation with the whole Linux experience.  It'll take a lot of coaxing to get him to try Linux again)

I actually prefer gnome to kde, but I don't hate kde users for it.  That Ubuntu and Kubuntu exist and co-exist makes me happy;  the nice thing about ubuntu is that it gave me a pretty good default DE, and when I decided that wasn't quite what I needed, it also made it very easy for me to change that (running fluxbox now).[/QUOTE]
 kopete, looks better because of QT, regardless of it's operation... and I would say it does *some* things better, like giving you a button that links you to the sign up page for each account.  (there's no way to access the new account option from GAIM) 

here's what I don't like in Kopete:

All the protocol icons look the same and don't look as polished as those in the latest GAIM.

The file transfers in kopete don't work.

The avatar ...  it's just dissapointing to see the other persons avatar the size of a button on the button toolbar.

it used to default to SHIFT + ENTER to send a message... this is stupid.

here's the one thing that I don't like about GAIM - (which is a subset of GNOME and GTK apps it seems (from the ones I've used) the options are not laid out in a clear manner, there is a screen for accounts, but it's not nearly as easy to use as the kopete account interaction screen.  It's also on a different screen from the rest of the GAIM options which again are laid out in a bizarre illogical fashion.  It works but it's not as *clean* looking as kopete's option interface.  honestly the best chat program would be a mixing of the two programs.

I like how minimalistic GNOME and KDE are in Ubuntu, and Kubuntu.  Only the programs you need by default are installed.  I think one thing it needs to do to really attract a more mainstream (bad term?) audience is include a couple of games out of the box default.

It's great to have the choice between gnome, kde, fluxbox, xfce and the variety of other WM's/DE's.  (as MasonM stated.)

### Post by justinflavin on 2005-04-14
well , the "kids" idea is based on my experience on tweaking a default Ubuntu install for my 3 year old - all the "kid" stuff is available on Synaptic, but since there's thousands of packages , you have to google around to find out the names of the packages. [I found loads of stuff](http://linuxnotes.blogspot.com/2005/04/linux-for-kids.html) which i posted on my blog.

I was then looking for a cartoony icon theme, and i found it [here](http://www.gnome-look.org/content/show.php?content=13527) - admittedly , its not available on the Ubuntu repositories (although it might be on Hoary Hedgehog , i'm on Warty) , but at the end of it , i was very surprised at how i could turn a Ubuntu desktop into something my 3 year old would love and use...

heck  of a lot of tweaking to get it to that stage, which got me thinking on the whole "kids" Ubuntu version... could be a means to get Ubuntu into schools maybe.

### Post by nautilus on 2005-04-15
[QUOTE=kassetra]In my experience, it's worked like this:

People that have only one app for the job and tweak their system so that it's "clean" or that use a mac love gnome.
People that install multiple applications for the same purpose and have a giant "start menu" tend to prefer kde.[/QUOTE]

^- oh that's fair

it's easy; remove all Gnome applications, your menu gets a lot smaller.

gnome is slowly catching up to kde in interoperability, but it's simply not there yet.  and yeah, those dialogues...

Gnome, in my experience, has always been for somebody who knows what they're doing, because it rarely works as-is.  Ubuntu does a good job of packaging it, granted, but most Gnome apps require configuring and/or tweaking to "feel" right.

i fire up kdevelop, and with 5 mouse clicks i can commit all the source to Subversion (via KIO), and update it.  Or publish it.  Anyway, I used Anjuta before switching to KDE, and it's a fossil.  It's meant to run in anything, so you can't drag/drop anything into it.

### Post by 23meg on 2005-05-19
- it has better looks, by default and when tweaked to hell
- it has one dialog, one app, one setting etc. for each purpose, not five for each; it's less bloated
- it's more tidy; everything in its right place
- it's less Windows-wannabe (it still is, just less)
- nautilus vs. konqueror
- gtk vs. qt
- kde reminds me of Fedora and Redhat
- kde reminds me of Windows
- kde is just...bad.

as you can tell by the last few reasons, it all comes down to taste, habits, personal preferences. please don't take anyone's word, try and see which one you find more comfortable with. also remember that [there are other options than gnome and kde](http://www.plig.org/xwinman/).

### Post by Stormy Eyes on 2005-05-19
[QUOTE=wowbuntu]Dear Ubuntu people
I have questions....

Why do u like Gnome more than KDE etc.?

And those into KDE,

Why do u like KDE more than Gnome etc.?[/QUOTE]

Asking such a question is no substitute for installing both and trying them yourself. If you installed vanilla Ubuntu, then **sudo apt-get install kde** will get you KDE, and **sudo apt-get install gnome** will get you GNOME. Use them both, and decide for yourself which is better for *you*.

Don't ask *me* which is better; I'll tell you that what I like best is better, and give my reasons for liking it when you ask me why my solution is better.

### Post by Knome_fan on 2005-05-20
*Sigh* not again please...

I made a promise to myself not to participate in threads like this anymore, but I just have to answer some of the things said here:

@kvidell:
"IE the whole Konqueror <=> Safari mess with Mac. They could have benefited, but they didn't think Macs code was "perfect" even though it worked better."
I'll try to stay calm, but things like this really make me angry. The situation with KHTML and Safari really is a little more complicated than you seem to believe. For a good explanation of what is going on from a KHTML devs point of view, look here:
[http://www.kdedevelopers.org/node/view/1049](http://www.kdedevelopers.org/node/view/1049)

In short, don't believe every /. blurb you read...

What exactly are you talking about?

"and it's slow."
Actually no, it isn't.
When did you last try it?

@pdk001:
"the reason to get gnome is different interface against windows OS rather than KDE"
Meaning what exactly? That it comes with two panels, one at the top, one at the bottom?

### Post by Knome_fan on 2005-05-20
> 
I commend you, however, for replying to something that bugs you in a non flaming matter. I wish more people could do that.
Thanks, it's probably just me getting old though. ;-D

> 
Mine may have been a little flamey I guess(the original), sorry.

It wasn't really bad, no, so no need to appologize.
But just to make clear what made me react to your post in the first place (and I hope I'm not taking this to off topic).
I really feel that the KHTML devs are getting a bum deal here.
First off all, following KHTML as a user for some time now I really have to say that they are doing fantastic work here. Konqueror has turned from something I wouldn't touch with a ten foot pole for web browsing into a browser I feel good about using as my main browser.

However, all the devs get out of their great work is flames and not even a thank you.
On the one hand people live under the false impression that all the KHTML devs have to do is implement the improvements made by Apple handed to them on the silver plate. As should be clear now after this whole discussion, nothing could be further from the truth. However, the minute Apple implements something into Safari the devs get flamed for not incorporating it promptly into KHTML. I can only imagine how frustrating this must be.

On the other hand the KHTML devs don't get nearly as much credit for their work as they deserve. As I already said, KHTML has improved greatly, yet most people do of course think that this is not due to the devs' hard work, but simply a matter of integrating stuff from Apple, which again isn't true.

So this leaves the KHTML devs in a position where they get flamed for not implementing things from Apple, though they are in no position to simply do so and on top of that they don't get credit for their work. Really unfair if you ask me.

Finally, there are, I think, very good reasons for not doing what the firefox dev you refer to suggested. Simply incorporating Apple stuff, without having a way of really testing it or even know what it does in the first place surely would create a mess that would make KHTML unmanagable. So not doing this, but instead insisting on keeping KHTML sane and managable sure is a good thing, imho.

---

[QUOTE=Knome_fan][ ... ][/QUOTE]I understand and agree with the fact that they're being shat on for... generally crappy reasons.
They have worked hard and made a full, complete, functional mostly clean product, and as of late have only been taking heat for it for something that's not completely their fault.

My opinion though is that for a long time the KDE dev team as a whole, while undeniably talented and hard working, focuses their resources on the wrong goals.

My opinion kind of goes in a sad loop, I guess. hehe.

### Post by kassetra on 2005-05-21
Understanding that each computing system GUI is a matter of personal choice, and an individuals' mode of comprehension - I clearly respect an individual's preference.

My preferences, are as follows:

[list]
[*]Gnome gives me a good base in which to start my customizations   
[*]I prefer the simplicity with regard to how I make changes to the overall appearance.   
[*]I like being able to fine-tune & choose options by way of a standardized approach   
[*]The general Gnome defaults match my idea of a sane organization scheme.   
[*]The HIG is awesome.   
[*]I like the flexibility and the multitudes of choices - right down to the window manager - but I appreciate the fact that I'm not bombarded with all options all at once. 
[/list] For my personal use, Gnome fits all of my criteria of what a good desktop should be.  :)

### Post by nuopus on 2005-05-21
Lots of the top rated programs are GTK. Examples are Firefox, Gimp, Abiword, GNUmeric. QT has SOME good apps, but mostly people ending up running GTK apps in their QT based KDE. QT based apps cannot compare ... KOffice are you kidding me?

Aside from the GTK vs QT thing, the environment itself boils down to this:

Use KDE if:

a. You are new to Linux and want a Windows-ish feel,
b. You know nothing about Linux and come from the Windows world.
c. You love overly complex menus just to perform one simple task.
d. You love to play hide and seek with a configuration setting.
e. You like the trouble of wading through useless things.
f. You want a user interface to give you "slowness" in your preparation to go back to Windows ... like an underwater diver must rise from depth slowly.


Use GNOME if:

a. You are comfortable with leaving the confines of a Windows-ish feel GUI.
b. You know something about Linux and want GTK applications that work.
c. You love menus that make sense so that you can find things easier.
d. You don't like to play hide and seek with a configuration setting.
e. You dont want to be bothered by 10000 useless programs that you have no choice of installing.
f. You are moving from a Windows environment and you want speed benefit.


Anyhoo ... I will leave the decision to the user. To each his own. Personally though, I hate KDE with a passion, and hope that more top knotch GTK programs like Firefox and Gimp get created to shadow the QT based apps. Let the flaming begin....

### Post by mrtaber on 2005-05-21
I'm relatively new to the Linux world (after spending most of my working life in the mainframe/DOS/Windows/Mac OS X world).  I'm all about the choice, and I'm very thankful for it.  

To me it's a strength of Linux that people can actually choose which environment works best for them.  Me, I'm a Gnome (and XFCE after they fix the awful file manager) man.  I wouldn't turn down an otherwise good distro just because it was KDE based, but I wouldn't be as happy.  

I like very subdued desktops and widgets.  I'm a long-time Mac user, and even though Mac OS X is an incredible leap forward over Apple's previous OS, the "lickable" interface drives me crazy.  First thing I do on my Apples at work is turn on the Graphite color scheme instead of Aqua.  What's nice looking for 5-10-15 minutes can really grate when looking at the screen all day.  The whole point of that seemingly pointless excursus, I guess, is that I like Gnome's subtlety.  

But I'm glad that we have the choice, no matter which side of the fence one falls on.

Mark :)

### Post by nuopus on 2005-05-22
[QUOTE=jerome bettis]excellent post i couldn't have said it any better. i was going to answer knome-fan's reply to my post, but you did it for me.

oh and you can use the gnome-panel in xfce. just kill the xfce panel: sudo killall xfce4-panel and start the gnome one with gnome-panel.[/QUOTE]

You can use whatever combination you want. Just run gnome-panel in your .xinitrc file and have whatever program you will "log off" from be the last item.

For example I use: in my .xinitrc:
```

lineakd &
gnome-session

```

The reason I put lineakd first with an & after it is when it comes to execute the commands it may stop at lineakd and not load gnome-session until its over. Specifying the & says to background the task and continue with the script. If you were to put gnome-session first witha &, then when you hit log out from the desktop menu, it will not quit, but exit to the only process keeping the session .. which is lineakd or whatever it may be. Hope this helps!

### Post by nuopus on 2005-05-22
[QUOTE=chadauld]I couldn't agree more..23meg nailed it.  I started with Gnome, switched to KDE, and I've come back.[/QUOTE]

Well did you at least use debfoster to make it easy for you to uninstall KDE?

For those that havent heard of it, install and run debfoster and say yes to everything that you want to keep. It will keep track of the selections.

Then install anything you want including kubuntu and all of the kde apps you want just to try it out, and after you are done ... just debfoster again. It will ask you if you want to keep the NEW packages that were installed after your first debfoster. Saying no to everything will get rid of all that was installed after the first time you ran the utility.

This may be a good way to try KDE if you have never seen it before. Just make sure you have an easy uninstall path because there are lots of packages with KDE.

### Post by Ironi on 2005-05-24
[QUOTE=nuopus]Lots of the top rated programs are GTK.[/QUOTE]
Only because GTK's licensing is more liberal than Qt's. I doubt very much that, if the licensing were equal, the situation would be the same. Qt and KDE are much easier to program for than GTK.

> 
a. You are new to Linux and want a Windows-ish feel,

a. You are comfortable with leaving the confines of a Windows-ish feel GUI.

The problem with KDE is that its default look resembles Windows. However, that can be easily changed. With GNOME, however, you're confined to same basic look -- and if you don't like it, tough.

> 
b. You know nothing about Linux and come from the Windows world.

b. You know something about Linux and want GTK applications that work.

Wait a second... I thought the that the whole *point* of choosing GNOME over KDE is because GNOME is easier to learn for people coming from Windows? Are you saying that GNOME has a steeper learning curve than KDE?

> 
c. You love overly complex menus just to perform one simple task.

c. You love menus that make sense so that you can find things easier.

Compared to Windows' menu, KDE's is wonderful -- organized, complete, and very customizable. GNOME's, on the other hand, is organized too, but ... well, somewhat lacking.

> 
d. You love to play hide and seek with a configuration setting.

d. You don't like to play hide and seek with a configuration setting.

You still have to do that with GNOME, at least for advanced settings (those few that are present, anyways). It's just the difference between searching in kcontrol or a preferences dialog vs. searching in reged...er gconf-editor.

> 
e. You like the trouble of wading through useless things.

e. You dont want to be bothered by 10000 useless programs that you have no choice of installing.

As far as I can tell, every KDE application can be installed and removed separately. Perhaps the metapackages install too many, but that can be fixed by the distributor.

> 
f. You want a user interface to give you "slowness" in your preparation to go back to Windows ... like an underwater diver must rise from depth slowly.

f. You are moving from a Windows environment and you want speed benefit.

Please. :rolleyes: A lot of apps tend to run faster under a properly-functioning Windows installation (including Firefox). That aside, I've seen people state that KDE is faster than GNOME and vice versa. It's all subjective.

---

Since when has Windows become the holy grail of usability? GNOME has great usability without having to emulate Windows. The whole point of choosing GNOME over KDE is because its usable for both advanced and new users.

### Post by Ironi on 2005-05-24
[QUOTE=Mike Douglas]Since when has Windows become the holy grail of usability?
[/QUOTE]
Where did I say that?

> 
GNOME has great usability without having to emulate Windows. The whole point of choosing GNOME over KDE is because its usable for both advanced and new users.
Oh boy, here we go with that nebulous "usability" word again... Okay, let's first see if we can agree on this definition:

> [http://dictionary.reference.com/search?q=usability](http://dictionary.reference.com/search?q=usability)

<programming> The effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction
with which users can achieve tasks in a particular environment
of a product. High usability means a system is: easy to learn
and remember; efficient, visually pleasing and fun to use; and
quick to recover from errors.


I'm not sure whether **any** desktop interface currently satisfies all of those criteria ("quick to recover from errors!?"). For me, KDE seems to satisfy most of them, but that's just me. What universal rule says that GNOME beats KDE in the usability area? For that matter, what happened to the old issues such as **features** and **extent of customization**? KDE beats GNOME hands down in those areas.

---

### Post by poofyhairguy on 2005-05-24
[QUOTE=Ironi]What universal rule says that GNOME beats KDE in the usability area? [/QUOTE]

Nothing does. They both have their advantages, and their quirks.

[QUOTE=Ironi]For that matter, what happened to the old issues such as **features** and **extent of customization**? KDE beats GNOME hands down in those areas.[/QUOTE]

Maybe, maybe not. I would say that Gnome is very configurable, but unlike KDE you might have to edit some text files to do it. That doesn't really bug me personally.

This a pointless debate. Some people like Gnome better (me), some people like KDE better (you). Since they are very different in many ways, no one can be better for all people. Its the apples and oranges thing all over again.

I'm glad both exist. I'm glad Ubuntu has both. I use Gnome- I love it. I can see why someone would like KDE though. But no one can prove one is better than the other.

unless this thread gets a more positive tone (which might be hard considering its subject matter) consider it locked in the near future.

### Post by jsimmons on 2005-06-28
[QUOTE=azz]Actually, it is a much improved way to use your computer.  If people want windows, they should stick with windows.[/QUOTE]

It's not a matter of "wanting windows' - it's a matter of not wanting to go into GUI shock.  I'm the same way. I generally like the look and feel of Windows (which I think is actually superior to Linux).  It's the hassles involved and philosophical objection to using Windows that has pushed me into Linux. If Ididn't have so much money tied up in my PC's, I'd buy an Apple and be done with it.

You don't have to admit that Windows does a lot of stuff better than Linux if you don't want to, but if you don't, you can't help Linux to be at least as good (or even better) at those same things.

It's all in how you look at it.

### Post by jsimmons on 2005-06-28

People that have only one app for the job and tweak their system so that it's "clean" or that use a mac love gnome.
People that install multiple applications for the same purpose and have a giant "start menu" tend to prefer kde.[/QUOTE]

That's a pretty narrow-minded statement.  I prefer the look and feel of KDE, but want the sparse menus found in Gnome.   I don't want a dozen web browsers installed on my system, or 17 different ways to read email.  As an example, I've uninstalled Evolution in deference to Thunderbird.

### Post by Rule on 2005-06-28

### Post by blueturtl on 2005-07-07
I like Gnome. IMO a window manager should make things easier and more graphic compared to using a commandline and that's exactly what Gnome does. KDE is just way too cluttered for me.. reminds me of Windows. Oh yeah, and that bloody web browser on the UI! This has to be one of the stupidest things ever conceived: having a web browser do file management, but there it is in Windows and it's being copied to a lot of Linux desktops. Ubuntu was a pleasant surprise. Here the web browser (Firefox or any other browser of your choice) does the browsing and Nautilus handles the file management. Now I don't know if Nautilus has web browsing capability but thankfully the folks developing Ubuntu have atleast gone as far as to totally disable/hide it from my view. Gnome is also very aesthetically pleasing and clean. If simplicity is the key, Gnome is the way to go as far as I'm concerned. If you don't like simple, you switch window managers - that's the beauty of Linux.  ;-)

---

### Post by poptones on 2005-07-07
*You don't have to admit that Windows does a lot of stuff better than Linux if you don't want to, but if you don't, you can't help Linux to be at least as good (or even better) at those same things.*

I was a windows power user for quite a long time. I used the develop tools and contributed what I was able to avisynth, I used jscript and could write some *really* advanced stuff using their shell scripting tools to create dlls and all sorts of things. 

I can't think of a single thing windows does better than linux. It does a lot of things differently, but nothing better. I have a USB hard drive that never worked quite right but seems to do ok in linux once they got the proper drivers (somewhere around kernel 2.6.2), my camera works just fine, the dev tools work just fine. I used pgpdisk in windows to keep encrypted partitions but in linux I don't need that, it's built in. And I don't need to spend two days installing development tools and stuff, that's all built in too. I can create bash scripts that interact with the gui REALLY simply and if I want more complex stuff I can use python - and it's all built in.

What does windows do "better?" Better out of the box hardware support? Try installing OS X on your laptop and see how far you get. Hardware support is a matter of selecting your hardware from the HCL - just as with windows. When I was working tech support the one call no one (including myself) wanted was a modem problem, because half the time you'd end up spending HOURS on callbacks and stuff over it. That's just one example but it makes the point that even windows is not immune to driver issue nightmares - they're pretty common, in fact. At least with open source software you don't have to live with the *assurance* that as soon as the next bigger, faster, hotter video card comes out you'll never see a new update to the driver for YOUR card. 

Blueturtl, regarding that browser-in-the-desktop th9ng, take a look at this:

[http://raphael.slinckx.net/blog/](http://raphael.slinckx.net/blog/)

There are all sorts of appropriate uses of this technology. That doesn't mean it is inherently as insecure as the windows desktop. I long for an XML-ified "zenity" complete with the ability to pass parameters from "web pages" to the command line. That would **rawk**.

---

### Post by ghostintheshell on 2005-07-15
I like the "simple way" of gnome.
Without all that useless and irritating stuffs you can find in the KD(E) environment.

In fact, I use Gnome as Desktop but I use a lot of KDE apps:
amaroK (sometimes, nothing better than XMMS ;)), K3b (THE cd/dvd burner), konqueror (but I use more often Nautilus -> depends on needs and feelings), Krusader, Kate for scripts, KRename for batch files rename, showimg (the free alternative to ACDSee), Kopete, Kaffeine and some others ...

I also used BlackBox for 6 monthes! You should try it ;)
I really love BlackBox (~ fluxbox) but with Ubuntu I'm discovering a lot of "auto-stuffs" which enjoy your life :) (I think about 'auto-mount', docklets, ... )
Give it a try! And come back here after ;)

But like said, the most important thing to remember is that Linux lets you the choice! Always! You're free (as in freedom)! Thus you have to choose the desktop which is the best for you.
And there is no simply answer: you have to try them!

And yes ... freedom of choice is not so easy ;)

To all the post that say kde is to windows-alike: you can choose whether you want a macOS look, a windowsXP look or just another different (unix)  look, it's rubbish to say it's a windows clone, maybe some ideas are adopted from windows (like konqueror compared to internet explorer/explorer, although to me, Konqueror is internet explorer/explorer done right, konqueror is way more powerfull than internet explorer or explorer since you still need to open two apps, and neither one of them can do what konqueror can do) What's wrong with taking the good ideas over and leave the stupid things in the windows world :-), as long as no one is forced to do things a way they don't like. ### Post by casperl on 2005-09-01
Battleground: KDE vs Gnome

(I hope this is the right place to post this - if not, please point me to the right area.)

A common theme that appears on Ubuntu forums concerns KDE vs Gnome.  The comment is often made that KDE libraries are bloated, KDE apps crash more often, Gnome might be pretty but is othwerwise useless, KDE programs work better.  

This is deja-vu to me.  I have lived through a similar squabble in the past.

Yes, everyone is right.  Everyone has a point.  Personally, were it not for Ubuntu, I would not have used Gnome.  For many years I have used ICE-Wm and it was a fast and stable windowing manager.  But I won't go back now.  Gnome now means productivity for my day to day work.  That is where I make my living and where it counts most.

The best program in the world is the one you know best.  That was true 20 years ago and is just as true today.  You only become productive when you know software well.  That is one of the major benefits of Ubuntu - the continuity of re-using your present experience and a permanent end to distro-hopping.  Over the past decade I have wasted far too much time in the 'Linux distro wars'!  My home is Ubuntu.  Period.

Nearly twenty years ago, I owned an Atari ST.  It was a phenomenal computer but it was constantly belittled by the tribe of Amiga owners.  The Atari tribe counteracted by deriding the Amiga tribe.  Both machines 'owned' the market in sound and graphics as far as the computing world below the high-end Macintosh was concerned.  Through the constant bickering and infighting neither Atari, nor Commodore realised that they actually have 'multimedia' computers and that they actually have the multimedia market to themselves.  It took a company called Creative Labs with their Soundblaster card to turn PC's into multimedia computers.  And the rest is history, Atari and Commodore left Microsoft as the supreme victor, despite the technological advantage Commodore and Atari had.

Today I sense that KDE feels like the Atari ST.  KDE programs have that feeling of working the way you expect a program to work, the way you want to work.  You actually can get excited about KDE apps - I do!  But they crash, and they crash often.  Very often!  They take forever to load!  They just don't understand printing the way I understand it.  Then again, they work the way I work and I become productive!  Gnome looks beautiful, it makes things easy, it is clear and simple.  But Gnome has a mind of its own.  Why on earth should Nautilus consume 54% CPU cycles while I am typing this?  Why should the taskbar apps disappear at random?  Why does Gnome work better if Gnome (not Linux) is restarted every day?  Yet, Gnome is where I feel at home right now.  In a very real, albeit personal, sense, using KDE applications feels like using the Atari ST while using Gnome feels like the Amiga.

Let me make a prediction.  As long as there are two exclusive camps, KDE or Gnome, a third party will ultimately win the war.  We are literally too busy squabling over who looks the cutest in our back yards while failing to see the major highway in front of our homes.  Maybe Ubuntu will have the vision to be the catalyst to change the face of computing for the better.  I certainly hope so and I have committed ALL of my computing life onto the Ubuntu platform!

For Ubuntu to work, Linux has to work.  For Linux to really work, it has to provide a productive environment in the workplace for people that actually use PC's to do work as opposed to fanatics who are continually installing distros.  To achieve that goal I presently have to use Gnome and I have to use KDE applications.  Just as I am presently using apps that use Motif, I use programs require nothing more than standard X-Windows and I use console applications.  I cannot afford wasting time through fidgeting, through endlessly battling my system and through cursing programs that work anything less than 100%.  Merely loading programs, opening files and not crashing for a limited while is not my definintion of a productive environment if the program cannot print through CUPS as well!  If I have to jump through hoops to print to a PDF file, it is NOT a productive computing platform!!!!  I cannot afford to have my increasingly limited time wasted because of the trivial turf wars of the Linux tribes.  There is a computing alternative where the OS is stable and all applications work together 99% of the time (Apple) and there is another OS where applications work together and it is a matter of time before they get the OS stable (Windows).

The ultimate Linux distro will be the one that is so stable and with such widespread acceptance, that third party commercial developers will target it as the platform of choice for their applications.  For a while (between versions 5 and 6) Red Hat held the ground as the environment targeted for commercial development, whereafter they seem to have lost the plot.  I am prepared to pay for quality commercial software that increases my productivity.  For decades I have done exactly that while using Windows.  GNU licenced software is great, but occasionally I suspect that spending a little money might boost my productivity.  To that purpose I have purchased Opera.  Opera 8.0x on Linux is without doubt the best browser I have ever used simply because I become more productive when using it!  Does it run on Ubuntu?  Yes.  Does it work 100%?  Sadly, no.  Will it work if I spend hours and hours of unproductive fidgeting?  Maybe, but probably not.  If I purchase other commercial titles, will it work?  Who knows?  Is there a lesson in this?  Yes, maybe, in addition to Ubuntu, Kubuntu and Edubuntu, there should be an Ubuntu release where the top 50 Apps of every catagory on Freshmeat ([http://freshmeat.net](http://freshmeat.net)) is certified to work.  Much better still would be a single Ubuntu where I, and everybody else, could trust everything to work!

The promise of Ubuntu is unparalleled by any other computing OS or platform - free updates every six months, no software obsolesence, and up to date system, freedom, cutting edge performance, in practice the best support (through IRC and the Ubuntu forums) that I have ever experienced.  But tribalistic rivalry could undo this promise because the best Ubuntu (IMHO) would be Gnome, where all KDE applications actually work 100%, and where 98% of linux apps work faster and more stable than on any other platform or distro or whatever.  Providing a productive environment has to become the objective!   One day into the future, I expect to spend a highly productive day without having to do battle with my computer system!  (The only way I can achieve that right now is by turning my PC off for a day!)  

Still, it cannot be too much to ask.

---

### Post by Wolki on 2005-09-01
[QUOTE=xequence]I heard somewhere gnome was made because KDE was comertialized...

KDE: Gnome, I am your father!
Gnome: Nooooooooo![/QUOTE]

Not exactly. Back then the Qt widget toolkit was not GPLed, but free software got licenses for free. Gnome gets formed using the widget toolkit originally developed for the GIMP called gtk+, which itself was created because the widget set of the first GIMP versions (called Motif) was not free. Trolltech, the Qt developers, later put Qt under GPL, but offer commercial developers to buy licenses that allow for closed-source programs.

More info:
[http://kdemyths.urbanlizard.com/viewMyth.php?mythID=10](http://kdemyths.urbanlizard.com/viewMyth.php?mythID=10)
[http://www.gimp.org/about/ancient_history.html](http://www.gimp.org/about/ancient_history.html)

---

### Post by Wolki on 2005-09-01
[QUOTE=benplaut]pico seems to just bring up nano... huh?[/QUOTE]

Pico is the editor developed for and used by the email software pine. (who here remembers using that? :)) This editor was easy to use even for less experienced persons, and so it became very popular. (no wonder when the alternatives are emacs and vi ;))
pine's licensing however was not compatible with the gpl and the debian guidelines, so some very nice developers created a clone and called it nano. Since people might expect to have pico even on a completely free unix, nano is often included with distributions and responds to "pico", too,

---

pine's licensing however was not compatible with the gpl and the debian guidelines, so some very nice developers created a clone and called it nano. ### Post by casperl on 2005-09-02
[QUOTE=casperl]Battleground: KDE vs Gnome

(I hope this is the right place to post this - if not, please point me to the right area.)

A common theme that appears on Ubuntu forums concerns KDE vs Gnome.  The comment is often made that KDE libraries are bloated, KDE apps crash more often, Gnome might be pretty but is othwerwise useless, KDE programs work better.  

This is deja-vu to me.  I have lived through a similar squabble in the past.

Yes, everyone is right.  Everyone has a point.  Personally, were it not for Ubuntu, I would not have used Gnome.  For many years I have used ICE-Wm and it was a fast and stable windowing manager.  But I won't go back now.  Gnome now means productivity for my day to day work.  That is where I make my living and where it counts most.

The best program in the world is the one you know best.  That was true 20 years ago and is just as true today.  You only become productive when you know software well.  That is one of the major benefits of Ubuntu - the continuity of re-using your present experience and a permanent end to distro-hopping.  Over the past decade I have wasted far too much time in the 'Linux distro wars'!  My home is Ubuntu.  Period.

Nearly twenty years ago, I owned an Atari ST.  It was a phenomenal computer but it was constantly belittled by the tribe of Amiga owners.  The Atari tribe counteracted by deriding the Amiga tribe.  Both machines 'owned' the market in sound and graphics as far as the computing world below the high-end Macintosh was concerned.  Through the constant bickering and infighting neither Atari, nor Commodore realised that they actually have 'multimedia' computers and that they actually have the multimedia market to themselves.  It took a company called Creative Labs with their Soundblaster card to turn PC's into multimedia computers.  And the rest is history, Atari and Commodore left Microsoft as the supreme victor, despite the technological advantage Commodore and Atari had.

Today I sense that KDE feels like the Atari ST.  KDE programs have that feeling of working the way you expect a program to work, the way you want to work.  You actually can get excited about KDE apps - I do!  But they crash, and they crash often.  Very often!  They take forever to load!  They just don't understand printing the way I understand it.  Then again, they work the way I work and I become productive!  Gnome looks beautiful, it makes things easy, it is clear and simple.  But Gnome has a mind of its own.  Why on earth should Nautilus consume 54% CPU cycles while I am typing this?  Why should the taskbar apps disappear at random?  Why does Gnome work better if Gnome (not Linux) is restarted every day?  Yet, Gnome is where I feel at home right now.  In a very real, albeit personal, sense, using KDE applications feels like using the Atari ST while using Gnome feels like the Amiga.

Let me make a prediction.  As long as there are two exclusive camps, KDE or Gnome, a third party will ultimately win the war.  We are literally too busy squabling over who looks the cutest in our back yards while failing to see the major highway in front of our homes.  Maybe Ubuntu will have the vision to be the catalyst to change the face of computing for the better.  I certainly hope so and I have committed ALL of my computing life onto the Ubuntu platform!

For Ubuntu to work, Linux has to work.  For Linux to really work, it has to provide a productive environment in the workplace for people that actually use PC's to do work as opposed to fanatics who are continually installing distros.  To achieve that goal I presently have to use Gnome and I have to use KDE applications.  Just as I am presently using apps that use Motif, I use programs require nothing more than standard X-Windows and I use console applications.  I cannot afford wasting time through fidgeting, through endlessly battling my system and through cursing programs that work anything less than 100%.  Merely loading programs, opening files and not crashing for a limited while is not my definintion of a productive environment if the program cannot print through CUPS as well!  If I have to jump through hoops to print to a PDF file, it is NOT a productive computing platform!!!!  I cannot afford to have my increasingly limited time wasted because of the trivial turf wars of the Linux tribes.  There is a computing alternative where the OS is stable and all applications work together 99% of the time (Apple) and there is another OS where applications work together and it is a matter of time before they get the OS stable (Windows).

The ultimate Linux distro will be the one that is so stable and with such widespread acceptance, that third party commercial developers will target it as the platform of choice for their applications.  For a while (between versions 5 and 6) Red Hat held the ground as the environment targeted for commercial development, whereafter they seem to have lost the plot.  I am prepared to pay for quality commercial software that increases my productivity.  For decades I have done exactly that while using Windows.  GNU licenced software is great, but occasionally I suspect that spending a little money might boost my productivity.  To that purpose I have purchased Opera.  Opera 8.0x on Linux is without doubt the best browser I have ever used simply because I become more productive when using it!  Does it run on Ubuntu?  Yes.  Does it work 100%?  Sadly, no.  Will it work if I spend hours and hours of unproductive fidgeting?  Maybe, but probably not.  If I purchase other commercial titles, will it work?  Who knows?  Is there a lesson in this?  Yes, maybe, in addition to Ubuntu, Kubuntu and Edubuntu, there should be an Ubuntu release where the top 50 Apps of every catagory on Freshmeat ([http://freshmeat.net](http://freshmeat.net)) is certified to work.  Much better still would be a single Ubuntu where I, and everybody else, could trust everything to work!

The promise of Ubuntu is unparalleled by any other computing OS or platform - free updates every six months, no software obsolesence, and up to date system, freedom, cutting edge performance, in practice the best support (through IRC and the Ubuntu forums) that I have ever experienced.  But tribalistic rivalry could undo this promise because the best Ubuntu (IMHO) would be Gnome, where all KDE applications actually work 100%, and where 98% of linux apps work faster and more stable than on any other platform or distro or whatever.  Providing a productive environment has to become the objective!   One day into the future, I expect to spend a highly productive day without having to do battle with my computer system!  (The only way I can achieve that right now is by turning my PC off for a day!)  

Still, it cannot be too much to ask.[/QUOTE]
 Déjà Vu all over again!

It really is like the war between the Amiga and the Atari ST over again with two irreconcilable sides argueing essentially over the same things!

On the same system, KDE apps appears more stable when I booted the Knoppix distro than when running KDE apps under Ubuntu / Gnome.  

Grome looks beautifull, but, I have to be productive as well and there lies the test!  I use some KDE apps because Gnome alternatives are not available, or because I am more productive in them and they work better for me.

Rather than wasting time on the KDE vs Gnome battle we should address these issues:

The gradual slowdown and greater use of resources / CPU cycles of Gnome over a number of hours is a very real issue for me.

The relatively greater unstability of KDE apps on Ubuntu is a serious issue for me.

As far as I can see there is little in the sense of a sustained effort to integrate commercial software into Ubuntu.  This is a real issue to me, since not all future software will be open source.  (Fact is that sooner or later nearly all competent open source programmers will develop commercial / in-house software for renumeration.)

The lack of an effort to package and release the wealth of java based software for Ubuntu as easily installable .deb pacakages is also an issue for me.  Java applications must not be written off on the linux desktop.  In certain critical aspects there are few real choices available for the linux desktop and Java based apps will fill that void.)

It is my untested observation that there are more KDE applications available than Gnome applications.  Could it be that it is easier and faster to develop for the KDE platform than for Gnome?

Also there are more applications in KDE that appear to address specific niche areas than in Gnome where applications tend to be general tools.  The KDE spoke calculator ([http://www.kde-apps.org/content/show.php?content=16040](http://www.kde-apps.org/content/show.php?content=16040)) is a good example but there are many others.  Indeed it would be a totally pointless waste of human hours duplicating every KDE app because there is not a Gnome alternative.

My purpose of the original post was not for Ubuntu users to kill each other!  :)  

However, we have to stop the desktop wars and concentrate on stability, usability and the productive aspects of working in Ubuntu!

Thanks for all the informed opions expressed as a result of my original post.

---

### Post by poofyhairguy on 2005-09-02

Thats because Redhat has that market tied up.

### Post by Knome_fan on 2005-09-02
Some comments:
1. About Gnome using more and more memory/cpu.
I don't have this problem, or at least I didn't notice it. 
Did you check with top or gnome system monitor what process exactly is using up memory and cpu?

2. About Kubuntu apps being unstable.
Remeber this is a first release, so there are bound to be problems.
However, there were some semi official updates, did you try if updating helped?
[http://www.kubuntu.org/hoary-kde-342.php](http://www.kubuntu.org/hoary-kde-342.php)

3. About Java.
Java simply isn't free, so it's problematic to package with a distribution of free software. However, I think that at least blackdown java made it into multiverse and in addition, there are great efforts to make a free java stack. Right now it's for example possible to run OO2 and Eclipse nativley on a free java stack.

4. Just out of interest, what programms exactly are you missing in Gnome?

### Post by neighborlee on 2005-09-02
[QUOTE=mike998]Hasn't this one been argued and fought over many, many, many times?

I've found that KDE is faster sometimes than Gnome, and other times, Gnome if faster than KDE.  Yes, KDE seems to have a larger number of apps but personally, I find Gnome has the right apps for me.  If this changes, I may switch to KDE for the apps.

The bottom line is : if you find KDE works for you, go for it.  If you find Gnome works for you, go for it.

Why argue?  Choice is good.[/QUOTE]

Choice is good but I do wish distros would unite under one banner, of course not trying to force ONE distro on anyone.  One package manager would of course be a way to solve that nightmare and I think SPM is a good candidate for it.  I've seen more than one person say 'gosh so many distros I can't decide so I tend to HOP'. I dont know how many people that represents but hey one is too many frankly.  Kde VS Gnome is pretty silly, as the one poster mentioned that each has contributed in length to Linux as a whole, and its clear you can run kde apps in gnome and vica-versa ( to what degree I dont know as I have not have extensive research in this field  ).

The sooner we have a united front the better and the we can all expect things to 'just run' and not to have to boot into that 'other' OS as we currently do <wink>. I"d LOVE to see the day come when apps/games are released Crossplatform by most vendors and we'll get there via a united front.

cheers
nl
