### Post by poofyhairguy on 2005-09-02
[QUOTE=neighborlee]Choice is good but I do wish distros would unite under one banner, of course not trying to force ONE distro on anyone.  One package manager would of course be a way to solve that nightmare and I think SPM is a good candidate for it.  I've seen more than one person say 'gosh so many distros I can't decide so I tend to HOP'. I dont know how many people that represents but hey one is too many frankly.  Kde VS Gnome is pretty silly, as the one poster mentioned that each has contributed in length to Linux as a whole, and its clear you can run kde apps in gnome and vica-versa ( to what degree I dont know as I have not have extensive research in this field  ).

The sooner we have a united front the better and the we can all expect things to 'just run' and not to have to boot into that 'other' OS as we currently do <wink>. I"d LOVE to see the day come when apps/games are released Crossplatform by most vendors and we'll get there via a united front.

cheers
nl[/QUOTE]

</my opinion>

Yep. Just like I wish there was one car company, one music company, one soft drink company, etc. Choices are what makes life worth living I say. The problem is the OS market has been messed up. People say its a natural monopoly and they may be right, but MS has created an expectation that one size fits all. That is not the case. 

I am always the one to say this. Sometimes I feel like a party crasher, but I know it to be correct....why let people live with false hopes for something not that good in the first place.

I will make this promise: there will never be "standards" in the desktop Linux space or a unified ANYTHING (more than what we have now) until one distro starts to "win" and get a large percentage of the marketshare. Linux and the whole GNU OS is a meritocracy... only the best lead. So far there is no winner. And there is a good reason for that- no Linux is THAT popular. When the masses catch on, whatever distro takes off (I say SUSE) is the winner. And if none do you can kiss standards goodby. 

I know it might seem like it would "make sense" for Linux to unify, but we aren't talking about a company. Or an organization. Or anything that is bound together. There is no way for force Linux makers or users to do anything. There will always be competition. 

Everytime someone finds out about Linux, they see the fragmented landscape and think "so much more could get done if the different projects unify." Thats true. But the problem is that its impossible. Within the group there are groups that would (and do) refuse to play together. No one wants to give up any control. Its the price paid for freedom. I mean, there are some standards and we are lucky there is only one kernel and one xorg, but no standard will ever wrap together the Linux community. They don't want to be wrapped together. 

If you forced it to come together, many would quit development. If you forced KDE and Gnome together, many of its volunteer developers would quit. The unified project would have a partial "brain drain." That goes for every case in Gnu-land.

Often once the new user realizes this, they get depressed at first. The power of GNU is awesome and it would be a world beater if it all came together. But it won't. It pretty much can't. Its got to be as good as you can be divided....which is still pretty good. At this point the people either accept it (I think its better than developers develop a new project that has something kinda like it rather than no development at all. I like Gnome so I would hate if KDE was the only choice, etc.), go back to Windows (unified development), or buy a Mac (unified development). Its the way of the world.

If someone could create an equation to solve the Linux unity problem, we should not waste it on Linux. If it could exist we should apply it to politics because they same formula would have to solve the basic problems that lead to world conflict and division.

</my opinion>

---

### Post by Wolki on 2005-09-02
I still don't see why some fragmentation is a bad thing.

Just consider desktop environments. KDE is without a doubt the most popular. But there are a lot of people who like Gnome, and for good reasons too (same as the people who like KDE). Now, if you reduce it to one project, that would be KDE (say by majority vote). But what about the People who liked Gnome better? They would be forced to use something that doesn't fit them well, and that *can't* be the purpose of Free Software. And if KDE in return was gnome-ified, the KDE users whould have every reason to complain. They chose KDE and not Gnome, it would be bad to force it upon them.

Try to combine things that are good in different ways, and most likely you'll get a mediocre product. Just let the competition bring out the best in each.

### Post by regeya on 2005-09-21
It's all a matter of perspective, really.  I got a laugh out of someone claiming that KDE was slower than GNOME; my recent experience has been just the opposite!   :)  KDE is slow to start up while GNOME is quick to start up, and KDE apps are fast to start up while GNOME apps are not (hint: install and set up prelink if you find KDE app startup to be unbearably slow.)

If it's an either-or, I tend to like KDE for including everything imaginable, and GNOME for the simplicity.  Somewhere in the middle is what a decent desktop ought to be.  I'm thinking something like a more malleable *Step clone, but not the direction Apple went with it.  Less eyecandy, more flexibility, something speedy on lower-end hardware for the people in the cheap seats, but keep an eye on interface design so it doesn't become an unusable mess.

Heck, both are lightyears ahead of the first computers I used.   ;-)

### Post by linbetwin on 2005-10-13
Hello,
 
I used Ubuntu 5.04 and 5.10 Preview, but now that 5.10 final is out, I'd like to give Kubuntu a try, because I saw it has KDE 3.4.3. I've got a few questions for more experienced (K)Ubuntu users:
 
What is the difference between Ubuntu and Kubuntu, apart from Gnome/KDE ?
 
What is the relationship between the two projects?
 
Is Kubuntu less supported than Ubuntu?
 
Is it less reliable?
 
Is Kubuntu shipped on request, like Ubuntu?
 
If you had to recommend either of the two variations, whould your choice depend solely on your preference for Gnome or KDE?
 
Is KDE 3.4.3 a major upgrade from 3.4.2?
 
Can I download Ubuntu and then install KDE from the repositories, or is it better to download Kubuntu if I want KDE?
 
Can I have Gnome and KDE installed at the same time?
 
How do I switch between Gnome and KDE?
 
Sorry if that's too many questions! And thanks in advance for your answers!

### Post by JediPottsy on 2005-10-13
theres no real difference apart from the GDM, and applications, ie kubuntu will come with KDE app's and Gnome with come with gnome app - its essentially the same os, just different packaging.

as for the kde version : [http://www.kde.org/announcements/announce-3.4.3.php](http://www.kde.org/announcements/announce-3.4.3.php) - theres information about the newest release.

You can download KDE from ubuntu, just use synaptic or terminal

"sudo apt-get install kde"

however this will give u both gnome and kde, then you have to uninstall gnome, if u just want KDE. I have gnome, kde and xfce installed, depending on what i want to do, and i started with ubuntu.

---

### Post by GreyFox503 on 2005-10-13
[I]What is the difference between Ubuntu and Kubuntu, apart from Gnome/KDE ?

[/I]As far as I can tell, nothing.  Of course, KDE and GNOME come with quite a few unique applications, but nothing you can't get from the repositories anyway.  I use Ubuntu mostly, but installed KDE apps like k3b and amaroK to use in GNOME
 
[I] What is the relationship between the two projects?

[/I]From what I understand:  They are extremely similar.  They use the same repositories and all the same packages should work on each other.  The main Ubuntu guys use GNOME, then when they release a version, the Kubuntu guys strip off GNOME and add KDE.
 
[I] Is Kubuntu less supported than Ubuntu?

[/I]Don't know, but I would guess the same.  At least on the forums you can get Kubuntu questions answered too.
 [I]
Is it less reliable?

[/I]Can't say from experience, but I'd guess its less reliable only if you think KDE is (most would say not).
 
* Is Kubuntu shipped on request, like Ubuntu?*

???
 
[I] If you had to recommend either of the two variations, whould your choice depend solely on your preference for Gnome or KDE?

[/I]Yes.  

[I] Is KDE 3.4.3 a major upgrade from 3.4.2?

[/I]I think its actually just a couple security/bug fixes.
 
[I] Can I download Ubuntu and then install KDE from the repositories, or is it better to download Kubuntu if I want KDE?

[/I]If you know for sure you're gonna want only KDE, then yeah, just get Kubuntu to save time.  However, adding KDE to the regular ubuntu install is really easy.  Just install one meta-package (**kubuntu-desktop**, i believe) and it will download everything you'd normally get from a Kubuntu install.
 
[I] Can I have Gnome and KDE installed at the same time?

[/I]Yes, its fun.  :)  I do it now.  At your login screen you can choose which environment you'd like to login to.  You can even be logged into both of the same at the same time in different virtual terminals!
 
[I] How do I switch between Gnome and KDE?

[/I]Either log out and log back in, or open a new login (In Ubuntu 5.04 its Applications > System Tools > New Login).




That's pretty much all I know.  If I made a mistake feel free to correct me.  As you can probably tell, I'm mostly a GNOME user who has dabbled in KDE.

---

### Post by asimon on 2005-10-13
[QUOTE=JediPottsy]You can download KDE from ubuntu, just use synaptic or terminal

"sudo apt-get install kde"
[/QUOTE]
Better install kubuntu-desktop instead of kde to get the whole kubuntu greatness, like for example the new Kubuntu package manager adept.

And if you installed Kubuntu you can easily get Ubuntu's Gnome desktop by installing ubuntu-desktop.

In most parts Kubuntu and Ubuntu are really the same. The packages are the same, everything is developed in the same repository, etc. The difference is really just what's on the CDs/DVDs and what gets installed by default.

If you have Gnome and KDE installed you can choose on the login screen (gdm or kdm) what desktop to use. Just use the "Session" menu there and choose KDE or Gnome while logging in.

BTW, there is one negative issue if you install both Gnome and KDE. The menus will get cluttered. But I am optimistic that for the next release an elegant solution will be found to that.

EDIT:
No, KDE 3.4.3 is only a minor KDE release. To see what changed, have a look at the [KDE 3.4.2 to KDE 3.4.3 Changelog](http://kde.org/announcements/changelogs/changelog3_4_2to3_4_3.php).

### Post by RawMustard on 2005-11-05
KDE is way too much, Gnome is way too little. Somewhere in between would be just right I reckon. And I have to agree with Stormy Eyes, metacity is a crime against humanity!

What I don't understand is why both have not tried something new, the task bar menu button thingy is so old, surely there are better ways of doing things? At least on windows they tried with XP, not so good I admit but at least they tried something.

Gnome just seems to want to rip everything out and take us back 20 years, I really wish they would start to innovate, I don't call spatial innovation.

Perhaps it takes someone with some radical ideas to present them properly and then maybe someone can code them up, but I've always felt the Gnome Devs were not interested in outside ideas, just look at the fuss over a menu editor and how long it took them to put a clipboard manager in or even the lameness of metacity.

All in all, it does give you the things you need to get stuff done, but jeez, it's 2005 and some parts of it are just so 1970 :(

Look at what Apple's done with OSX, they moved forward and did some pretty amazing stuff and now everyone's going gaga over it. I realise some people don't like it, but a hell of a lot do and are now calling for it to be released from its Mac shackles. I've never ever heard a windows user rave like this about Gnome or KDE.

So when you have people say stuff like this 
[IMG]http://members.optushome.com.au/tillm/images/ep-bookwin.png[/IMG]

looks better and is more functional than this,
[IMG]http://members.optushome.com.au/tillm/images/fox-bookwin.png[/IMG]

and Epiphany is more usable than [this]("http://members.optushome.com.au/tillm/images/firefox-ftp.png")

I don't hold out much hope of Gnome taking away any thunder from anyone.

It will be interesting to see what Gnome 3 and KDE 4 have to show off, we already know the puss that is vista and if Apple ever release OSX from its mac shackles, then it will be an entirely different ball game IMHO.

These are just some of my thoughts, I hope I haven't offended anyone, I wasn't trying to :)

### Post by bonzodog on 2005-11-20
I have just put Ubuntu in a cybercafe, and the first thing I did was consolidate the 2 panels into one, remove the 3 menus and added the main menu. I also stopped the clock displaying the date by default, it displays the date on mouse hover-over. I also removed the desktop switcher - it wasn't needed.  I then put the quick launch Items next to the main menu button. We sat back and watched 3 customers come in, check their mail with no help whatsoever on hotmail - It all just worked. I personally will never go near KDE - it's bloatware as far as I'm concerned. I was forced to remove novell suse from my system because of it. Sorry, but i'm a die-hard gnome + GnuStep fan.

### Post by 23meg on 2005-11-20
[QUOTE=Stormy Eyes]I don't. I think the two-panel default makes sense. It is unforgivably stupid to jam the taskbar in with everything else and expect it to be useful; once you have more than a few apps open, how the hell are you supposed to use the taskbar if it doesn't have a panel to itself?[/QUOTE]
The one-panel setup makes sense for users who make use of multiple workspaces and/or task grouping and don't have a ridiculously high number of apps running at any given time. I have habits of placing certain apps in certain workspaces and my single panel which is quite populated still handles the window list quite well, since it usually only has to display 3-4 apps at most.

### Post by 23meg on 2005-11-20
[QUOTE=Stormy Eyes]That's good for about four apps.[/QUOTE]My point is that it's good for about four apps *in one workspace* (I can actually bear with 6-7 but it's rarely needed) as long as you don't set the window list to display windows from all workspaces.

BTW, [that article]("http://djst.org/blog/2005/09/21/gnome-shouldnt-use-two-panels/") is really lame in that it wants to do away with the workspace switcher altogether. I hate it when people extrapolate based on very few real life observations that "noone but the nerdiest people use it anyway" about FOSS apps / features. It's no coincidence that these are people who have mainly used Windows most of their life, thus carry with them their Windows-based prejudices about how things should be and can't stand any real change; that's fine by me in fact, as long as they speak just for themselves and not "the majority of people".

### Post by poofyhairguy on 2005-11-20
[QUOTE=23meg]Same for me; Windows users I've seen immediately start raving about how useful multiple workspaces are as soon as they see them for the first time. This guy is begging for the removal of the workspace switcher *as a Gnome default*, though; not even a distro default. Some people really want everyone to go twenty years back just because they themselves can't get used to a certain feature.[/QUOTE]

I don't think thats the motive. I think instead the writer believes (like many) that the purpose of the Linux Desktop (and therefore Gnome) is to provide an easy to use alternative for Windows switchers. So, since Windows does not have two panels neither should Gnome. Since Windows does not have virtual desktops by default neither should Gnome.

Of course the falacy is that by doing this Gnome hides some of the features that make its desktop better than a default Windows desktop. Many who fall into this trap believe that other problems of Windows (malware, viruses, cost) will drive users to the Linux Desktop if the defaults would welcome switchers more. The obvious problem with this analysis is that those who can't deal with Windows problems (in general) won't know what the unmarketed Linux IS and won't have the skills/devotion needed to switch over.

---

### Post by nKov on 2005-11-20
I **love** virtual desktops. It really hurt to lose them when I switched from an Nvidia Geforce to an ATI Radeon. I think you'd have to be completely crazy to get rid of them.

And they aren't hard to use at all. I'm 15 now, and it was probably about 3 years ago that I was using them.

Edit (clarification):
My point is that if your average 12 year old can "get it," most adults should be able to too.

### Post by BoyOfDestiny on 2005-11-20
[QUOTE=poofyhairguy]I don't think thats the motive. I think instead the writer believes (like many) that the purpose of the Linux Desktop (and therefore Gnome) is to provide an easy to use alternative for Windows switchers. So, since Windows does not have two panels neither should Gnome. Since Windows does not have virtual desktops by default neither should Gnome.

Of course the falacy is that by doing this Gnome hides some of the features that make its desktop better than a default Windows desktop. Many who fall into this trap believe that other problems of Windows (malware, viruses, cost) will drive users to the Linux Desktop if the defaults would welcome switchers more. The obvious problem with this analysis is that those who can't deal with Windows problems (in general) won't know what the unmarketed Linux IS and won't have the skills/devotion needed to switch over.[/QUOTE]

Indeed. When I switched to Linux I decided to give gnome a try. I did not remove the top panel, and then make the bottom like the windows start bar. I want to give this "new way" a chance. I still do. My only "qualm" with gnome is no recursive permission changing with nautilus (no biggie thanks to chmod... I still think they should add an option).

P.S Why did this post get drudged up again... It's like 9 months old and had faded into obscurity weeks and weeks ago...

---

### Post by 23meg on 2005-11-20
[QUOTE=poofyhairguy]I don't think thats the motive. I think instead the writer believes (like many) that the purpose of the Linux Desktop is to provide an easy to use alternative for Windows switchers. [/QUOTE]
That's exactly what I meant in [my post on the last page]("http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=507778&postcount=120"): the Windows-centric way of thinking is to blame. 

What I keep pointing to people who rant about "Gnome defaults" is that "Gnome defaults" does not necessarily mean the default Gnome settings that come with your distro. Distros configure Gnome to suit the needs and expectations of their user bases. Has anyone here ever installed Gnome? Anyone fiddled with Garnome? Probably no, because Gnome comes preinstalled with distros, and it's safe to assume that most people use distros, not LFS. When you rant about "Gnome defaults" you're complaining about Gnome's settings that will be presented to every distro and everyone who installs Gnome from scratch. In most cases it's more sensible to complain to the developers of your own distro about how they configure Gnome by default.

### Post by Qrk on 2005-11-21
I couldn't dissagree with that article more. The thing I like most about Gnome is it has more panel space that just about any other desktop. Thats good because Gnome's panels very good.

Having two panels means that I have space to organize all of my launchers. Having the three-split menu means I have to click one less time. It also is nice to split apps from places, a la Mac OS X. 

And the workspace feature is really well implimented in gnome (particually with the icons for the app shown there) Its not enlightenments, but it is very well done. 

Windows and KDE don't put priority on the most important corner of the screen... the top left. I admit with an app running and the menu structure, that corner can get cluttered, but its better than having nothing there but WM controls like windows. 

So please don't take my beloved Gnome away. I will forgive it odd spatial browsing and iconified location bars because of it's lovely panels and sane menus.

My only real problem with Gnome, however, is that it splits configuration between the "System-->preferences" menu and the configuration editor.

### Post by Wolki on 2005-11-25
[QUOTE=Qrk]I couldn't dissagree with that article more. The thing I like most about Gnome is it has more panel space that just about any other desktop. Thats good because Gnome's panels very good.[/quote]

I agree. The gnome panel is very powerful (it's what sold me to gnome initially), people will understand the usefulness of having room to add their own things.

> And the workspace feature is really well implimented in gnome (particually with the icons for the app shown there) Its not enlightenments, but it is very well done. 

I agree, and I think that even new users can grok the concept. I'd suggest lowering the default to two virtual desktops though - it makes it simpler to understand and reduces the chance of people getting lost between workspaces. People who understand it already and want more will find out how to add more.
A "You've just changed workspaces. More information about this feature <blah>, you can change back by <blah>" information dialog the first time that feature is used might be a good idea - People who don't understand what the feature does might click on another workspace accidentally, and then think all their applications crashed because they're not there anymore. Of course, only the first time (or maybe with a show this again checkbox) or it'd get annoying really fast.

### Post by ed_d on 2005-11-25
When I first encoutered gnome, I found it to be pretty easy, then I went to KDE, had some great features, and for what I do, the konsole was great. The ability to save the history to a file, made my life easy, as I would have to try and save config files to a floppy, then move it over...... Too much wasted time.
I have used Red Hat from 6 up to 9, then Core 1 through 4. I gave Debian a run, had toomany issues with configs, and Gnome would lock up all the time. Went back to Core and ran Gnome. Found some tweaks with the terminal, changed the scroll back to 5000 and now just highlight and copy, not as easy as konsole, but works good.
When I installed Ubuntu the first time and noticed how clean the desktop was, as in no icons for anything, I thought it was odd, but then again I never really used the desktop icons.

Back to topic. I found that KDE had some nice features, but was really bloted to me on the system I was using and that gnome just moved very well. I have not messed with my desktop on Ubuntu since installing. Only today did I really change the theme and the background and added gdesklets. I had tried XFCE as well, but found it to be too much like CDE on AIX. Yes it is lite and fast to load, but under Red Hat, a lot of the features were in-accesible to normal, non-root user. So went back to gnome. I think that it might have changed in later releases, but you know I just like gnome the best.

Ubuntu to me is the best distro I have used. Like it a lot more the Red hat, and will continue to "sell" it.

### Post by super on 2005-12-01
> windows manager virtually it handles all i need
that reminds me, anybody have any clue when gnome will be able to remember things like window size and position? it seems to do it for nautilus but not anything else. it gets kinda annoying having to resize totem all the time.

other than that, gnome is great!
i love the idea of labeling the programs with generic name by default for beginners. (eg. rhythmbox= music player, eog=image viewer, nautilus=file manager, etc...)

kde is ok, i used it for long time when i was running mandrake. but how is a newbie supposed to know what 'kppp' is supposed to do? :rolleyes: 

also labelling the menus with their contents is smart if the person has never used windows. clicking a menu labeled 'applications' to run an application makes more sense than clicking a 'k' with a cog to run everything.

but linux is all about choice right? so my opinion doesn't make yours any less valid. :D

### Post by sappman2 on 2005-12-09

It's kind of like sitting on a brand new screamin' Jap-bike at the dealership with no keys to start it up. You can grip the handle bars, you can smell the fresh rubber in the tires, you can see your reflction in the gas-tank paint....you know there's real power there, but you can't get to it yet. I feel like if I could just find the keys and get it moving that I'd finally experience this freedom everyone keeps mentioning. So, I'm SLOWLY getting acquainted with Linux, GNOME, and a few applications I've learned to use. But, so far, I really like it....I dunno... make sense? I'm glad it's NOT like Windows.

### Post by Adrian on 2006-01-02
[QUOTE=sitka]Which is your favorite desktop?  Kde or Gnome?[/QUOTE]

KDE:
+ I like the configurability. I always end up wanting to do some "not so usual" operation, and KDE makes that possible (easily) most of the time.
+ I prefer QT to GTK+ for programming (and KDevelop, my preferred IDE, runs a little faster under KDE on my ancient computer).
+ It's a little snappier than Gnome on my machine.

GNOME:
+ I think it looks very good! I find the KDE defaults to be very ugly actually (yes, I'm not very fond of crystal stuff). Gnome = style!

So, I've been using KDE for the last months, and hence KDE gets my vote. However, I've configured it to look more or less like Gnome, since I really prefer that look.

### Post by xbruceyx on 2006-01-02
GNOME gets my vote for elegance in both design and applications. It's meant to get work done in a minimalist type of work environment; from the layout to the choice of applications, GNOME is heavily centered towards rewarding usuability. However, I do find that KDE is a very well matured desktop more geared to the power-user or osx-type fanboy/girl. KDE performs a bit faster on identical hardware but that is something that GNOME developers are always working on improving.

Besides, you can install Ubuntu or Kubuntu and then just install the respective package for the other desktop environment in Synaptic or via apt-get command-line and have the best of both. Sure, you'll sap more free hard drive space but that's negligible nowadays.:rolleyes:

### Post by newbie2 on 2006-01-02
this answer of Erik van Luxzenburg could almost be exactamundo my answer -->> My problem is, I like certain programs of the one, and certain of the other. I like the scheme-ability of KDE, which I can make look like an OS X system to many other ways, GNOME lacks this. I like AmaroK, which is much greater than Rhythmbox (though the later is filling the gap), I like the KDE Control Centre to edit my KDE desktop, as well as I like many graphical tools under KDE. However, I prefer Nautilus over Konqueror, Firefox over Konqueror, Thunderbird over KMail, and all those run smoother under GNOME I feel than under KDE. I prefer GAIM not Kopete, but also that isn’t running smooth under KDE. And the same counts for OpenOffice, feels like it runs easier under GNOME than under KDE. On the other hand, Skype matches better under KDE than under GNOME!

So even though I would like to use KDE, I run GNOME, as OpenOffice, Firefox, Thunderbird, GAIM and Nautilus are my most used programs. So agreeing some programs are not the best is a good thing, I keep on tracking KDE, and when I feel it matches what I like, I switch again. In the meanwhile, I read the whole discussion and sigh:

Why couldn’t KDE and GNOME be more blended, so I can run a desktop I like? Using the best of both worlds!
[http://blogs.qtdeveloper.net/archives/2005/12/21/kde-ease-of-use-and-the-year-of-the-linux-desktop/](http://blogs.qtdeveloper.net/archives/2005/12/21/kde-ease-of-use-and-the-year-of-the-linux-desktop/)
:cool:

### Post by Derek Djons on 2006-01-29

Since years I had installed KDE again. It is certainly improved! KDE s by default faster as Gnome and surprisingly lightweight. Though there were a lot of preferences which could be edited (but not useful or interesting in my opinion) it was more easier  to setup.

Actually I became pretty comfort using KDE for those two days. Apps like Firefox and Mozilla were looking quite different than KDE did but okay, it's was still looking alright. But for some reason I really quickly started missing Gnome. There were little (personal) things which annoyed me and them together made me switching back.

Tough KDE can be modified very quickly and with ease, it's very lightweight and it look very neat! I'm looking at Gnome again.

But I wouldn't be surprised if future changes and features will draw the attention of  the current Gnome users.

### Post by el3ktro on 2006-01-30
I'm surprised to read that Gnome is told to have performance problems. I was a KDE user for many years, and after quickly trying out Dapper I was so impressed from Gnome that I decided to install Ubuntu 5.10 instead of Kubuntu. Gnome seems to be much faster tan my previous KDE install.

Well what I like on Gnome is it's simplicity. I like KDEs functionality, but the way t s presented to the user is just too much, it's cluttered, overwhelming and not well organized. I've been involded in kde-artists.org a lot where we talked about KDEs GUI, and I always said that KDE should _somehow_ keep it's functionality, but make the UI easier at the same time. Well now that I discovered Gnome, I think that most of KDEs functionality is merely just configurability. I really like this simplyness now. When I wasburning a CD in K3b for example, I was always tweaking all kind of settings to get "the perfect CD", but now when I burn a CD with Nautlius, I just drop the file over and it's burnt, and it works, too, surprisingly. I found out that - at least for me- KDEs enormous offer of configuration optons kind of forced me to tweak them all, I always had to look over all of them to see if everything is really set alright, and I didn't realize how much time I loose with that.

So for now, simpliness is the way to go for me. I'm still looking forward to KDE 4 actually, because if all of our ideas relly get implemented this would give a whole new desktop experience, but I hope that by that time Gnome will also introduce some new concepts (but still maintaining it's simplicity & easiness)

Tom

### Post by mcduck on 2006-01-30
[QUOTE=stimpack]I dont think simplicity is everything, Ive used computers for 20years, I dont want to be treated like a moron. I still maintain KDE gives an identical look+feel to OS X when setup (5min job). GTK apps do not even respect the top OS X style KDE menu.

Simplicity isn't everything, but neither is simple GUI things like Mac style top menu. But for every mac user I know, simplicity is the important thing, and top menu is just something they are used to. 

Also, hiding options doesn't help anything. The user has to actually browse through all options, and either know what all of them do or just randomly hide some. That's not going to work. Things should be done the other way, showing more options when advanced user needs them..

### Post by el3ktro on 2006-01-30
Only because I want a simple interface, that doesn't mean I'm dumb, I rather have work to do, so I need an interface which doesn't get in my way. With KDE apps it's just often that I'm getting lost between all those menus & toolbars that I can't get my work done as fast as I wish I could.

If you want to reflect Mac OS, there's MUCH more than having a top-screen menu bar. Mac OS is intuitive because it's simple, but yet powerful. Compare Safari to Konqueror (as web browser) for example. Safari's interface is just so much better, sorry. My girlfriend recently got annoyed by pop up windows on her iBook running KDE. She told me she was able to turn this off in Safari, and she complained that Konqueror can't do this. I told her she can, she just didn't find this function. You'll now hy:

Safari:
Click on Safari->Block popups

Konqueror:
Settings->Configure Konqueror, scroll down to "Java & JavaScript", click the JavaScript tab, set the option to have JavaScript popups filtered intelligently.

You can't tell me that you don't think the first one was easier. This has nothing to do with being dumb, my girlfriend is using her computer for her studies, and SHE SIMPLY HAS NO TIME AND IS NOT WILLING to scroll trough hundreds of menus to find such an obvious function like blocking popup windows - and I fully understand that.

Another example is getting your digital photos on the computer. This task is sooo much simpler in Mac OS and also in Gnome than it is in KDE. In Mac OS, you plug your camera in, and it takes ONE SINGLE mouse click to get your photos, in Gnome, you plug your camera in and you have to enter a name and one mouseclick to get your photos. In KDE this has always ben much more complex for me (if you know an easier solution, plz tell me).

So to sum it up: I like KDEs functionality a lot, but after a while it just got into my way, because I had more and more work to do, I had to study, and I simpy don't have time for such things anymore. This is also the reason why I switched from Gentoo to Ubuntu - I just don't have time & want to fiddle around with the system all the time. I simply want it to just work, and Ubuntu does this pretty much.

KDE is great, but if the KDE people don't find a solution to better organize all this functionality & configurability, KDE will really have a problem. It will get so crowded and cluttered that it becomes harder and harder to use - and I really hope for KDE that there are usability experts out there who find a way to solve this.

Tom

---

### Post by el3ktro on 2006-01-30
[QUOTE=mcduck]Simplicity isn't everything, but neither is simple GUI things like Mac style top menu. But for every mac user I know, simplicity is the important thing, and top menu is just something they are used to. [/QUOTE]

Ack. Simplicity is not good if it cuts down needed functions, but sometimes configurability can also be too much. In KDE it's often too much imho. 

> Also, hiding options doesn't help anything. The user has to actually browse through all options, and either know what all of them do or just randomly hide some. That's not going to work. Things should be done the other way, showing more options when advanced user needs them..

The problem that many KDE apps have imho is that some devs seem to think that they make the GUI better if they put all the functionality directly in-face into the toolbars. Every single menu entry also has a toolbar button. This is just wrong. Apps like Showimg show me this, this app has great functionality, but it's GUI just sucks because it's so overcrowed and messy, and it's really hard to find this one function you need between all the dozens of toolbar buttons.

A toolbar should present the most commonly used functions - not more. A good example how this can be done wrong is KDevelop. It's GUI is just a mess, I never liked this app because you get lost in it. Look at XCode instead. When you look at it you may think this program is just ake but it has almost the same functionality as KDevelop - it's just thousand times simpler to use, because it puts only the most commonly used functions in-face, all other functions are well organized in the menus.

Tom

---

### Post by Viro on 2006-01-30
Excellent examples, el3ktro. Couldn't have said it better myself. Honestly, if anyone thinks that KDE and OS X are similar, they really need to use OS X for a few months, then come back and make that claim. KDE and OS X (IMHO) are polar opposites. OS X tries to make things as easy as possible and doesn't bother you with needless options, while KDE gives you everything *and* the kitchen sink.

In that respect, GNOME is much more similar to OS X than KDE is. As a simple example, look at the control panel of KDE and GNOME. See how many options there are in KDE compared to GNOME and decide if the 'lack' of options in GNOME is a disadvantage.

### Post by Iandefor on 2006-02-20
Ubuntu uses the GNOME desktop environment, whereas Kubuntu uses KDE.
Simply put, they're different ways of handling the desktop. There are a ton of threads around detailing their differences. Do a search for "GNOME vs. KDE" or something similar. It comes down to personal preference. Why did I choose Ubuntu? I've never really liked KDE, although KDE 4 looks promising.

### Post by SuperDiscoMachine V.5.7-3 on 2006-02-20
Oh, this is a subject for a holy war. :-D 

There are two versions because there are two major desktops for Linux. Gnome and KDE.
[http://www.gnome.org/](http://www.gnome.org/)
[http://www.kde.org/](http://www.kde.org/)

Ubuntu is the version using Gnome by default and Kubuntu uses KDE.

Now asking people what the differences are etc. is surely asking for trouble as many people feel very strongly about their desktop of choice. But as you asked:
I like and use both and I think both are very usable desktop form most users.
That said, as you want to use ubuntu, I think you'll be better of chosing ubuntu (the gnome version), as in my experience this version is a bit maturer than the kde version. (Which of course isn't really surprising, as Ubuntu started out as a Gnome distro and Kubuntu came later).

Also, I think more people around here are using Gnome for the reason mentioned above, so it might be easier to find answers to your questions and problems if you also use it.

Hope this helps and have fun.:-D

### Post by DigitalDuality on 2006-02-20
A little more of an explainer.. from a semi-beginer to a beginer..

I started offwith Ubuntu (Gnome) and moved to Kubuntu (KDE) recently and have gotten a good taste of each.

Between Ubuntu and Kubuntu there a good many difference (as with KDE and Gnome).

KDE desktops come with a whole different set of applications pre-installed.  YOu have Kopete instead of GAIM for instance.  Kate instead of gedit (text editors),  It also doesn't come with GIMP standard if i'm not mistaken either (forgot waht the KDE equiv was).

and then there's Konquerer.  Konq is both a file browser and web browser (much like Explorer.. well, kinda)  Konq is used to browse the files on your system and for web browsing.  It's tabbed like firefox and shares a good many of firefox's features.  

Gnome/Ubuntu comes with Nautilus as a file manager, and firefox as the web browser.  Nautilis isn't as feature rich as Konquerer, but it is signifigantly faster IMO.  I also prefer firefox to Konq as a web browser.

KDE's strengths IMO lie in the fact that it's eye candy is about a year ahead of Gnome.  Drop shaddows, composite effects, etc..  The navigation bar of KDE is very windows like on first boot and many windows users will find it familiar.

While Gnome on first boot, as 2 navigation bars.  One at the top of the screen with 3 drop down menus..and quick launch.  And a task bar at the bottom to show you what's currently open and running.

I personally.. like KDE's eye candy..hence why i'm using it.. but currently working on getting Nautilus from Gnome imported and setup the way i like and kicking Konquerer to the curb.  I've also uninstalled a good deal of default KDE applications and put on FF, GAIM, Gimp, Thunderbird, etc. etc. And i've set it up to have 2 toolbars like Gnome.  Kind of a best of both worlds scenario.

---

### Post by xequence on 2006-02-20
> If their both essentially "Linux" why the 2 versions then?

Each is a different desktop environment (de). There are also many other different ones but these two are the most popular.

Basically, a DE is what is on the outside. Linux is what is on the inside.

Gnome and KDE are very different. Trust me, once you try each out you will know that =)

### Post by SuperDiscoMachine V.5.7-3 on 2006-02-20
[QUOTE=*John*]
KDE is evil? Its development actually goes against the ethos of the GPL licence? Even though the final product is ultimately free?
[/quote]
No. Qt, the library KDE uses, used to be under a lisence that was not compatible with the GPL. That was I think the main reason why Gnome was started. However, Qt now has been under the GPL for iirc 5 years, so the initial reason for creating Gnome or for considering KDE evil is gone.

---

### Post by Kvark on 2006-02-20
[QUOTE=*John*]Why are there 2 versions then?

I still dont really "get" the difference between the 2..... apart from a slightly different interface and different default software bundles.... they seem...... well....... pretty much the same?

Why 2 versions?

:-k * Brain IMPLODES trying to work it out..... :cry: *[/QUOTE]
A Window Manager is a program that decides where and how to display other programs and what to display besides the other programs (if it should put window borders around the other programs, panels/docks at edge of the screen, pop up menues etc). There are many different opinions about where and when programs should be displayed and what is needed besides the programs so there are many different window managers.

Gnome, KDE and XFCE are desktop environments, a desktop environment is a window manager backed up by other tools such as a file browser to form a complete user environment.

So the differences between the different window managers and desktop environments are both everything you see around and besides your programs and what file browser and other tools they come with.

---

### Post by imagine on 2006-02-20
[QUOTE=*John*]If their both essentially "Linux" why the 2 versions then?

As a noob, it seems to me, well..... weird? 2 versions of Linux that do exactly the same thing, even look similar, but each others software wont work on the other one?[/QUOTE]
Hmmm you'll have to learn a lot.
Strictly speaking, Linux is a piece of low level software that "talks" with the hardware [1]. Often complete operating systems based on Linux are *also* referred to as GNU/Linux distributions [2], or short just "Linux". However in the latter case there is of course not one single Linux (distribution) but countless many, one of them being Ubuntu.
When someone says he is using Linux, he isn't only running the Linux kernel on his computer but one or many Linux distributions.

And about the windows managers, desktop environments, etc you may want to first read another thread in this forum [3].

There are not only two window managers out there but around 20-30 and this isn't weird at all. It would be weird if there was only one. Or what would you say if there only existed for example one text-editor? 
Different people have different needs, different tasks need different applications.

There are about ten million webpages about which window manager is the best but instead of reading any of them I suggest you grab two Live-CDs somewhere, one with KDE and one with Gnome and try them out. You cannot decide which one fits better your needs if you only know one (or none).


[1] [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linux_kernel](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linux_kernel)
[2] [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linux_distribution](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linux_distribution), [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Linux_distributions](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Linux_distributions)
[3] [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=87276](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=87276)

---

The commercial version caused the confusion in my mind. Thanks for enlightening me.

### Post by IYY on 2006-02-20
[QUOTE=*John*]So...... Based on what i've read here:

[http://wiki.linuxquestions.org/wiki/KDE_v_Gnome_history](http://wiki.linuxquestions.org/wiki/KDE_v_Gnome_history)

KDE is evil? Its development actually goes against the ethos of the GPL licence? Even though the final product is ultimately free?

So now I see...... the only "real" difference between Gnome and KDE, is INTERFACE?

So now I think i'll use Gnome out of principal, even if KDE may suit me better!

Thanks Guys! \\:D/[/QUOTE]

Yes, the only real difference between Gnome and KDE is the interface. In fact, that's all that a window manager is - an interface. Underneath both lies the same operating system. However, an interface is a very important thing, much more than just eye candy. It's a set of GUI configuration tools, file and internet browsers, etc.

However, I think that the main difference between Gnome and KDE is the philosophies behind them. Gnome is all about simplicity, whereas KDE is about customizability. If you want something that "just works", choose Gnome. If you want something you can configure in a million different ways, choose KDE. 

Also, I believe that KDE is faster and more responsive on my hardware. Other people claim to have had different experiences.

---

### Post by Iandefor on 2006-02-20
[quote=IYY]Yes, the only real difference between Gnome and KDE is the interface. In fact, that's all that a window manager is - an interface. Underneath both lies the same operating system. However, an interface is a very important thing, much more than just eye candy. It's a set of GUI configuration tools, file and internet browsers, etc.[/quote] No, there are a lot of differences between GNOME and KDE. Different libraries, different widget toolkits, etc, etc. These differences pile up to make different interfaces between them. And GNOME/KDE aren't window managers, they're desktop environments. GNOME's window manager is Metacity, whereas KDE's window manager is Kwin. A window manager handles the windows and the rendering of window decorations and borders, but is only a part of a desktop environment.
 
> 
However, I think that the main difference between Gnome and KDE is the philosophies behind them. Gnome is all about simplicity, whereas KDE is about customizability. If you want something that "just works", choose Gnome. If you want something you can configure in a million different ways, choose KDE.  GNOME is also a wee bit slower when it comes to rolling in new features. For instance: KDE 3.5's kwin supports compositing natively, whereas metacity is just getting composite support in May.

> Also, I believe that KDE is faster and more responsive on my hardware. Other people claim to have had different experiences. In general, this tends to be true. GNOME 2.14 is going to have a lot of improvements in performance over GNOME 2.12, though, so we'll see.

---

### Post by mips on 2006-05-20
What is the difference between Ubuntu and Kubuntu, apart from Gnome/KDE ?
**Nothing.** 

What is the relationship between the two projects?
**The base system is the same. Sponsored by Canonical. Lead dev/proj manager is Jonathan Riddell and he is on canonicals payroll. **

Is Kubuntu less supported than Ubuntu?
**No, not officially. Community support is a bit less but most ubuntu support also applies to kubuntu if the problem is not gnome centric.**
 
Is it less reliable?
**I would not say so from personal experience. **

Is Kubuntu shipped on request, like Ubuntu?
**Yes, but only from Dapper 6.06 onwards will it be shipped via shipit. Previous versions were not shipped.**
 
If you had to recommend either of the two variations, whould your choice depend solely on your preference for Gnome or KDE?
**Yes, personal preference is KDE but yours might differ.**
 
Is KDE 3.4.3 a major upgrade from 3.4.2?
**Dunno **


Can I download Ubuntu and then install KDE from the repositories, or is it better to download Kubuntu if I want KDE?
[B]Choice is yours. Only thing I don't like is you have a mixture of kde & gnome apps which duplicates things if you install the other desktop.
[COLOR="Red"]sudo aptitude install kubuntu-desktop[/COLOR] or [COLOR="Red"]sudo aptitude install ubuntu-desktop[/COLOR]  will install either of the two.[/B]
 
Can I have Gnome and KDE installed at the same time?
**Yes. **

How do I switch between Gnome and KDE?
**At the login screen you click on the session buttom and select which one you want. **

### Post by aysiu on 2006-06-06
For further reading:
[Which is your preferred DE? GNOME or KDE?](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=158036)
[kde or gnome?](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=110554)

And my answer: I prefer both. I switch back and forth between them constantly. I know someone is going to say KDE is more "Windows-like" because it (by default--this can be changed, of course) has one panel at the bottom instead of a panel at the bottom and one at the top as well.

KDE fans will say Gnome doesn't offer enough options.
Gnome fans will say KDE is too complicated--not simple enough.
Both KDE and Gnome fans will say the other looks uglier or less professional.

### Post by Ptero-4 on 2006-06-06
[QUOTE=dimatrod]I prefer Gnome for the reason that it doesn't look like Windows (I've grown bored of the windows feeling), it runs faster (at least I feel it faster), and people that come and use my computer usually are quick to find "Applications" up on top left and select an app easily, whereas people get confused in KDE with several menus. I agree with you on that. I'm bored and mad at all this Windoze look&feel everywhere. Everyone seems to want to make that look&feel the only one available. But gnome is actually 50% Mac-like (which is good) and 50% Windoze-like (which is bad). The 50% Mac-like comes from the layout, the simplicity, the spatial Nautilus, and it's nice and easy themeing. The 50% windoze-like comes from the in-window menubar (the "File Edit View ..." menus in each app window), the lack of a collapsible bottom bar, and lack of color labels.

### Post by fluffington on 2006-06-07
First, a nit pick: GNOME is an acronym ([SIZE=-1]GNU Network Object Model Environment) and should be in all caps.

And on to the question at hand: I prefer GNOME, though there are things I like in both.

Things I like in GNOME: better handling of multihead setups, I prefer the way the default setup looks, it's more stable (at least on Ubuntu, and I'm confident the difference in stability is temporary), Synaptic, GTK, apps don't all start with the same letter (not that GNOME's exactly innocent on this one, but everything starting with K (or G, or X, etc.) makes it hard to find things in the menus), Firefox, and it's better integrated with Ubuntu (again, temporary).

And some things I like in KDE: KIOSlaves, DCOP, Kate, the print system, KOffice, address bars in the Open File dialog, order of OK/Cancel buttons, and the infinite customizability.
[/SIZE]

### Post by aysiu on 2006-06-07
[QUOTE=njf]Can gnome allow one to use different wallpapers on different visual desktops? I can't figure out how to do such simple thing in gnome.[/QUOTE] Well, this is the big debate, isn't it?

Gnome users like the simplicity of Gnome.
KDE like the configurability of KDE.

I happen to like simplicity and configurability, so I bounce back and forth between the two. Someone, in a recent thread, just asked how to print-screen to the clipboard instead of to a file. My solution--use KSnapshot. I couldn't see anything in *gnome-screenshot* to copy to clipboard. I even tried ```
man gnome-screenshot
``` and I got: ```
No manual entry for gnome-screenshot
See 'man 7 undocumented' for help when manual pages are not available.
``` Gnome does, in fact, have a beautiful simplicity to it, but if you want to do complicated configurations, you've got to learn your way around *gconf-editor*, learn the Gnome configuration files in your home directory, and/or dig around for third-party applications to help you do stuff (like rotating wallpapers).

### Post by aysiu on 2006-06-07
I really wish I could mix and match. I was using KDE for a couple of months. Just last week I switched back to Gnome.

Immediately after the switch back, there are things I'm thankful for having back and other things I'm mad about not having any more.

A few examples:

1. Renaming files: in KDE, you press F2 and the whole filename (including the extension) gets highlighted for renaming. In Gnome, you press F2 and only the name (not the extension) gets highlighted.

2. Screensavers: in KDE, if I choose slideshow, I can immediately choose what folder I want the slideshow from. I thought this option was available in Breezy for Gnome, but apparently in Dapper... it's gone. "Simplicity" got pushed a little too far there!

3. Screenshots: On the one hand, I love *gnome-screenshot* because it just goes straight to saving the file--one key does a full-screen shot, another does a window shot. It's annoying that I have to go to the Configuration Editor to get a keyboard shortcut for ```
gnome-screenshot --delay 5
``` but that's a one-time setup thing.

4. Image info: Konqueror has image info in the tooltips (height and width) and a special "image" view that has thumbnails with specifications on one side and the enlarged image on the other side. In Nautilus, I have to right-click on the file, select Properties and then Image to see the height and width.

5. The Trash: In Gnome I can create a keyboard shortcut for emptying the trash. If I create the same keyboard shortcut for emptying the trash in KDE, the files get removed, but the picture doesn't get refreshed.

Well... you get the picture. I'm loving Gnome right now, but I'll probably switch back to KDE in a month or two.

### Post by gray-squirrel on 2006-06-07
[QUOTE=aysiu]
KDE fans will say Gnome doesn't offer enough options.
Gnome fans will say KDE is too complicated--not simple enough.
Both KDE and Gnome fans will say the other looks uglier or less professional.[/QUOTE]

I think they offer the same amount of options (as far as configuring the system goes) from what I've seen for the past year, it's just the presentation of those options that is different.

I actually switched to KDE some time ago because memory and resources were not being used up as quickly under KDE (at least for my system).  Also, KDE does not automatically mount removable media, which is a good thing: my Lexar flash drive was trashed when I was working in Gnome.  I don't know if the feature was there, but I don't think I ever saw a feature similar to the "Safely Remove" in Konqueror.  I know about [FONT="Courier New"]sudo umount /media/sda1[/FONT] that I could have used in the terminal in Gnome, but I tend to forget.  Anyway, I can trust that my data will be reasonably safe if I manually mount and remove the flash drive.

On the other hand, aRts is so annoying; it's like the referee for a game that never required one.  I had trouble getting sounds to consistently play for a time within KDE 3.4.x.  Sometimes after patching a kernel, I had no sound at all.  Each time there was a problem, aRts was connected to it somehow.  Fortunately, aRts will be dropped when KDE 4.0 is released.  :-D 

Well, seeing the nice additions to KDE after I installed Dapper has convinced me to stay right where I am.  There is no way I will be bored for the next four months or so.

### Post by ShanghaiTeej on 2006-06-07
I like both a great deal, but I'm stick with Gnome right now for these reasons:

1. Gnome is more aesthetically pleasing to me (I love Cairo)
2. I like the elegance of Gnome's dictionary applet
3. Evoution integrates into Gnome's taskbar calendar
4. Deskbar applet is an awesome application that I can't live without (I hotkeyed it to <alt>spacebar like Katapult)
5. Ubuntu's weather-applet works while Kubuntu's Kweather does not
6. The integration of GTK in firefox.
7. Gnome now has Glipper
8. Integrates better into xgl/compiz at the moment
9. Gnome's system monitor is more detailed than KDE's ksysguard.
10. Nautilus is all I really need and I like how its a dedicated file manager (certainly makes conifguring it a lot easier than konqueror)
11.  I love the look of Tango Icons, but can't easily incorporate those into KDE yet.
********
But I use a lot more KDE apps than Gnome apps like: Amarok (although Listen, Banshee and Exaile are both great projects to look out for), K3b, Kdissert, RSIbreak (which is easier to use for me than Workrave), Keep, Ktorrent and Gwenview.  I'd like to use Kontact, but it isn't integrated into Gnome's desktop.  I also like the constant progress in Koffice, it's really great.

But I'm excited for KDE4.  I really respect the integrity of the KDevolopers, especially Aaron Seigo.

### Post by Jucato on 2006-06-08

The problem is, the default look varies from distro to distro. For example, KNOPPIX, IMHO, has the most cluttered defaults, while Kubuntu has cleaner ones. My point is, there is no "default" default look for any DE, because it has been left to the distro's devs to adjust. Unless of course someone makes a distro that has pure GNOME/KDE, without any alterations whatsoever.

But anyway, arguing whether one DE is cleaner or messier is useless. One person might see something as clean, while another person might see the same thing as incomplete. One person might see something as complete, another person might see the same thing as an utter mess. 

You can't please everybody. Thank goodness there are so many choices in Linux.

### Post by aysiu on 2006-06-16
*Bloat* is a vague term that's non-descriptive but insulting. The noun form, as used here, means essentially *the quality of being swollen*.

People like to throw around vague terms like *Windows-like*, *user-friendly*, *intuitive*, *professional*, *clean*, *ugly*, and *confusing*, because they don't want to be challenged, and they don't want to be bothered to be specific.

Gnome and KDE just have different philosophical approaches. You should pick which one works for you. Gnome tends to go for fewer configuration options and even fewer graphically-accessible configuration options. KDE tends to go for as many configuration options as possible and as many graphically-accessible ones as possible.

Critics of Gnome generally attack it for having too few options--it's not functional enough. Critics of KDE generally attack it for having too many options--it distracts people from getting work done.

Oddly enough, oftentimes the people who are attracted to Gnome ask for features that are available only in KDE (Mac-like toolbar or rotating wallpaper). Go figure.

---

### Post by Tazix on 2006-06-16
[QUOTE=bruce89]That's the first time I have heard GNOME being described as bloated![/QUOTE]

Heh.... not as bloated as KDE... but it does use a lot more memory and needs a ton more libraries than the "more lightweight alternatives".

XFCE has come a long way in terms of comparable functionality, and Xubuntu is a great "out of the box" solution... even on a modern machine. (Who says you have to run lightweight on a 486 only?)

ICEWM... while even lighter weight... you have to install things like ROX Filer and Panel (for icons on the desktop with drag and drop functionality), or idesk and xfe... a lot of manual configuration for set up, and a lot of manual configuration for installing new software.

FLUXBOX... same situation as ICEWM.

EQUINOX is looking good... but still fairly buggy.

JWM has been around longer than EQUINOX, but still as buggy... so I don't know if that one will ever be stable.

That said... XFCE has the most functionality, out of the box, although it's not as lightweight as the other ones I just mentioned.

### Post by Tazix on 2006-06-16
[QUOTE=darkhatter]When does something go from having lots of features, to bloat. I don't get this so if some cares to explain to me I would be very happy[/QUOTE]

Well... I suppose it's different per person.

For me... "non-bloated" is basically a full featured gui desktop that doesn't go beyond using the system resources, of say, Windows 98. (at idle... aka desktop sitting there waiting for you to launch something).  This, of course, doesn't include server daemons, or startup programs like anti-virus, etc.

This includes system memory, cpu utilization, and disk space, (no matter how cheap, the larger capacity and faster hardware is getting).

I want as much functionality as possible, with as little impact on my system as possible.  I'm an efficiency nut, I guess. "Streamlined" would probably be a more appropriate word for what I'm talking about, though.

My (multiple) apps are supposed to suck up all my resources... not my Desktop. ;)

So if you guys are still thinking I'm throwing out "vague terms" just to be insulting....  I don't know what to tell ya.

-Taz

### Post by Tazix on 2006-06-16
[QUOTE=aysiu]So Kubuntu and Ubuntu are bloated, then, I guess, by your definition.

Xubuntu isn't, though, right?[/QUOTE]

Yes, as far as the desktop utilizing system resources, goes.

There's also another 'type' (if you will) bloat... "distro bloat".

Example: Mandrake, Fedora, a few other "full blown" distros...

I don't need 4 web browsers, 5 gui text editors, 4 terminal text editors (including crappy VIM), 3 e-mail applications, 4 media players, etc. etc. installed by **Default**.  It's ridiculous.  And if you try to "pick and choose" exactly what you want durring the installation process... you could be there days, since a zillion packages are listed (Ok, that's an exaggeration... but you get the point).  Also, more often than not... if you "pick and choose" exactly what you want... the desktop menu is way incomplete.... especially if you didn't choose packages that are in the distro's "default" bundle.

None of the Ubuntu variants seem to have 'Distro Bloat', though I've never tried Kubuntu.  So that's a good thing. ;)

### Post by asimon on 2006-06-17
[QUOTE=Tazix]There's also another 'type' (if you will) bloat... "distro bloat".

Example: Mandrake, Fedora, a few other "full blown" distros...

I don't need 4 web browsers, 5 gui text editors, 4 terminal text editors (including crappy VIM), 3 e-mail applications, 4 media players, etc. etc. installed by **Default**.  It's ridiculous.  And if you try to "pick and choose" exactly what you want durring the installation process... you could be there days, since a zillion packages are listed (Ok, that's an exaggeration... but you get the point).  Also, more often than not... if you "pick and choose" exactly what you want... the desktop menu is way incomplete.... especially if you didn't choose packages that are in the distro's "default" bundle.[/quote]

Actually it makes some sense because you satisfy more people with the default installation. If you for example left out vim you disappoint a lot of people who expect that vi should exist on any system. You install just one single media player? Well, fine for the fans of that specific player, but many people want to use other players (therefore we have so many), and these people have to install them extra, which is inconvinient, especially if you have to do this for many apps.

Although there is some bloat in having multiple apps of the same type installed, I think if the result is to satisfy more people by default in that they find their favourite apps to be already part of the installation then this is more valuable than to save a few cheap mega-bytes on the hard disc.

Of course it's a balancing act. Having 20 email clients is surely too much, but if you can satisfy say 82% of the users with having two email clients installed, I think it's not that bad.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2006-06-17
When I first started using Linux (with Mepis), I loved having multiple applications for the same task. It gave me a chance to explore different programs and compare them easily (as a beginner, I wouldn't have known what programs to install and try or how to install them). It also gave me an immediate sense of the wealth of open source applications that are out there.

Now, though, as a more advanced beginner, I prefer a slimmed-down install with one application per task. If I don't like the default, I don't have to replace four defaults with my one default--I can replace only one with my one.

---

### Post by Tazix on 2006-06-18
[QUOTE=asimon]Actually it makes some sense because you satisfy more people with the default installation. If you for example left out vim you disappoint a lot of people who expect that vi should exist on any system. You install just one single media player? Well, fine for the fans of that specific player, but many people want to use other players (therefore we have so many), and these people have to install them extra, which is inconvinient, especially if you have to do this for many apps.

Although there is some bloat in having multiple apps of the same type installed, I think if the result is to satisfy more people by default in that they find their favourite apps to be already part of the installation then this is more valuable than to save a few cheap mega-bytes on the hard disc.

Of course it's a balancing act. Having 20 email clients is surely too much, but if you can satisfy say 82% of the users with having two email clients installed, I think it's not that bad.[/QUOTE]

While I understand what you are saying... and can agree that there are users out there that want to experiment with everything they can, "right out of the box"...  your average winblows user / linux newbie is most likey going to be intimidated and overwhelmed with several apps that do basically the same thing.... espeically with all those "strange" names (vim, ed, nano, kwrite, gedit, tea, leafpad, mousepad, emacs, pico, jed, joe, mcedit, nedit, sam, beaver, acme, editpad, kate, zile, yudit, scite, etc. etc. etc.)

Even as an intermediate to advanced user (by far, not a guru expert)... I prefer a good base, then just install whatever extra I want.  So if you look at an overall range of users... beginners and advanced users are more likely to prefer a solid base, than a "shotgun blast" of including near everything installed by default. (IMO)  That's not saying there aren't users out there that want to try everything with as little installation woes as possible... I just don't think that a distro needs to blast a bunch of apps pre-installed in order to attract people to try the distro in question.

-Taz

### Post by aysiu on 2006-06-19

its sad that kde had to create this site but it should help keep the kde-haters quiet

I can't wait till kde 4 :mrgreen: :mrgreen: :mrgreen:[/QUOTE]
This is probably the most often repeated myth in these forums: > **Myth  	KDE Looks Like Windows**
=================
**Facts 	**

KDE looks how you want it to look. You can make it look unique, or you can make it look like another desktop environment (such as Windows, Mac OS X, Gnome, CDE etc).

The KDE 3 default settings do indeed resemble Windows in areas such as having the panel on the bottom of the screen rather than on top. This is simply because the bottom of the screen is where most computer users expect to find the panel.

However, you can move the panel to any side of the screen by simply dragging it there with your mouse. You can also add extra panels, if you want more of them.

If the KDE 3 default settings, which are meant to cater to the expectations of most users, have made you assume that KDE does not innovate, your fear is fortunately unfounded: one of the most important goals for KDE 4 is breaking out of the WIMP (Window/Icon/Menu/Pointer) cage and creating a desktop workspace fit for today's realities. The Plasma team has been assembled to do just this.

### Post by steveneddy on 2006-06-19
[QUOTE=aysiu]For further reading:
[Which is your preferred DE? GNOME or KDE?](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=158036)
[kde or gnome?](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=110554)

And my answer: I prefer both. I switch back and forth between them constantly. I know someone is going to say KDE is more "Windows-like" because it (by default--this can be changed, of course) has one panel at the bottom instead of a panel at the bottom and one at the top as well.

KDE fans will say Gnome doesn't offer enough options.
Gnome fans will say KDE is too complicated--not simple enough.
Both KDE and Gnome fans will say the other looks uglier or less professional.[/QUOTE]

I agree with **aysiu**. I use many different DE's. I like them all and sometimes I just want a very fast and easy dasktop. So I boot Fluxbox. When I want to show how Linux **CAN** look like windows, I boot into KDE, I also like IceWM because of the options of radically different looks one can get during one given session. **AND** it's very **FAST**!

Make your own decision. They are all great in thier own little ways and you will find the one you like to use best if you just try asd many as you care to install. They will all be available at the logon screen.

Enjoy. -SE

[QUOTE=Tazix]
espeically with all those "strange" names (vim, ed, nano, kwrite, gedit, tea, leafpad, mousepad, emacs, pico, jed, joe, mcedit, nedit, sam, beaver, acme, editpad, kate, zile, yudit, scite, etc. etc. etc.)[/quote]
I think application names are no longer a problem. At least in KDE's menu those apps can appear as "Name (Description)", "Decription only", or "Description (Name)". So it's mostly an issue of wrong defaults or bad descriptions and bad menu layout (Like one terminal app is under System, an other under Utilities). 

And searching for "editor" in the package managers gets you emacs, vim, kate , and others too.

Even as an intermediate to advanced user (by far, not a guru expert)... I prefer a good base, then just install whatever extra I want. ### Post by fluffington on 2006-06-20
[QUOTE=Mr. Wizard]Well, since you prefer both and switch back and forth between them constantly, maybe you can tell me what I need to install/configure so my AMD64 system will allow me to do the same.  I can't seem to find as many interesting programming tools for GNOME as I can for KDE, at least not when I look through the list presented by Add/Remove Applications. :-(

KDevelop C/C++ looks like what I am seeking and it will apparently word under GNOME, but says it is designed for the KDE environment as its name suggests. Kscope looks interesting, too. Are there no devtools for GNOME, or am I missing something?[/QUOTE]

Here's the list of GNOME dev tools I use or have used in the past:
[list]
[*][Anjuta](http://anjuta.sourceforge.net/) - C/C++ IDE
[*][Screem](http://www.screem.org/) - Web development
[*][Bluefish](http://bluefish.openoffice.nl/) - More web development (I prefer this one over Screem)
[*][medit](http://mooedit.sourceforge.net/) - A GNOME equivalent to KDE's Kate (and AFAIK not in the repos)
[*][gedit](http://www.gnome.org/projects/gedit/) - GNOME's default text editor; has decent syntax highlighting, and after enabling a few default plugins, a Python console and configurable external commands
[/list]

Of course, I feel that KDE's options are better all around in this area. I prefer KDevelop to Anjuta, Quanta Plus to either Screem or Bluefish, and Kate to (m|g)edit.

### Post by Tazix on 2006-06-20
[QUOTE=darkhatter]This is a web site that talks about all the kde MYTHS that are floating around:

[http://kdemyths.urbanlizard.com/](http://kdemyths.urbanlizard.com/)

its sad that kde had to create this site but it should help keep the kde-haters quiet[/QUOTE]

First... I don't hate either KDE or GNOME. (I know I said that "I dispise the bloat that they have become"... but that doesn't mean I dispise the environments, themselves.  You have to understand that I've been fighting Windows bloat on every flippin new release).

Quote from that site you linked:[QUOTE=Kdemyths]As an environment, **KDE is not and never will be as "light" as Blackbox, Window Maker or XFCE**. On the other hand, KDE offers much more in the way of functionality than they do. This functionality requires resources, the question is simply one of how much.[/QUOTE]

Ok... I would say that the same situation applies to GNOME.

XFCE provides (out of the box) a ton of functionality (especially 4.4) for it's "lightweightness".  That's the point... it's more efficient.  It may not have all the Bells and Whistles of KDE or GNOME, but it more than gets the job done easily and efficiently.  Then you install whatever packages you want to cover what it can't do compared to KDE or GNOME.

Same goes for the other "Lightweight" solutions... they just take a lot more configuation / extra apps to get them as functional, but still only utilize minimal system resources.

I agree that it's a trade off... functionality vs. gobbling up system resources.

As an efficiency freak... I like to start smaller and grow the environment to my exact needs... Not start big, then scramble to figure out how to "trim the fat".

I am also "App neutral".  Some apps, I prefer GNOME based... some apps I prefer KDE based, and some apps I prefer generic X based.  While this may seem like a 'bloaty approach', I actually end up installing less libraries for the GNOME and KDE apps, than I would running either a full GNOME or KDE environment, then installing minimal libraries for the opposite environment's apps.

I'm not trying to come off like an XFCE fanboi (I actually prefer IceWM, truth be told... but we're talking about an "out of the box solution")... but even the site you linked admits that XFCE won't ever be beat by KDE as far as efficiency goes. ;)

-Taz

I voted GNOME. I've tried to like KDE I really have but have never felt comfortable using it. In my case this may well be because I started off using Fedora (which also has GNOME as its DE), and often what you start off using is what you prefer.

And I'm grateful for the work the KDE team do, since I mix and match apps. and many of the apps I prefer are KDE ones. ### Post by aysiu on 2006-07-16
> **Fenyx said:**
> Can we let sleeping threads lie?

@aysiu, rather than have one megathread per topic, why not a sub-section for "hot" topics like this. I think it will save the mods some work in combining threads, and it will be less intimidating to read (5 threads with more or less 15 pages each vs. 1 single thread with around 100 pages?)
I don't see how a sub-section saves the mods any trouble, as people who don't post in appropriate places (say, the KDE v. Gnome megathread) would not post in the appropriate sub-section, and the mods would have to move it to the sub-section... which is about as much work as just merging it with the megathread.

And I think having five threads about the same thing is more intimidating than one thread, as it means you have more you have to keep track of if all are updated. No one's going to read *all* the posts.

So it doesn't matter if it's 10,000 posts in five threads or 10,000 posts in one thread. No one is going to read 10,000 posts either way. So how is having five (or more) threads advantageous?

But if there are twenty recent posts about KDE v. Gnome, wouldn't you rather read the most recent twenty posts in one thread than the most recent twenty posts spread out over five threads?

### Post by Jucato on 2006-07-18
> **ComplexNumber said:**
> hehe. you couldn't be further from the truth. kde-look is about 90% wallpapers and 'other' because kde theming is crippled. the only aspects of the actual theme engine(ie style) that can be changed is the colour scheme.  whereas, in gnome, the engines can be mixed and matched within one theme, the colour scheme changed, and the style and size of all the widgets can be changed. etc.

I can't seem to reconcile this with my experience, and I've been using GNOME exclusively for close to a month now. 

*"kde-look is about 90% wallpapers and 'other'."*
Care to back this up with actualy figures/examples? There are tons of of 'other' stuff in kde-look that would definitely exceed more than 25% of the total content of the site. But even if it were true, even if wallpapers made up more than 75% of the site, it would only be natural. Tell me, which would be more easier to make, from the point of view of a newbie/intermediate linux user: wallpapers or widget/control themes? 

*"the only aspects of the actual theme engine(ie style) that can be changed is the colour scheme"*
Really? For one, the color scheme is actually a separate "theme" from the style. Also, you seem to be implying that the theme engine = style theme. FYI, there are different theme engines, most notably those that control the styles, window decorations, and splash screens. KDE styles are the equivalent of GNOME controls. They are just one part of the whole picture.

"whereas, in gnome, the engines can be mixed and matched within one theme, the colour scheme changed, and the style and size of all the widgets can be changed. etc."

GNOME does not yet have a way to change color schemes separately from the control themes. At least, not yet in the version used by Ubuntu. 

And who says you can't "mix and match" themes, styles, and colors in KDE?

When it comes to themes/appearances, both KDE and GNOME practically have the same features. They just call those different components by different names. They also present different ways to modify/customize these parts. 

I know of only 2 areas where KDE has advantage over GNOME in this area, at least for now: color schemes (soon to be a new feature in the next GNOME release, AFAIK) and different wallpapers for different workspaces (a Metacity/Nautilus issue, again AFAIK).

If there's one thing that GNOME has an advantage over KDE when it comes to themes, it's the drag & drop feature in the Theme manager.

---

### Post by Jucato on 2006-07-18
> **sadscientist said:**
> (I'm also in the K-prepended-to-every-app-name hatin' camp.  This isn't Mortal Kombat.)

Then you might be pleased to know that this "tradition" is slowly being dropped in favor of more sensible naming conventions. Of course, those that are already well established would probably not be changed (Konqueror, Kate [which is actually an acronym, not a real word], KOffice, etc.). But some others have already changed (amaroK to Amarok, oKular to Okular, etc.). 

I personally don't find the "K" naming convention when they make sense. It becomes a bit akward though, when the names are forced (akregator comes to mind...).

Btw, just an FYI, Krita and Kopete are real words in some foreign language. I just forgot which ones.

### Post by ComplexNumber on 2006-07-18
**Fenyx**
 [this]("http://www.kde-look.org/content/files/42731-Crises.kcsrc") is a colour mod for kde. contrary to what you believe, changing the colour scheme in gnome is exactly the same - ie just changing the gtkrc file. i made my own colour mod of clearlooks recently by doing just that.

btw FYI what you refer to as 'a theme engine' is merely the equivelent of the index.theme file in gnome and the theme manager theme in kde. that is not an engine. the engine is a library that draws the widgets, and it goes in /usr/lib....not in /usr/share.

### Post by Jucato on 2006-07-18
> **ComplexNumber said:**
