 [this]("http://www.kde-look.org/content/files/42731-Crises.kcsrc") is a colour mod for kde. contrary to what you believe, changing the colour scheme in gnome is exactly the same - ie just changing the gtkrc file. i made my own colour mod of clearlooks recently by doing just that.

This is how you change colors in KDE.

[IMG]http://i73.photobucket.com/albums/i206/jucato/screenshots/kdecolors_small.png[/IMG]

Of course, it's just a front-end for the changing the .kcsrc file, but it's a hell lot easier than manually modifying the configuration file. You can easily import and export/save other color schemes/kcsrc files, too.

### Post by ComplexNumber on 2006-07-18
> Of course, it's just a front-end for the changing the .kcsrc file, but it's a hell lot easier than manually modifying the configuration file. well, somebody has had to write that kcsrc colour scheme...in the same way that someone wrote a colour mod of the gtkrc file. what do you do if a particular colour scheme is not available? you can't just specify somewhere in the kcontrol that you would like the background of the application widget to be black, the frames to be indigo, etc. 
effectively, its exactly the same as it is in gnome...and neither are perfect, yet.

---

### Post by Jucato on 2006-07-18
> **ComplexNumber said:**
> what do you do if a particular colour scheme is not available? you can't just specify somewhere in the kcontrol that you would like the background of the application widget to be black, the frames to be indigo, etc.

You open up KControl/System settings, go to the Appearance/Color module, and just change it. You don't need to download a color scheme. As long as you can fire up KControl, there's nothing to stop you from changing, creating, or removing color schemes. You don't need any pre-existing color schemes. You don't need to have a .kcsrc file. You can "mix and match" to your liking. You can create your own if you want. You can modify any pre-existing color scheme and save it as a new scheme. All within the GUI. You wouldn't even know that a kcsrc exists unless you actually look for it or know that it exists already. Heck, you don't even have to know what a kcsrc file is (but it's good to know about it).

EDIT: Just in case you don't believe me, here are two more screenshots. The first one shows a drop down list where you can choose which part of the window/control you want to modify. The second one shows the color chooser.

[IMG]http://i73.photobucket.com/albums/i206/jucato/screenshots/kdecolors1.png[/IMG]

[IMG]http://i73.photobucket.com/albums/i206/jucato/screenshots/kdecolors2.png[/IMG]

GTK-qt-libraries which enable using gtk apps more native in a qt environment and vice versa comes from... uhu KDE. its you who doesn't know what   he's talking about. first you need understand that gstreamer, dbus, cairo, and all the others are not gnome technologies - they are freedesktop technologies. and 80-90% of them have been implemented more quickly on gnome. to reiterate because you obviously don't fully understand, they are COMMON TECHNOLOGIES  (ie that are to be shared by both). so kde is clearly not "one light year ahead" because kde is either only just starting to implement them (eg dbus, gstreamer, etc) or hasn't got that far yet(eg cairo/arthur etc).

---

### Post by Jucato on 2006-08-04
> **ComplexNumber said:**
> its you who doesn't know what   he's talking about. first you need understand that gstreamer, dbus, cairo, and all the others are not gnome technologies - they are freedesktop technologies. and 80-90% of them have been implemented more quickly on gnome. to reiterate because you obviously don't fully understand, they are COMMON TECHNOLOGIES  (ie that are to be shared by both). so kde is clearly not "one light year ahead" because kde is either only just starting to implement them (eg dbus, gstreamer, etc) or hasn't got that far yet(eg cairo/arthur etc).

Again with the discussions... but I just can't help myself:

1. D-Bus was inspired by DCOP. DCOP came before D-Bus. D-Bus came about because DCOP was a KDE-only implementation of the concept. 
2. GStreamer is younger than aRts. And compared to the now defunct aRts it is indeed better. However, KDE will not implement a "one multimedia framework to rule them all" thing again, because of its history.
3. Can't comment on Cairo.

These "common technologies", as you call it, are only "easier" to implement in GNOME because GNOME doesn't have anything else to implement anyway. You see, when D-Bus came along, DCOP was already well integrated into KDE, so it was not feasible to immediately implement D-Bus on the same level that DCOP is implemented. When GStreamer (and freedesktop.org for that matter) came along, KDE was already using aRts, and was, unfortunately, tied down to it. Make no mistake, they hated that fact. That's why they're trying to make sure it won't happen again.

Some more facts: DCOP has been dropped in KDE 4. They're working on D-Bus now. While it may seem that KDE is "late", you have to take into account that they were using a similar system before D-Bus became a freedesktop.org standard. So by KDE 4, KDE already had DCOP then D-Bus. GNOME started with D-Bus immediately.

Also don't think that KDE 4 won't support GStreamer. Through Phonon, KDE will be able to support GStreamer PLUS xine PLUS NMM PLUS etc. So GNOME will have GStreamer. KDE can have GStreamer+Xine+NMM+...

A lightyear is a measure of distance, and distance can be relative. It's not so easy to make "objective" distinctions. What matters more is that they are both evolving and moving forward, in their own pace, in their own style.

---

### Post by asimon on 2006-08-04
> **ComplexNumber said:**
> exactly. all i'm saying is that kde is not lightyears ahead of gnome(as erronously claimed by garba), because common desktop standards is all that we have to compare.
There is much more to compare. How easy is it to develop with the given environment? How good are the APIs? How much fun is it developing for the environment? How productive are you? Do you have to follow every HIG thing by hand, or does to toolkit automate a lot of stuff? How good is the infrastructure and code sharing? What about protability? How good are existing reusable components? How helpful is the developer community? etc. There is much more to compare.

> **asimon said:**

Do you have to follow every HIG thing by hand, or does to toolkit automate a lot of stuff? How good is the infrastructure and code sharing? What about protability? How good are existing reusable components? How helpful is the developer community? etc. There is much more to compare.

### Post by asimon on 2006-08-04
> **ComplexNumber said:**
> if *both* gnome and kde have implemented these share technologies already or are due to, then they're common standards.
Maybe but I prefer to call them pseudo-standards as long as there is no formal standards organization involved. (Of course a technology doesn't need to be a standard to be good or used by different parties.)

> **ComplexNumber said:**
> 
almost everything you've stated there is subjective from the users point of view. for you, its kde. for me, its gnome. for someone else, its X(whatever). they're poor and difficult to quantify criterias to compare.
Actually the things I stated aren't important for (most) users at all, most were things only developers care for. They are subjective, but I would not call them poor, for a developer these things can be very important. But isn't the usage of dbus, cairo, gstreamer subjective too? There may be various reasons to like or dislike them. Anyway from a user's stand point I really don't care if gstreamer, xine, or somewhat else is used, if cairo or arthur is used to paint. A user cares more if his sound and video is working, if the quality is good, that there is no clipping or latency or syncronization problems, that the application is easy to use and does what the user wants, etc. I doubt that it's important for the user if his application is linked against libgstreamer or libxine or something else.

But if you want to compare technologies, I think it's very worthwhile to take a developer's stand point because they are the ones who actually work directly with these technologies whereas the users usually only use applications which are based on various technologies but don't get their hands dirty with the technologies themselves.

### Post by aysiu on 2006-09-16
Um, what you're saying isn't true.

There are several metapackages, and they have varying degrees of bloat.

You'll see in Synaptic Package Manager, there is *gnome*, *gnome-desktop-environment*, and *ubuntu-desktop*. Likewise, for KDE, there is *kde*, *kde-core*, and *kubuntu-desktop*. If you want Gnome without all the extra applications, you shouldn't install *gnome*. Likewise, if you want KDE without all the extra applications, you shouldn't install *kde*.

*kde-core* is a very simple and basic set of KDE packages. If you want even less bloat than that, you can install *kdebase*, which has the bare minimum to run KDE.

I don't really see what your beef is actually--in both Kubuntu and Ubuntu, there is only one application per task.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2006-10-19
There are things KDE can do that Gnome either plain cannot do or cannot do without the help of external applications.

The only ones I can think of off the top of my head are:

1. Configure numlock behavior at login
2. Have multiple wallpapers per desktop or a slideshow of wallpapers
3. Have a Mac-like "universal" toolbar

There may be more, but that's all I can think of right now. You can certainly say those things don't matter to you, but they are, in fact, things KDE can do that Gnome can't.

Personally, I don't care. I like Gnome, KDE, XFCE, and IceWM. Each has something to offer that others can't. I don't advocate one as being the be-all and end-all of environments. Use what works for you.

### Post by aysiu on 2006-10-19
You asked for things KDE could do that Gnome couldn't. That's what I gave examples of. Whether they're cosmetic or not wasn't an issue before.

**Edit**: I stand corrected. I re-read your earlier post--you were talking specifically about "functionality."  I guess it depends what you consider "functionality." Some people consider the ability to change cosmetic stuff... functionality.

Still, numlock is not cosmetic. It's functional. Of course, if it's that important to you, and you love Gnome, you can install *numlockx*. There's no "Edit as root" in Nautilus, but you can create one with Nautilus scripts. You can't view and sort music files by their ID3 tag information in Nautilus, but you can in Konqueror.

The bottom line--if you want maximum configurability in the GUI with minimal fuss, you should use KDE. There are many things that can't be done in easily in Gnome but that *can* be done. Then, there are some things that can't be done at all.

This doesn't make KDE *better* than Gnome. It just suits a different kind of user.

### Post by maniacmusician on 2006-10-22
lol, a GNOME zealot at war with a KDE zealot. what a bore.

Both are decent for me, but here's an example of a reaction i recently got when converting a friend to linux. first, I gave an ubuntu liveCD to mess around with. He found it pretty easy to use, and after mucking around a bit with the menus, he figured out where stuff was.

Then, I gave him a Kubuntu liveCD. he followed the same process as in GNOME. loaded up Kopete for IMs, looked in System Settings to see what he could do, and his reaction was "holy shit! so many options!" and he played with it for hours. he ended up choosing kubuntu in the end, and when I asked him which one was easier to use, surprisingly, he replied kubuntu. It seemed to me that he had a much easier time with GNOME, but he said that KDE made him feel like he had a million choices and he could really do whatever he wanted, whereas with GNOME, even though he could find the basic things he needed, there were nowhere near the amount of options.

Which, i guess is the fundamental difference between KDE and GNOME. K chooses to have all its things built in with many options, whereas gnome allows people to add stuff onto it later to get the exact functionality they want.

---

### Post by tubasoldier on 2006-10-22
> **fuscia said:**
> how is kde more functional than gnome? i hear that a lot but, being an end user (i guess), i don't see it. even during the height of my kde nuthuggery, i still was under the impression gnome was more functional. i'm not arguing with you. i just don't know.

In Konqueor: 
  You can ssh into a machine using fish://
  audiocd:// lets you rip music from an audio cd. its quite easy. cut and paste
  kamera:// will allow you to connect to your camera directly. Transfer your pictures by a simple cut and paste.

The Menu is quite configurable. I dont consider this to be cosmetic. I consider this to make it easier to use it.

KDE print system is nice.

Konsole has more functionality. try using it. There are more options and more configuration. This is just the way KDE does things.


Now that I have pointed out a few funtionality items I would also like to say that these KDE vs. GNOME threads are quite obnoxious.:mad:  If you want to configure everything to the minute detail then use KDE. If you want to work and not worry about what your system looks like or how it functions then use GNOME. GNOME just lets you get down to business. then there are people who like to change everything, thats where KDE comes in. I have used both and I like both. 

When it comes to speed they are both just as "slow/fast" as the other. If you dont believe me then Google it. Benchmarks have been done and they both function at very close to the same speed.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2006-10-22
> **tubasoldier said:**
> 
Now that I have pointed out a few funtionality items I would also like to say that these KDE vs. GNOME threads are quite obnoxious. I agree with your general sentiment. The word I would use (instead of *obnoxious*) is *unnecessary*.

You don't have to choose. You can use both/either. And your choice doesn't stop others from choosing something else.

If someone has a factual question (Can KDE do ____? Can Gnome do ____?), it makes sense to get the factual answer to that question. But once you start talking about which is "better," the discussion makes about as much sense as discussing "best" ice cream flavor or "best" movie--it ultimately comes down to personal preference.

And I agree--I've read people adamantly asserting that KDE is faster than Gnome and others just as adamantly asserting that gnome is faster than KDE. If speed is your thing, try both and see which is faster **on your computer**.

---

### Post by Sushi on 2006-10-23

### Post by qalimas on 2006-10-23
I prefer KDE.  I find it faster, and I just prefer the amount of customizing I can accomplish on it.  I want MY desktop MY way.  I'm comfortable in GNOME, and sometimes (rarely) in Windows.  But, in KDE, it looks EXACTLY like I want it to, so I'm much more comfortable, a personal thing.

### Post by blackrim on 2006-11-09
i am not trying to start any wars here. just honestly curious if i am missing something. 
let me begin.
i started using ubuntu little over a year ago now. i enjoyed it much more than my previous linux distros (RedHat -> Debian -> Fedora -> Debian -> Ubuntu, in that order over the last 10 years). i liked ubuntu because of the repos and the forums (of course), and the one cd installation (instead of a dvd or 5 cds). 
then i gave xubuntu a try to see if memory management was better because i am a developer and i love my memory. didn't really make much of a difference for my machine. then i tried kubuntu and immediately saw better cpu and memory usage (once i turned off some of the ridiculous eye-candy like the bouncing cursor and the panel popups, etc.). 
now i have been using kubuntu for about 6 months (on a laptop, two desktops and a disk server) and couldn't be happier. the options are exponentially greater with kde than with gnome. 
then i check distrowatch and these forums and i still see the vast majority with ubuntu and i am wondering why?
is it just age old misconceptions about kde and licensing? speed? memory? i say this cause i definitely use to feel that way until recently. and the defaults on kubuntu (kde?) are pretty absurd. but the options are great. if you have a fast video card and some memory, you can turn on transparency and all the eye-candy. if you have a slow machine you can turn it all off. cant wait for kde 4.

just wondering why kubuntu isn't gaining more ground and whether i am missing something great about ubuntu.

---

### Post by maddog39 on 2006-11-09
I wanted to try Kubuntu after I had installed Xubuntu 6.10 and the beta software had too many bugs so I install it and this is one of the first times ive used KDE, atleast Ubuntu based. I have used a few other KDE based distros in the past. Well anyway, I wasnt impressed at all. The software installer program was horrid compared to the one for GNOME/XFCE and I was really disapointed mostly by that. I also found it was alot harder to use and just not as comfortable of an enviornment to work in. However I was impressed with its really flashy interface and nice menus. Another thing which made me sort of gag, was, in Konquerer, when I loaded a page it would lag like hell like a game with no hardware acceleration, like I couldnt scroll pages smoothly at all. This wasnt on the LiveCD either, this was after I had physically installed it on my HD. So for now I prefer Ubuntu and use it 99% of the time over windoze of course. if only my printer worked I could free up 60 gigs of wasted ntfs **** filesystem. rofl.

[Edit]
Overall my favorite would be Xubuntu. Waiting for 6.10.1.

### Post by vayu on 2006-11-10
I have all three on three computers.  I bounce back and forth a bit but overall KDE rocks for me.  Back in Hoary days I was using XFCE exclusively and loving it, but KDE is so rich.  I just like the way it works, it does more.  I like the visual style of Gnome in some ways, but it tries to hide too much, reminds me of the Mac which is beautiful and I use it for some things but please empower me don't limit me.  I do mostly use Gnome on my laptop though.  It doesn't have far to go to be usable to me, but it keeps heading farther in the wrong direction.  Plus I hate waiting for folders to open.  On the same machine, Windows opens every directory instantly, KDE has an ever so slight pause and Gnome I can make a sandwich while waiting. (ok not a sandwich but it just doesn't have a responsive direct connect feel)

### Post by GeneralZod on 2006-11-10
Kubuntu for me :) I love GNOME's simple and clean looks, but KDE just gels with me in every possible way, right down to "behind the scenes" stuff like the language it's written in, the organisation and power of the libraries, and the (lack of!) politics in the KDE community to the functionality of the desktop itself.  

I've never really understood the "clutter" argument - the desktop itself can be made as uncluttered as you like, so I always assumed people meant the KDE apps themselves.  But then, people always seem happy to use these apps, so I'm :confused:

I can see why a newcomer would take one look at KControl and turn tail and run, though ;)

---

### Post by mdsmedia on 2006-11-10
> **GeneralZod said:**
> Kubuntu for me :) I love GNOME's simple and clean looks, but KDE just gels with me in every possible way, right down to "behind the scenes" stuff like the language it's written in, the organisation and power of the libraries, and the (lack of!) politics in the KDE community to the functionality of the desktop itself.  

I've never really understood the "clutter" argument - the desktop itself can be made as uncluttered as you like, so I always assumed people meant the KDE apps themselves.  But then, people always seem happy to use these apps, so I'm :confused:

I can see why a newcomer would take one look at KControl and turn tail and run, though ;)I'm not sure if you're referring to my comment that it felt cluttered.

I'm not complaining that KDE's desktop is cluttered. It just "feels cluttered". It's just the feeling I get in KDE. I'm not even sure that that is the problem I have with it. I don't have a problem with KDE really. I just feel more comfortable with Gnome.

That said, using Kontact regularly, the help documentation stinks. It all seems to be linked to the KDE project. Often I'll click on a help link and find nothing there.

I've often considered using KDE by default (as it's already installed and I really don't have to do anything other than select it) but every time I go into KDE I just feel uncomfortable compared to Gnome. Not nearly as uncomfortable as I feel in Windows. I just prefer Gnome.

### Post by oobuntoo on 2006-11-10
Everyone has their preference and I can understand why many new linux users prefer Ubuntu. The default setup looks much more pleasing and can be changed with minimal effort, but not as configurable as Kubuntu. You cannot easily change gnome color scheme and keep the same theme. KDE, on the other hand, gives you much more options to tweak the look and feel if you know where to look.

I used Ubuntu for the last two months but have to switched back to Kubuntu because I don't like the way KDE applications work under Gnome. I have to have my KDE applications (Amarok, K3B, etc,..) and they have to work the way they work under KDE.

One thing about KDE-based distro that I dislike is that they all have horrible default look, Kubuntu Edgy included. I know that look can be changed, but if they want to attract linux noobs to KDE-based distro, these people need to make the default look much more pleasing than it is now. After all, first impression is important. Call me shallow, but I don't think any guy would want to start a conversation with an ugly girl he doesn't know :mrgreen:

---

I'm really waiting to see KDE 4 :D :D

### Post by aysiu on 2006-11-10
> **fuscia said:**
> aside from cosmetics, which configurations do you find in kde that are not in gnome (a few examples will do)?
Well, just found one today: *kwebdesktop*.

Someone posted [a thread on trying to get Google Calendar on the desktop. Ended up resorting to doing screenshots of Google Calendar after every change and then setting those as the Gnome wallpaper.](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=297027)

In KDE, you can just have *kwebdesktop* handle the wallpaper and make Google Calendar your desktop.

Probably isn't that useful a feature for most people, but it's useful to at least one--doesn't mean KDE is "better" than Gnome--but it **is** something KDE can do that Gnome can't.

### Post by Bizmac on 2006-11-10
I played with Ubuntu and was not so impressed.
Then I switched to Kubuntu and I cannot get enough (KDE 4...)

I am using a lot of KDE apps (Ksnapshot, Amarok, Kontact...), they are really helpful and combined a lot of features.

Some can complaints about Konqueror, it is very complex, a lot of features but it is really nice being able to do a lot of things with it (FTP, web, files transfer). If I was able to save my last session (like in Firefox), I won't need Firefox anymore.

I don't have an independent graphic card just an Intel, but it is sufficient to use Beryl and AIGLX. Cube effect is working, transparency, blurs, expose Mac style = unbelievable under my small configuration.

I never played with windows again, yes maybe sometime to check if when I am sending a ppt presentation to my boss or a xls file there are no mis-interpretation with OpenOffice...

Love edgy, love Kubuntu! I am now out of the evil Microsoft...;)
The only big thing still missing for me is a WebMeeting app, very useful if you are in sales...

### Post by maniacmusician on 2006-11-10
I voted none because techinically, I don't use Kubuntu. However, I do use KDE.

I prefer KDE over Gnome because I like my options, and lots of em. It also just seems to fit my style better.

### Post by fuscia on 2006-11-10

### Post by ComplexNumber on 2006-11-10
> **fuscia said:**
> 

i, personally, find that kde lacks a hierarchy of choices. almost all the choices one can make in kde are treated with equal billing. and, i have often found some of the more essential choices one can make to be more hidden than some of the more cosmetic.
i think i would agree with that. i'm currently using kde 3.5.5 on pclinuxos at the moment and i have been doing for the previous week. this is only until the fedora core 6 cover disk is released on Linux Format issue 88. then its back to gnome(ie home) for me. i thought i would install lots of different distros, but have stuck with pclinuxos because i like the mandriva control centre.
i really don't think there is any sense of design in kde. everything is all about configuration ability, but its all thrown together with little sense of design and structure. so, as you correctly say, some of the more essential configuration options are more hidden than the more cosmetic. gnome, on the other hand, has intelligent design and structure. i also realised many years ago(things are still the same in kde now as they were years ago) that the additional configuration options in kde effectively offers no real advantage. 
i'm glad to report that konqueror has only crashed 3 times in a week, but kpov modeler crashes the X server every time its run (ie it boots me back to the login screen). other apps have crashed too. some of them just don't play nicely with the particular theme running at the time - for example, when i apply bold formatting in an application called knowit when i'm using klearlooks theme, it crashes the application. when i change it to qtcurve and do the same, it doesn't. weird or what? :confused:. 
i hope kde 4 solves some of the numerous problems, but i think it may be many years before that happens.

### Post by NyquistLimit on 2006-11-11
I recently switched from Ubuntu to Kubuntu and KDE seems much more mature and usable to me compared to Gnome.

The problems / inconsistent user interface bugs I had with Gnome were:

-Dropdown menu bug when moving mouse while clicking dropdown menu (the menuitem under the mouse would instantly get selected)

-Inability to set a global mouse wheel speed (KDE has this in the mouse configuration options)

-More stable / coherent panel. In Gnome I found that the icons in the tray would move around when certain applications closed. Or they would leave gaps between icons in the tray.

-In edgy when moving the bottom window list to the top of the screen (so that the main panel is above the window list panel but they are both at the top of the screen....when you log out and log back in the panels have switched places.

-Dragging a file from file roller to Nautilus is completely broken because you have to hold down the mouse button while waiting for file roller to decompress the archive.

Anyway KDE has been working out well for me. I was talking to a friend recently and he said that KDE has been in development much longer than Gnome so it's understandable that it's more mature. That and something about Gnome developers being on crack? :)

Other advantages are...the memory footprint of KDE is smaller than Gnome for me with more apps running at the same time. Super karamba works "out of the box". The ability to remember window positions and sizes. Much better dual monitor support (ability to set different wallpapers and stuff). I could list more but I have to go and cook dinner now :)

### Post by Charles Hand on 2006-11-24
I prefer KDE. Gnome seems dumbed-down to me. KDE has a richer set of apps/features (of course, I can run all those apps under Gnome, too). I don't see the "eye candy" thing. I don't perceive KDE to be any more or less eye candy than gnome. Maybe I just configured KDE the way I liked it so fast that I missed the eye-candy-ness. I put a panel at the top of the screen because I've come to like that. Under the hood, KDE configuration files are logically organized and the XML tags make sense. I have been really annoyed at Gnome's obfuscation of configuration - the "registry" concept. Gnome seems anemic to me.

How about this: if you like your appliances to have lots of buttons and knobs, you'll like KDE. If you like your appliances to have one button and one knob, you'll like Gnome.

### Post by TeraDyne on 2006-11-24
Personally, I perfer KDE and Kubuntu. KDE, IMHO, just seems to be easier for me to use and more stable for me than GNOME. It's also much more appealing to me because fo how easy it is to change the look of KDE, and all the options that I can change for it.

### Post by shin on 2006-11-26
To be honest - I like KDE better.

I started with KDE long time ago. Then moved to GNOME. Gnome is great. Really great. Can't say anything bad about it. Still I just dont like the look and feel of Gtk. Tried xfce. Didnt like it. Moved back to KDE.

Right now it's more about eyecandies and level of customization that KDE offers me. That's just it. I hate KMail, KOffice, Konqueror (Nautilus suits me better in fact) and stuff. I rather use Gnomish (running on gtk) Thunderbird and Firefox. Yet I love KDevelop. There's no other IDE that good. 

Anyway, I also hate SUSE (which is in my opinion the worst distro ever with extremely xpish and idiot-proof solutions) and I find default Kubuntu KDE environment not as bad as you did. Actually there is update manager, which I dont like.

As you can see I got completely different opinions from yours on some topics. You cant also say that I'm inexperienced. I tried lots of distros and spend a lot of time on Debian Sid, Fedora and Ubuntu. When it comes to programming, I know C++ like the back of my hand (oh another reason for not liking Gnome - they mostly prefer C - yuck!). But to be honest all of these doesnt matter at all.

It is all about 'like' and 'not like'. This is why I doubt that Gnome or KDE will ever definitely "win" unless development will be stopped.

UPDATE

Oh and about all those companies preferring Gnome - maybe it's just because of the license. Gnome's GTK is AFAIK GPL. There is a port for Windows (not very good one). C# versions (also ported - again not that successfully but at least there is something). And for KDE? We got QT from some Trolltech company, confusing license, fully commercial for Windows for very long time (now free for non-commercial products). I find QT very well designed but the fact that it is commercial product for Windows is disturbing for me. 

KDE developers seems to ignore most of this. For example - extremely popular cross platform wxWidgets framework has a wxGTK port but noone created wxQT port. That's just sad.

---

### Post by Bloodfen Razormaw on 2006-11-26
SuSE is still primarily KDE.  It just makes GNOME more accessible now.  In fact, GNOME has been largely losing in the field of desktops.  Ubuntu and Red Hat are its only major Linux supporters.  Almost all other major distros are KDE oriented or neutral, and KDE tends to be the de facto preferred desktop in neutral environments.  KDE not only is more common in the West but is absolutely dominant in the East.  KDE is also far more preferred than GNOME by governments, and the only desktop to break out into the conventional retail market.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2006-11-26
People have their preferences... even developers. Sometimes they will prefer KDE, other times Gnome.

I don't know if it really matters. You don't have to use the default desktop environment on a distribution. Luckily for you, Ubuntu has both Gnome and KDE, and it has KDE in a number of different varieties--*kdebase, kde-core, kubuntu-desktop*, and *kde*.

P.S. I've merged this with the larger KDE v. Gnome thread because it seems to just be heading down that same road...

### Post by bailout on 2006-11-27
> **darkhatter said:**
> I don't want to compare or anything I just want to know why most companies are ignoring KDE, more like not giving it the same "love" that Gnome gets

I think the reason there has been a big shift to gnome is mainly due to ubuntu being gnome based and grabbing such a large part of the desktop market and also that the big distros aim at the commercial market. Ubuntu has caused a big fuss and has brought a lot of new people into linux. It is primarily a gnome distro and any newbie is going to install ubuntu rather than kubuntu and hence equates linux with gnome.

Gnome is ideal for corporate desktops and I think has recieved quite a bit of funding by corporate developers. A desktop for a corporate environment needs to be simple, standadised and users aren't allowed to change it anyway. Redhat has always pushed gnome as it is aimed at the corporate world. Suse is now heavily commercial and is targetting the same market so gnome makes sense.

I think KDE has been developed for and appeals most to the enthusiast user who will want to have more control over their system. Unfortunately for KDE ubuntu which has really come to dominate the home user sector, especcially new users, chose to be exclusively gnome and kde was only added as a community project and is still as you put it 'unloved'.

I saw a poll a couple of years ago on the Opera forums, which are distro neutral, which showed a large majority, 70+%, used KDE. I suspect if that poll was done today the figures would be reversed and most linux users would be using gnome. I don't mind gnome as a desktop, although I prefer KDE, but I do find most KDE apps to be much better than gnome equivelants. I suspect this will probably change though as gnome is now dominant.

---

### Post by Lucifer The Dark on 2006-11-27
I tried Gnome, KDE & XFCE before I decided which to use, KDE had far too many options when looking for something specific, it's easy to get lost.

XFCE was the exact opposite of KDE with no options at all. So Gnome was the clear winner being right in-between them both with a perfect balance of options & empty space on screen. Plus to me it's the most like Windows Explorer.

### Post by cjnkns on 2006-12-02

Personally I have tried both GNOME and KDE; so far I much prefer KDE. Don't get me wrong GNOME is awesome to it works great. In fact when I first started using KDE ummmm not sure which version it was, but I kept getting crashes when closing our opening windows. I have been using it lately with no problems what so ever.

(again my lack of exp), but that's why people develop such pretty gui's right so we can use them....

### Post by shining on 2006-12-02
> **deanlinkous said:**
> KDE control center : designed by a hundred crazed monkeys and used only at your peril. :D

(it s joke people)

lol
Well, proposing more things to configure also means it's more complicated to organize them. Hopefully, that will improve in kde4.

I like how kde is more configurable, but I already ran into several things I couldn't configure, in konqueror and other kde apps.
I also ran into more bugs using kde. Same for my dad, I don't remember him reporting bugs to me when he was using gnome. I switched his box to kde a while ago, and he found a couple of bugs in konqueror (file manager mode). These are minor bugs which were reported ages ago on bugs.kde.org , and it seems like they are never going to be fixed.
But anyway, there is a big list of more important bugs, like crash, in both konqueror and nautilus, and these should probably be fixed first:
[konqueror bugs]("http://bugs.kde.org/buglist.cgi?product=konqueror&component=general&bug_status=UNCONFIRMED&bug_status=NEW&bug_status=ASSIGNED&bug_status=REOPENED&order=bugs.bug_severity,bugs.votes")
[nautilus bugs]("http://bugzilla.gnome.org/buglist.cgi?product=nautilus&bug_status=NEW&bug_status=REOPENED&bug_status=ASSIGNED&bug_status=UNCONFIRMED&remaction=&query_format=advanced&order=bugs.bug_severity,bugs.bug_status%2C%20bugs.priority%2C%20map_assigned_to.login_name%2C%20bugs.bug_id&query_based_on=")

So I just think kde and gnome are both very promising desktops, but they both still have a long way to go.

### Post by scorpion-kde on 2006-12-06
I started out with KDE, but I recently installed Ubuntu on one of my machines, and I think I see what's keeping Kubuntu back.  I like the choices presented by KDE, but Ubuntu just seems more polished, and I would feel more comfortable giving Ubuntu to a new user than Kubuntu.  It seems to have received more attention in development, which I suppose isn't terribly surprising, but does it have to be that way?  For example, Adept is in dire need of attention.  Synaptic gives the user a dialog box for things like the Java License.  When I installed Flash 7 in Adept, it just sat there until I opened the built-in terminal and responded to the prompt.  Hopefully the situation will change with KDE 4 and Feisty+1.

### Post by RAV TUX on 2006-12-16
like some here I started originally with Linux with Ubuntu and the Gnome desktop.....I have found many distros that do beautiful, speedy and functional XFCE desktops (most notably: Dreamlinux) and some very inspiring Fluxbox desktops (most notably: dyne:bolic)

But I have come to prefer KDE....I guess this started when I had KNOPPIX INSTALLED for about 6 months, even though the DVD install comes with the option to install a Gnome desktop (and honestly I feel KNOPPIX does the best Gnome Desktop)

I am currently using on my primary computer Sabayon Linux and again while both the Gnome and XFCE (and others) desktops can easily be selected upon booting the OS...I prefer KDE specifically.

I have comme to prefer Ksnapshot, Kopete, Konsole, K3B, Kword, Ksudoku, and a host of other KDE applications that simply work seamlessly under KDE.

I honestly loved Gnome and still think that Gnome and all other desktops are awesome. This is not a KDE vs anybody post...

just a simple reflection on the evolution of my preferences.....just wondering if anybody else had made the transition if purely by happenstance?

### Post by patrick295767 on 2006-12-16
> **fuscia said:**
> kde on my laptop is faster than openbox

Sounds like weird a bit... 
 
you might check your MEM with torsmo while using your pc :

my /home/user/.torsmorc
```
background no
  font -xos4-terminus-medium-r-normal--12-120-72-72-c-60-iso8859-1
  use_xft no
  xftalpha 0.8
  update_interval 7.0
  own_window no
  double_buffer yes
  minimum_size 65 5
  draw_shades yes
  draw_outline no
  draw_borders yes
  stippled_borders 0
  border_margin 0
  border_width 0
  default_color green
  default_shade_color black
  default_outline_color black
  alignment bottom_left
  
  gap_x 12
  gap_y 12
  
  no_buffers yes
  uppercase no
  




# Possible variables to be used:
#
#      Variable         Arguments                  Description                
#  acpiacadapter                     ACPI ac adapter state.                   
#  acpifan                           ACPI fan state                           
#  acpitemp                          ACPI temperature.                        
#  adt746xcpu                        CPU temperature from therm_adt746x       
#  adt746xfan                        Fan speed from therm_adt746x             
#  battery           (num)           Remaining capasity in ACPI or APM        
#                                    battery. ACPI battery number can be      
#                                    given as argument (default is BAT0).     
#  buffers                           Amount of memory buffered                
#  cached                            Amount of memory cached                  
#  color             (color)         Change drawing color to color            
#  cpu                               CPU usage in percents                    
#  cpubar            (height)        Bar that shows CPU usage, height is      
#                                    bar's height in pixels                   
#  downspeed         net             Download speed in kilobytes              
#  downspeedf        net             Download speed in kilobytes with one     
#                                    decimal                                  
#  exec              shell command   Executes a shell command and displays    
#                                    the output in torsmo. warning: this      
#                                    takes a lot more resources than other    
#                                    variables. I'd recommend coding wanted   
#                                    behaviour in C and posting a patch :-).  
#  execi             interval, shell Same as exec but with specific interval. 
#                    command         Interval can't be less than              
#                                    update_interval in configuration.        
#  fs_bar            (height), (fs)  Bar that shows how much space is used on 
#                                    a file system. height is the height in   
#                                    pixels. fs is any file on that file      
#                                    system.                                  
#  fs_free           (fs)            Free space on a file system available    
#                                    for users.                               
#  fs_free_perc      (fs)            Free percentage of space on a file       
#                                    system available for users.              
#  fs_size           (fs)            File system size                         
#  fs_used           (fs)            File system used space                   
#  hr                (height)        Horizontal line, height is the height in 
#                                    pixels                                   
#  i2c               (dev), type, n  I2C sensor from sysfs (Linux 2.6). dev   
#                                    may be omitted if you have only one I2C  
#                                    device. type is either in (or vol)       
#                                    meaning voltage, fan meaning fan or temp 
#                                    meaning temperature. n is number of the  
#                                    sensor. See /sys/bus/i2c/devices/ on     
#                                    your local computer.                     
#  kernel                            Kernel version                           
#  loadavg           (1), (2), (3)   System load average, 1 is for past 1     
#                                    minute, 2 for past 5 minutes and 3 for   
#                                    past 15 minutes.                         
#  machine                           Machine, i686 for example                
#  mails                             Mail count in mail spool. You can use    
#                                    program like fetchmail to get mails from 
#                                    some server using your favourite         
#                                    protocol. See also new_mails.            
#  mem                               Amount of memory in use                  
#  membar            (height)        Bar that shows amount of memory in use   
#  memmax                            Total amount of memory                   
#  memperc                           Percentage of memory in use              
#  new_mails                         Unread mail count in mail spool.         
#  nodename                          Hostname                                 
#  outlinecolor      (color)         Change outline color                     
#  pre_exec          shell command   Executes a shell command one time before 
#                                    torsmo displays anything and puts output 
#                                    as text.                                 
#  processes                         Total processes (sleeping and running)   
#  running_processes                 Running processes (not sleeping),        
#                                    requires Linux 2.6                       
#  shadecolor        (color)         Change shading color                     
#  stippled_hr       (space),        Stippled (dashed) horizontal line        
#                    (height)        
#  swapbar           (height)        Bar that shows amount of swap in use     
#  swap                              Amount of swap in use                    
#  swapmax                           Total amount of swap                     
#  swapperc                          Percentage of swap in use                
#  sysname                           System name, Linux for example           
#  time              (format)        Local time, see man strftime to get more 
#                                    information about format                 
#  totaldown         net             Total download, overflows at 4 GB on     
#                                    Linux with 32-bit arch and there doesn't 
#                                    seem to be a way to know how many times  
#                                    it has already done that before torsmo   
#                                    has started.                             
#  totalup           net             Total upload, this one too, may overflow 
#  updates                           Number of updates (for debugging)        
#  upspeed           net             Upload speed in kilobytes                
#  upspeedf          net             Upload speed in kilobytes with one       
#                                    decimal                                  
#  uptime                            Uptime                                   
#  uptime_short                      Uptime in a shorter format               
#
#  seti_prog                         Seti@home current progress
#  seti_progbar      (height)        Seti@home current progress bar
#  seti_credit                       Seti@hoome total user credit


# variable is given either in format $variable or in ${variable}. Latter
# allows characters right after the variable and must be used in network
# stuff because of an argument

# stuff after 'TEXT' will be formatted on screen

 $sysname $kernel -${color gray} $time - ${color gray}Uptime$color $uptime - ${color gray}RAM:$color $mem/$memmax - $memperc%${color gray}Swap:$color $swap/$swapmax - $swapperc% $ - ${color gray}CPU :$color $cpu% $ - ${color gray}Processes:$color $processes - ${color gray}NetUp:$color ${upspeed eth0} k/s${color gray} - Down:$color ${downspeed eth0} k/s



  TEXT
  
  $color$stippled_hr
  ${color green} Uptime:$color  $uptime
  $color$stippled_hr
  ${color green} File systems:${color green}
  
   /hda1 ->${color green}[  ${fs_free /} ]
   /home ->${color green}[  ${fs_free /home} ]
   ${color green}${fs_used /home}  ${fs_bar /home} ]
   
  $color$stippled_hr
  ${color green}Down:$color ${downspeed eth0} k/s - ${color green}NetUp:$color ${upspeed eth0} k/s
  ${color green}RAM:$color $mem/$memmax ${color green}CPU:$color $cpu
 
  $color$stippled_hr
  ${color green} Cpu  Mem   PID Procs${color green}
  ${execi 10 ps gaxo %cpu,%mem,pid,comm | sort +0nr | head -n 7 | sed -e 's/-bin//' | sed -e 's/-media-play//'}
  $color$stippled_hr
  ${color green} Battery:${battery 1}
  $color$stippled_hr
  ${color green} Time: ${color white}${time %H:%M} ${color green}${time %D}
 
  ${color green} ${execi 3600 cal}
  $color$stippled_hr


```

### Post by Erunno on 2006-12-17
ComplexNumber is probably talking about excessive memory consumption if you run Gnome and KDE applications at the same time. Gnome and KDE applications are dependent on certain libraries during runtime which are provided by the desktop environment. Ergo, the runtime dependencies from both desktops have to be loaded into memory if you use applications from both DE worlds. If you only use the applications from your native DE you'll have both a better memory footprint and better startup times for programs as the relevant libraries are already loaded. KDE excels in that discipline as Konqueror and KOffice are native parts of KDE contrary to Firefox and OpenOffice.

On a side note, I recently switched back to Ubuntu as I think Kubuntu is the worst KDE distribution I've ever encountered. I came back from Gentoo before that and Kubuntu's KDE implementation feels a lot slower. Right now I don't have time to try out new distributions so Gnome will have to do for a couple of weeks but than I'll probably try out OpenSuse or Mepis which (judging from anecdotal evidence) have a much more refined KDE implementation. Plus, Kubuntu looks even worse than the standard KDE desktop which is quite a feat if you ask me :p

### Post by helloyo on 2006-12-30
I would just like to start off with some background. I have been a GNOME user for a long time now, but every so often would give KDE another chance. Without fail i would return to GNOME. Usually due to a bloated, clunky feeling, or the strange arrangements of settings.

I did continue trying KDE every so often though and this last time it has stuck. Sure it has its faults, but it has two features that linux users appreciate: excitement and power. GNOME is a fantastic desktop, and what i would install for most end users, but its boring. Very little configuration or customization, and simplified software. GNOME is like a European small car, efficient, practical and problem free. KDE is like a custom restored muscle car. Its little quibbles are more then made up for in brute power and enjoyment.

Some applications and why I have been loving them in KDE:
Amarok: No explanation needed here...
Kopete: Fantastic look. Features not available in Gaim.
Kontact: Not as clean as Evolution, but undoubtably more powerful. 
Kaffeine: Great media player, handy options include tray dock, pause on minimize, and like almost all KDE apps, completely customizable shortcuts.
KMyMoney: I thought i'd miss GnuCash, and miss some features, but this is a very worth replacement.
Konqueror: I thought it was clunky and bloated at first (well, still do), but its very powerful, and has some great features like file size view.
Adept: New favourite apt package manager.

So here is my challenge to you: install kubuntu-desktop and use it for 2 weeks. Go through every option and customize it, try stuff you never have before, and play with all the features. If you stick through the initial impression I think you will be surprised. It took me literally half a dozen attempts before I switched back to KDE.

---

### Post by kvonb on 2006-12-30
I just tried K again after a year of not using it.  I forced myself to stick at it and had a lot of fun with "kbfx", it is very pretty but ultimately not very practical.  I found that it is very customisable, and turned out better looking than Gnome in some things.  The main thing I like over Gnome is having the ability to save window size and position, although once I loaded Beryl, that went out of the, well, window I guess :rolleyes:.  Also on shutdown/logout it too a long time with Beryl running and gave really bad artefacts all over the screen.  The other thing I didn't like is the main menu, only because it isn't organised as simply as Gnome's, it is logical, but not aesthetic making it hard to find things quickly.  Have a look through my screenshots (link in my sig) at what I achieved if it interests anybody.  I will definitely be trying the next release, although I don't think K has come very far since 1998 in overall looks and design, and as soon as I see that crusty old blue mixer icon it makes me sigh!

### Post by fistfullofroses on 2007-01-04

I dislike KDE, because when I first tried it it crashed quite often. Since the days when I first started using Linux KDE has gotten much better but I cannot rid myself of that old stigma. One more reason I liked the minimal window managers was speed. It was just faster to type the name of a program in a terminal and have it launch. The disadvantage was that to work with wireless networks or any other thing that one would find in the System section of the GNOME menu, I had to do a **** load of work in the terminal because those applications' names were unknown to me, and still are. Thus it was too hard to pull them up and I had to manually edit config files. Not hard. But tedious. Annoying.

### Post by maddog39 on 2007-01-08
Hello all,

Well I just made the brave switch from the safety of GNOME to KDE and Kubuntu. I have encountered a lot of problems with KDE in the past and was afraid I wouldn't be able to do it this time because something would get messed up. So instead of Edgy I decided to go with 6.06.1 since I hear from a lot of people that it's more stable.So anyway, overall I think that the interface is kind of nice and all but I'm always shuffling through the menu looking for programs that I know are there but can't seem to find.

Onto my problems with it. Well, I've just stated one slight annoyance although the menu thing is just a matter of getting used to the new interface and therefore isnt really a problem. However, KDE continues to get to a point with me where the annoyances get over bearing and I just cant take it anymore. So back to problems I've had with KDE. The first thing I'd like to add would be internet/wireless connections. I dont understand why every time I start up my computer I have to manually open the Wifi manager and select and open the connectio. In GNOME, it will automatically establish that connection at startup, what it with KDE and making more work for the end user.

Okay enough of that rant, now for my other problem. When I close the lid on the computer, nothing happens and there is no option or KDE just simply cant, turn off the screen when I do that. I understand that most laptops have alot of issues suspending and sleeping and what not but it could at least turn off the screen. In GNOME, when  you close the lid it turns off the screen to save power, KDE doesnt do anything, and I've been looking through options to fix that for ages. The only thing I found to do was to lock the screen before hand so it;s at least a black screen, but it still will not physcially turn off the screen. So now I have to completely turn off my computer every time I walk away for 15 minutes and then start it up again when I come back.

Well thats it for big (and what I would consider "unexcusable" problems) and time for some small things that I dont really understand/dont like/waste my time/bug the crap outa you. Okay, so Konqueror. I really like the browser as a whole and was pretty impressed with it in the past (I used to use MEPIS for a little while when I wasn't really too equanted with linux yet). However, even though Konqueror was extremely fast on my Dual Core 3.0GHz desktop, on my 1GHz laptop it lags and crashes all the time and will refuse to load pages sometimes and is normally very very slow. My belief is that this has to do with a definciency in the renderer and so I have to use the much more efficient (in this case) firefox. But on my laptop Konqueror just acts like Internet Explorer with tabs and thats it (this is inposing a bad thing) cuz IE is just so slow. Although on my dual core machine it seems to be faster than firefox.

Other than that, in the settings thing, whenever there is something that requires administrator authentication, it makes you wait to load the form itself, but it disables all the tools and you have to physically click the "Administrator Mode..." button and then authenticate yourself and ThEn wait for the form to reload itself. This is a big waste of time when in GNOME you just enter you password at the time that the form loads or if there are some functions in the settings panel that dont require admin auth. and you want to let non-admins. use them, fine, then let people ignore the admin authentication form and only be allowed to edit settings that dont require admin authentication.

I've also encountered this VERY BIG problem when making the switch to Kubuntu. I noticed that the first time I tried compiling a program, it wasn't able to recognize my system type, and this previously was not a problem in GNOME. So now all source packages fail to compile. I posted another topic with all the details on this problem in the General Help section but no one helped me really.

Well thats my rant, I think everything I stated was very well explained and everything. My overall opinion is, since that most of my problems were laptop related, that KDE just isnt ready for the mobile device/computer yet and I think that it's very clear. If anyone has solutions to these problems, feel free to share.

-maddog39

---

### Post by ComplexNumber on 2007-01-08
> So anyway, overall I think that the interface is kind of nice and all but I'm always shuffling through the menu looking for programs that I know are there but can't seem to find.install katapult. its kinda nice when one loses their compass  when navigating through miles of rural undergrowth and false leads within the depths of the kde menu.


come on, lad, back to gnome. leave kde for the tinkerers. its obviously not for you....at least not on a laptop. kde has some fantastic features such as katapult, yakuake, kontact, kparts, kioslaves, ultimate control over configuration, etc. from a superficial point of view, they are REALLY tempting.......but unless you feel comfortable with the desktop as a whole, then those tempting features are nowt but mirages that only look good in one's imagination, effectively. go with the desktop that you feel comfortable with, because at the end of the day, thats the only thing that matters.

### Post by Terracotta on 2007-01-08
Might I add that the laptop suspending stuff has been added in Edgy for Kubuntu?
The annoyance with KDE sometimes (though in Edgy I encountered this problem less), is that some programs don't show up in the menus until a complete reboot.
I once succeeded in having Kubuntu connecting to a wireless automatically, but next time I reinstalled it it didn't work like that anymore, sad but well, I suppose adding Knetworkmanager to fiesty will make this problem solved as well. Don't know what you experience with Konqueror, I have it installed on my laptop and it works fine in edgy, another solution to for konqueror might be Opera, it works nicely together with KDE (QT).

When it comes to the systemsettings authentication, I think I prefer  the way it is now: some modules are loaded that don't need authentication, and to access others one needs to get permission. Two modules can be handled on one page while one needs it authentication the other doesnt. There's no need to ask automatically for authentication.

Well, that's my little rant ;-).
Perhaps Fiesty+1 with kde4 might be the one for you, if you insist on trying something you don't like ;-).

### Post by seijuro on 2007-01-08
> **Terracotta said:**
> 
The annoyance with KDE sometimes (though in Edgy I encountered this problem less), is that some programs don't show up in the menus until a complete reboot.

I've seen this issue I'm not sure why this problem persists but there are two methods to fix it with out a full reboot. First you can simply go to logout and end the current session and log right back in. Second you can open the K menu editor and simply click save the entry is there already for some reason the Kmenu didn't refresh but the save from the editor seems to force a refresh. I duely agree this could be a source of some irritation and should not have been a problem to begin with.

### Post by groggyboy on 2007-01-08
> 
So anyway, overall I think that the interface is kind of nice and all but I'm always shuffling through the menu looking for programs that I know are there but can't seem to find.
Complex-Number suggested Katapult, which is neat.  Another option to consider is [Kickoff]("http://www.kde-look.org/content/show.php?content=50240") (maybe it actually isn't much easier, but then again maybe it is...).  It is used by default in SUSE10.2, but the link I posted is for a Kubuntu port - think of it as SUSE's KDE equivalent of SLAB.

> When I close the lid on the computer, nothing happens and there is no option or KDE just simply cant, turn off the screen when I do that.
Correct me if I'm wrong, but I believe Kubuntu Dapper uses Kpowersave to manage power on laptops.  You could try Kubuntu Edgy, which uses a newer package called kde-power-guidance-powermanager.  It works well for me.  Unfortunately, it is rather bare-bones at the moment.  Another option to try is Klaptop.  Search the forums for more info on them.> I don't understand why every time I start up my computer I have to manually open the Wifi manager and select and open the connection.
Have you looked into network manager?  Make sure, if you do, that you also install the KDE frontend (called "knetworkmanager", you can find it in the repos).  Its a powerful little app that can handle WPA connections and hidden SSID's, and automatically defaults to a wired connection if one is available.  I think its an essential laptop program, for GNOME *or* for KDE.

> I noticed that the first time I tried compiling a program, it wasn't able to recognize my system type, and this previously was not a problem in GNOME. So now all source packages fail to compile.
I'm not sure I understand your problem.  Could you explain what you mean by "not recognizing system type", or link to the other thread you mention?


As for your beef with Konqueror, I'd have to say that I'm not a huge fan of it myself.  I do like how it is an integrated file and web browser (Windows/Internet Explorer, anyone?), but I guess I'm just too much of a Firefox fanboy.


Well, all that said, I'm pleased to hear you are giving KDE a(nother) shot.  I really like it, especially its customization potential.  And with a graphics card that supports composite rendering, Beryl almost becomes redundant!  I can't wait for KDE4, and some of the improvements that will bring (like Phonon - I think the sound system in KDE3 is utter sh*t - trying to get KDE to recognize my external USB speakers has been a nightmare.  GNOME recognized them and set them up the moment I plugged them in).

Keep at it maddog.  Get the kinks out, and give it a try.

### Post by yurimxpxman on 2007-01-08
Personally, I use Gnome. I've never been a big fan of KDE. Although I appreciate the developer's desire to add as many options as possible, I have never liked their implementation. Their menus are messy and swarming with options. Menus are embedded within menus within menus within menus. I think that's what people mean when they say they can't find anything in the K menu. It's just a long, unintuitive list.

Another thing I really hate about KDE is the way it displays the currently running programs in the taskbar. Having multiple rows of programs in small boxes means I have to read each box before I know which one to click. Even Windows is more efficient than that. :roll:

For me at least, it's not so much the way things are labeled but their placement. I do not believe it's something that one can grow accustomed to, but rather learn to endure in trade for the extra configuration options they get in return. Personally, Gnome does enough for me and I love the fact that it's so intuitive and easy to use.

---

### Post by riven0 on 2007-01-09
Well, I've never had exactly the same problem as you guys, but what really gets me is how windows-like KDE looks. I mean, what is up with Kicker's "start" menu and the shiny, toyish looks? I thought I got away from that when I wiped out XP. 
And it seems that no matter how much I customize KDE I just can't get it looking exactly the way I want it. I can barely get half the themes I download installed correctly. No thanks; it's Gnome for me unless KDE4 brings something revolutionary to the table.

### Post by GameManK on 2007-01-09
> **maddog39 said:**
> Hello all,

Well I just made the brave switch from the safety of GNOME to KDE and Kubuntu. I have encountered a lot of problems with KDE in the past and was afraid I wouldn't be able to do it this time because something would get messed up. So instead of Edgy I decided to go with 6.06.1 since I hear from a lot of people that it's more stable.So anyway, overall I think that the interface is kind of nice and all but I'm always shuffling through the menu looking for programs that I know are there but can't seem to find.

Onto my problems with it. Well, I've just stated one slight annoyance although the menu thing is just a matter of getting used to the new interface and therefore isnt really a problem. However, KDE continues to get to a point with me where the annoyances get over bearing and I just cant take it anymore. So back to problems I've had with KDE. The first thing I'd like to add would be internet/wireless connections. I dont understand why every time I start up my computer I have to manually open the Wifi manager and select and open the connectio. In GNOME, it will automatically establish that connection at startup, what it with KDE and making more work for the end user.

Okay enough of that rant, now for my other problem. When I close the lid on the computer, nothing happens and there is no option or KDE just simply cant, turn off the screen when I do that. I understand that most laptops have alot of issues suspending and sleeping and what not but it could at least turn off the screen. In GNOME, when  you close the lid it turns off the screen to save power, KDE doesnt do anything, and I've been looking through options to fix that for ages. The only thing I found to do was to lock the screen before hand so it;s at least a black screen, but it still will not physcially turn off the screen. So now I have to completely turn off my computer every time I walk away for 15 minutes and then start it up again when I come back.
First of all, you should go with Edgy.  It's quite a bit better than dapper.  For one thing, it has guidance-powermanager so laptop stuff works right and very simply.

Feisty has Knetworkmanager which fixes the network issue.  You can also have it automatically connect to wireless by putting the SSID in in the network settings instead of using the wireless lan manager (which is pretty much useless).

I really don't get what the problem is with finding things in the menu though.  How hard is going to Internet for internet apps, Graphics for graphics apps, Office for office apps, etc?  I don't want to offend anybody, but I really don't get what the problem is!  Anyway, from what I've seen, GNOME has the exact same setup except that the menu says "Applications" on it instead of just being a little button which is a complete waste of screen space (of course not nearly as much of a waste of space as the rest of the top panel).  Though one of the times I tried it (hoary) I couldn't even find the Terminal.

> **yurimxpxman said:**
> Personally, I use Gnome. I've never been a big fan of KDE. Although I appreciate the developer's desire to add as many options as possible, I have never liked their implementation. Their menus are messy and swarming with options. Menus are embedded within menus within menus within menus. I think that's what people mean when they say they can't find anything in the K menu. It's just a long, unintuitive list.

Another thing I really hate about KDE is the way it displays the currently running programs in the taskbar. Having multiple rows of programs in small boxes means I have to read each box before I know which one to click. Even Windows is more efficient than that. :roll:

For me at least, it's not so much the way things are labeled but their placement. I do not believe it's something that one can grow accustomed to, but rather learn to endure in trade for the extra configuration options they get in return. Personally, Gnome does enough for me and I love the fact that it's so intuitive and easy to use.
Menus within menus within menus -- this is actually one of my problems with GNOME.  I considered installing it (edgy beta) and when it got to the part where you pick the timezone and region and set the clock I had to click into an extra window just to pick the timezone for no reason.  I was annoyed by the philosophy already but I went on with the install, but had some other problem so I never really got to using it.

As for the multiple rows in the taskbar, that's actually one of my (dozens of) favorite features of KDE.  I can have a dozen programs in a relatively compact area, whereas in windows I have to either make the task bar bigger and uglier to get more space or have tiny squeezed together buttons.  I haven't run that much in GNOME, but it looks like the taskbar is really lacking space, where the heck are the programs supposed to go?


Anyways, it doesn't sound like KDE is for you, and that's why you have choices.  Also the ubuntu developers have done a great job making sure everything works perfectly in GNOME.  If only we had a few more Riddells.

Live and let live.

### Post by calvmari on 2007-01-09
Sorry I'm a bit tired to give proper responses to this thread, but I wanted to give my impression of KDE. I started off a heavy gnome advocate, but I gave KDE an honest try, and it's been wonderful. There's a few kinks I haven't gotten down to working out (like Kopete fails to load links and I wish I could change desktops with ctrl+alt+arrow), but overall KDE feels gorgeous to me.

To me, it feels more friendly than gnome. Little frills like how KDE handles tool tips, and the overall feel appeal greatly to me. This is very vague, and I appologize since it probably doesn't contribute much to the thread, but I feel more "at home" with KDE than gnome.

Oh the one thing I love about KDE, is it detects wireless networks without me having to know their names. That's one thing that bugged me in gnome, when I went to a new location (like sitting in the lobby while my car is fixed or the local cafe shop), I'd ask a random attendant what the SSID was, and they'd just stare blankly at me. Now I can select it from a list, so I'm quite happy :)

Here's a quick image of my desktop that I'm happy with: [http://i24.photobucket.com/albums/c35/calvmari/desktop-1.jpg](http://i24.photobucket.com/albums/c35/calvmari/desktop-1.jpg)

### Post by smarttaz on 2007-01-09
> **ComplexNumber said:**
> Graham Morrison's "30 days with gnome" journal was significantly fairer to gnome than the other guy was to kde. **the gnome guy's 3 days with kde was just a joke**, almost as if he had decided that he wasn't going to like kde at the start. thats not the right way. 
if you're going to use kde in an attempt to like it, don't follow the above persons lead. go in with an open mind. otherwise, the outcome is predictable."Almost as if..."?! "A joke"?! Man, I have stopped using KDE in May 2005! And that was because GNOME served better my needs, and its usability is superior, according to **my** personal standards!

But I wanted to try KDE again, knowing that I disagree with many of its implementation decisions.

Should you have ever bothered to read a more recent post: [http://beranger.org/index.php?article=2215](http://beranger.org/index.php?article=2215) [GNOME-like KDE desktop layout...], you would have noticed that **my main intention is to succeed in getting along with KDE [COLOR=Purple]where needed[/COLOR]**: *«Therefore, no matter my WM/DE of choice is GNOME, and I will keep it so with Debian, CentOS and even Mandriva, I will consider that **Dropline GNOME 2.16.2 is not suitable for Slackware 11, as it breaks its legendary stability and trustworthiness**. [...] As I want to **keep Slackware 11** for a while on my crappy Celeron/850 laptop, it's more reliable to either use an "old-school window manager", or **to use KDE**.»*

I have even tried to build the latest KDE4 snapshot, without fully succeeding: [http://beranger.org/index.php?article=2223](http://beranger.org/index.php?article=2223)

Don't blame me for not being in love with KDE! Graham Morrison is not in love with GNOME, either! (Yet he kept Ubuntu on the laptop; in a similar way, I will continue to use KDE on my laptop, as long as my desktop will remain GNOME.)

The nice thing is that Ubuntu 4.10 helped a lot in my conversion to GNOME, but I am now "free of Ubuntu" (i.e. I am not using it anymore).

Béranger

### Post by NeoChaosX on 2007-01-09
> **calvmari said:**
>  I wish I could change desktops with ctrl+alt+arrow)

You can in KDE. In System Settings > Keyboard and Mouse > Keyboard Shortcuts, just set whatever shortcuts you want to "Switch to Next Desktop" and "Switch to Previous Desktop".

the funniest part of your whole rant was, you're accusing the guy commonly known as "international KDE-killer" (;) i jest, CN) of defending KDE. No, you didn't say it outright, but the implications were obviously there.

and the thing is, I actually agree with ComplexNumber. No one blames you for not being love with KDE, that's not even the issue (ComplexNumber himself despises KDE3, almost with a vengeance). The issue is that **while trying to imitate a fair and objective article that someone else wrote,** you ended up approaching it as a bigoted, opinionated individual who was determined that he wasn't going to like KDE.

Way to go, now my good mood is ruined :evil:

### Post by Erunno on 2007-01-09
> **ComplexNumber said:**
> install katapult. its kinda nice when one loses their compass  when navigating through miles of rural undergrowth and false leads within the depths of the kde menu.

Tsk, tsk, are we spreading FUD again ? The KDE menu in Kubuntu is exactly one layer deep (two, if you count clicking on the kmenu button) which is exactly the same as in Ubuntu's GNOME implementation and they share a similar layout concerning how the applications are organized in the catergories.

Anyway, the original poster should really give Kubuntu 6.10 a try as there has been some nice improvements over its predecessor like improved Laptop support, a new theme (an ugly one imo to be totally honest) and Katapult comes preinstalled which is a pretty intiutive way to start things. Some word of warning, Adept is still a GUI disaster so better get used to using the command line to install programs. I'd still like to now how this ugly disaster of an interface ever passed QA.

Quick question to the public, does anyone know if there is an option to improve Konquerors forward/backward behaviour in web mode ? Compared to Firefox and Opera it's annoyingly slow.

### Post by smarttaz on 2007-01-09
I have been told by a friend that you can switch entirely to the GNOME-style of keyboard shortcuts (not present as an option in the CC):

Small tip:
```
[KDE]
ButtonLayout=1
```Place the two lines above in your **~/.kde/share/config/kdeglobals** file and you'll get the button layout from GNOME.

Have not tried it yet (I'm lazy).

### Post by seijuro on 2007-01-09
> **yurimxpxman said:**
> 
Another thing I really hate about KDE is the way it displays the currently running programs in the taskbar. Having multiple rows of programs in small boxes means I have to read each box before I know which one to click. Even Windows is more efficient than that.

actually the task bar is pretty much the same with Gnome/KDE/Windows the title of the window is displayed as a button in the task bar. The difference is linux allows more configuration of the bar. Second, if you don't like multiple lines on the task bar in kde CHANGE IT, its completely customizable my system is set up more like gnome with top and bottom pannels and only a single row for the task bar. However KDE does offer one more feature that can be annoying or very useful depending how you look at it which is task bar gathering if you have for example 5 terminal windows opened and a dozen other things open rather than makeing everything tiny and unreadable it will compress the 5 terminal windows into one task bar button with 5 options.

### Post by shining on 2007-01-09
> **happy-and-lost said:**
> Does anyone else have serious issues with fonts when switching to KDE? I use Subpixel smoothing on Gnome, and if I try out KDE, log out and log into gnome, the fonts are unreadably fuzzy. I still haven't found a fix for this annoyance.

Just go to kde control center, Appearance -> Fonts, and configure them like they are in gnome.
I believe gnome only uses X resources (see xrdb -q), but kde also writes a ~/.fonts.conf file. It's better to have the same settings in both.

### Post by Zyphrexi on 2007-01-10
Back in the old days I used KDE with redhat and then slack. Now I use Ubuntu and gnome. Really this should be "what window manager do you use and why?". The only thing the different *buntu's have going for them are the default install packages. The thing is, nearly everyone adds stuff to the install later, so i honestly don't understand why there are multiple types.

Right now I have probably about 10 window managers installed, since I like to try new things. And to directly answer this question, KDE seems busy to me, menu structure is an eyesore. It used to be that (imho) KDE was the best desktop environment (KDE ain't a window manager) but now since ubuntu has taken the stage, a lot more attention has been paid to gnome, and more support is going into it.

It really boils down to what you like, gnome has a lot of themes, kde does as well, but there doesn't seem to be much variance as in gnome. So download a few through synaptic, play with them a bit. The great thing about linux is the tweakability.

Oh, one point KDE has over GNOME is dynamic color scheme changing, but there is a project being worked on to add that functionality, it's just not very user friendly yet.

WM's like fluxbox can be great for gaming, since less resources are used, more resources can be put into what really matters... gaming. It could work for work as well, since some WM's can easily distract you with "ooh, I wonder what this does?".

---

### Post by k1001001 on 2007-01-10

### Post by kexodusc on 2007-01-10
I use Ubuntu primarily, but also have separate Kubuntu install on my system.  My wife and relatives prefer Kubuntu big-time, which kind of surprises me.  I'm in the camp that finds it a bit too busy, but thats' because there's so many applications that it can become a bit overwhelming.

Lately I've been giving Kubuntu a workout, just to say I gave it a fair shot.  I've found Ubuntu to be slightly faster on my system (barely), though KDE seems more "polished" and pleasing to the eye, and more familiar (coming from windows).  And maybe a bit more intuitive and easier to learn as a noob in hindsight.  Though the Ubuntu support is probably a bit stronger than Kubuntu's.  

I think, if I was going to make a sales pitch to GNU/Linux newbies who are looking at Vista, I'd probably pick Kubuntu.  I think it would be the easier Destop Manager to migrate to.

Overall, I still prefer Ubuntu, but if Kubuntu could tighten up just a few things, I might find it the better of the two.  Kubuntu's crashed a few times and has had a few annoying errors, Ubuntu's been perfect so far.  I love mixing it up though.

Think I'm going to install Xubuntu on my old P3 machine just to have a faster, streamlined web-browser/music playin' box.

---
