### Post by jimcooncat on 2007-01-11
> **UncleFoo said:**
> I have been trying to like Kubuntu because the interface will be more familiar to PC users, but I have found it to be flakey and the admin side is sketchy (compared to Ubuntu).

If you want something that more closely resembles Windows, try  IceWM with the Silver XP or Windows Classic theme.

My opinion is that there are so many underlying differences between Windows (modern or classic) and *buntu that you shouldn't try to make appearances similar. When I first started using Linux with a GUI (not that long ago, I ran servers before) I kept looking for stuff that wasn't there. 

Where's my antivirus? Where's my antispyware? Where's Windows update? Oh yeah, I don't need to go through those pains anymore. I don't have to reboot to change my DNS numbers? Why did I ever think that rebooting for such a minor change was normal?

Now when I sit down at my computer I know I'm not in Redmond any more. What a relief -- the calm washes over me.

But I've yet to test my theory on die-hard Windows users. The folks I've introduced to Ubuntu have been noobs, and they're quite happy with their setups. I'll have my chance with the die-hards soon at the office.

I'd sure welcome a very light kubuntu-desktop without a gazillion options, though.  KMail and freinds are very nice, but I'll see how they fare under XFCE before jumping into KDE headfirst again.

### Post by ffi on 2007-01-11
> **yurimxpxman said:**
> Personally, I use Gnome. I've never been a big fan of KDE. Although I appreciate the developer's desire to add as many options as possible, I have never liked their implementation. Their menus are messy and swarming with options. Menus are embedded within menus within menus within menus. I think that's what people mean when they say they can't find anything in the K menu. It's just a long, unintuitive list.

Another thing I really hate about KDE is the way it displays the currently running programs in the taskbar. Having multiple rows of programs in small boxes means I have to read each box before I know which one to click. Even Windows is more efficient than that. :roll:


I have exactly the same feeling...about GNOME. So I guess it's just a personal thing. When I switched from windows to linux I could do all the things easily in KDE because everything seemed organized and logical whilst gnome was a frustrating nightmare. All the options seemed hidden, things like undo/apply buttons don't seem to exist, the menu look like a mess with stuff all over the place etc. etc. etc.

### Post by Erunno on 2007-01-12
Currently I'm using Kubuntu 6.10, as one might guess, because of KDE. 

I like the tight integration between the applications and prefer some of the standard apps over its GNOME counterparts (mainly Konqueror, Kontact. Kaffeine and Amarok). I'm not a big fan of the GUI though, the Ubuntu developers did a much better job with it than their KDE colleagues, especially with the colour scheme and the icon theme. Nevertheless, it's still easier to get the KDE GUI into a likeable shape via kde-look.org, kiosk mode and generall customizations to the toolbars than getting the missing features into GNOME. So I'll stick with it and just keep the fingers crossed that KDE 4 will clean up the interfaces without hiding the additional configuration options in an obscure place like the windows registry, err, I mean gnome-conf. ;)

---

### Post by OrangeCrate on 2007-01-12
> **Quillz said:**
> I think that's a good decision, although it could be a while before the next LTS version is released. (I don't think 7.04 will be LTS.) If you stick with 6.06, you'll be missing out on some much-needed enhancements that were made in 6.10, such as faster booting and more stable software.

You could be right, but by design, I think the LTS versions will be updated pretty frequently with enhancements. I've noticed that there has been a pretty aggressive upgrade cycle since the first of the year, including a bunch this morning for OOo.

Maybe one of the gurus could comment here...

My guess is that what works well from a stability and usability standpoint in the new versions such as 6.10, would probably be retrofitted to the LTS versions. For future Ubuntu marketshare increases for the business segment, keeping the LTS versions at the cutting edge of reliability would be something they would think about seriously.

Anyway, since there were no fools raised in this family, if there is a new feature I would really like in an upcoming version, I wouldn't hesitate to dump my original plan and upgrade. But for now, I'm pretty happy.

### Post by arvster on 2007-01-15
I used Gnome briefly (couple of months) when I was using Ubuntu. Before that there was KDE for a long time. Now I went on and moved to Arch Linux and after some thinking installed KDE on it. I just realized that KDE is more suited for me. Gnome is ok, but I just had to customize it quite a lot to make it fit. That said, I also have customize KDE as well to make me like it. IMO vanilla versions of both are not that great and need some work to make them usable the way I like it.

Talking about KDE being bloated- I don't think so. Gnome on Ubuntu took much more RAM and lagged more noticeably than almost any other distribution with KDE before it (except Suse- that also was bad). KDE seems faster and more memory efficient, at least on my machine. On more recent hardware I probably wouldn't notice any difference.

---

### Post by GeneralZod on 2007-01-15
> **arvster said:**
> 
Talking about KDE being bloated- I don't think so. Gnome on Ubuntu took much more RAM and lagged more noticeably than almost any other distribution with KDE before it (except Suse- that also was bad). KDE seems faster and more memory efficient, at least on my machine. On more recent hardware I probably wouldn't notice any difference.

Yep - two recent studies have been done on KDE vs GNOME memory usage.  The first (Sept 06) was by a KDE developer, and using the outdated GNOME 2.14:

[http://ktown.kde.org/~seli/memory/desktop_benchmark.html](http://ktown.kde.org/~seli/memory/desktop_benchmark.html)

KDE and GNOME usage was found to be practically identical, with KDE having a very slight edge.

The experiment was recently  (Dec 06) duplicated by a GNOME dev, with up-to-date versions of both KDE and GNOME:

[http://spooky-possum.org/cgi-bin/pyblosxom.cgi/kdevsgnome.html](http://spooky-possum.org/cgi-bin/pyblosxom.cgi/kdevsgnome.html)

and again, KDE and GNOME have practically identical memory usage.

The whole "KDE is bloated" thing is a myth that needs to die, in my opinion, unless of course the "KDE is bloated" camp have a different meaning of the word "bloat", in which case they should probably actually explain what they mean and hopefully cut down on pointless flamewars.

Stimpack's old sig said it best:

"Arguing about which of GNOME and KDE is the more bloated is like two fat men arguing about who is heaviest", or words to that effect :)

---

### Post by Kindred on 2007-01-15

Also I installed just a tiny fraction of the KDE package and the 'start' menu was already a mess and I felt I had more than enough to manage a desktop.  No wonder people say these things are bloated when distros install an enormous amount of junk... they don't have to be so fat but people don't really see that.

### Post by IYY on 2007-01-28
I always find that a list of adjectives is a fairly good way to compare the two. Here's what comes to mind:

KDE: crisp, sharp, cold, vivid, bright, blue, **robust**, functional.
Gnome: smooth, natural, warm, **simple**, elegant, intuitive.

KDE is big on adding more and more features, whereas Gnome actually *removes* features as times goes on. Gnome is more focused on simplicity, whereas KDE is focused on quantity. 

The interesting thing is that "simple vs. robust" is not the same as "easy vs. complicated". For example, sometimes KDE offers a graphical solution for something that needs the command-line in Gnome. However, sometimes the quantity of options in KDE can be overwhelming, and Gnome can accomplish the task much more elegantly.

### Post by kodoku on 2007-02-05
As a former XP user, I opted for KDE for my first leap into the linux world with kubuntu.  Appearance-wise, KDE definately wins: everything is shiny, glassy, colorful and eyecandy-esque.  That's fun and everything, but after a while, the "wow" effect wears off, and the environment just seems too bloated and clunky.  I reinstalled with GNOME, and appearance aside, it is (for me) much more efficient and clean.  With KDE, I used to have a few annoying problems like programs not launching (icon bouncing forever, you know the story), and the [very] occasional lock up (which was worse than Windows).  I have yet to have any problems with GNOME.  Even with Beryl running, it seems less sluggish than KDE (My system's modest, but not low end: Pentium 4 HT 2.8 Ghz, 1.5 Gb RAM, nVidia GeForce 4MX).

### Post by Tuna-Fish on 2007-02-16
> **Tomosaur said:**
> KDE is too irritating for me to use for extended periods of time. Yes, Gnome is stupid sometimes too, but at least it's not all in my face. I spend what, 2 seconds actually using gnome? Great. The number of times I need to use the gnome-panels is negligable, I just launch most stuff via launchers on the panel, or through the terminal. KDE just seems to want to show off all the time, and it just makes things take longer. Konquerer is a great piece of software though.

KDE too irritating, gnome too stupid? I know, happened to me too. Do as I did, switch to Xubuntu. XFCE4 does a very good job for everything that the DE should do, more specifically, it does 4 things very well:
(a): It doesn't assume the user is an idiot.
(b): It doesn't assume the user is a masochist.
(c): It understands its place: It doesn't belong in your face, but humbly on the background supporting you when you need it.
(d): Compared to both KDE and Gnome it is very, very fast, and thus, very pleasant to use.

The version of xfce4 that shipped with edgy is not really very good, but when you migrate to feisty, I suggest you give xubuntu a go.

### Post by gorilla on 2007-02-16
It's exactly what I think about Gnome as well. Sure it's nice that Gnome makes it easy for people who have problems with using computers, but they could just place useful functionality somewhere where it doesn't confuse troubled people. But instead they put away with everything that is too advanced from a grandma point of view (before you say it, of course there are also many old people who are not computer-impaired).

Gnome is completely designed for first-timers, without any possibility for the user to advance beyond his first-steps stage. With other software, you find out cool tricks over the time, handy new features, in short, you'll learn how to use it in an advanced and more sophisticated way, getting more out of your software. 
Not so with Gnome; A user feels comfortable on his first day, but he'll stay a 0-day user for the rest of his life.

### Post by warp99 on 2007-02-17
All three desktops (KDE, Gnome, Xcfe) have good and bad points. I prefer KDE because I have a little bit more control over colors and customization then with Gnome, plus I cut my teeth on Linux with SuSe when they were still a German company. I use KDE on my amd64 Athlon tower. It has the resources to handle the desktop, so I don't notice any difference between KDE and Gnome  

Xcfe is on my Mac G3 ibook because it's only 300mhz, but it's got 545 of ram and a 20 gig HD. At one time or another I had Gnome on the ibook, but it was a resource hog compared to Xcfe. 

Anyway it's all about choice and the more the choices the better. :D

“If you limit your choices only to what seems possible or reasonable, you disconnect yourself from what you truly want, and all that is left is compromise.” - Robert Fritz

### Post by chavo on 2007-02-17
Linus has been against Gnome as long as I have, ever since they castrated it. When they started taking the choice away from me I went to KDE. 

Linux is about choice, if you choose not to have a choice and use Gnome that's all good, but don't spread FUD about KDE using more resources. It's simply not true [http://ktown.kde.org/~seli/memory/desktop_benchmark.html](http://ktown.kde.org/~seli/memory/desktop_benchmark.html). KDE is faster, sleeker and much more configurable. Have it your way.

### Post by Kalinda on 2007-02-17
Well, as long as we're on the subject: I do find it somewhat frustrating at the lack of Kubuntu-specific support around here.. and KDE probably runs better on other distros.. but..

The problem with switching is that I then lose the very large community (all the howtos here use the terminal commands so they're easy to use in all the forks), the fact that Ubuntu is designed for n00bs (and I'm not any higher then an intermediate), and that it's been relatively easy to get most things working here, things I couldn't make work in SuSe. And that aside, I hate RPMs because of their problem with dependencies, whereas debs and apt are a much better package management system... and the Ubuntu repos have 95% of all the programs I want to install anyway. Then there's Automatix, which cuts down the amount of time it takes to setup a fresh install.

So, even though KDE support is somewhat poorer then I'd like, I don't think I want to switch. I briefly considered Sbayon (Beryl!!), until I realized it was based on Gentoo, the distro where you have to compile everything (or so I've heard).. I don't mind compiling things, but having to compile everything would be a royal pain. I tried the Live CD and it was nice.

My only gripe with KDE is its seeming lack of support (or interest) for Beryl; it has more problems with Beryl then GNOME does in Ubuntu. Also there were some of the irritating little bugs I've found it has.

---

### Post by warp99 on 2007-02-17
> **Sklasko said:**

This is why Linux will never have a major hold in the desktop computer market. The average user doesn't want to listen to the man who's supposed to have a hold on things, bitching about how a certain desktop environment isn't designed the way he likes. However, don't get me wrong, I respect him for Linux; but this is simply unnecessary.

Please, Bill Gates has been crying for years over desktop this or that and what's Microsoft market share again? Linux does not have a major market share because it is not preloaded on your brand new computer from Best Buy, Circuit City, PC World, et al. plus is goes against the planned obsoletion model that manufacturers have embraced to keep turning profits.

I personally believe that the road to Linux acceptance is through multi-function devices and the transformation of the personal computer itself. Linux should get a jump on the competition and start moving towards multi-touch input systems:

[Multi-Touch Interaction Research]("http://cs.nyu.edu/~jhan/ftirtouch/")

Vista is way too bloated to ever achieve the next logical step for future computing interaction.  Linux is the perfect candidate for this technology, why are we worrying about desktops when obviously they are going to be a relic of the past. :D

### Post by maddog39 on 2007-02-17
Well I must agree that I also believed Linux would rise above this. He went in and completely bashed them, in their faces to.. kind of unexpected behavior if you ask me.

> KDE is faster, sleeker and much more configurable. Oh and by the way that memory benchmark is from 3.5.2 and the past couple of KDE releases have concentrated on speedups, oh you poor Gnomians.This is what I really dont like, because this is only a matter opinion. Simply because I have had opisite results from you. I believe that to me GNOME feels faster than KDE (as far applications init's and stuff go). I have made several honest attempts to like KDE and even documented them and I have come to the conclusion that it just isnt for me. So I continue to use GNOME. 

Another thing I find is that Ubuntu/Debian just isn't that great of a mix. I am always having issues screwing around with IPv6 or whatever it is and tinkering KDE config files to get my net to be decently fast. I have used KDE on other distros and my results have been much much better.

Yet another problem I have is that I much prefer certain GNOME apps over KDE ones. Such as, epiphany 2.17.x (i really cant stand konqueror) and some others. On the other hand I like certain apps Kate better than say gedit. But not only that, I also program GTK+ interfaces and prefer its toolkit and how its structured much more so than Qt. I have tried Qt 3.3.x's tutorials a few times and I just dont like the way it does things. Also, GTK interfaces in KDE look really ugly and KDE tries to emulate its theme for GTK apps and usually it doesn't turn out that great (in my opinion).

### Post by Mateo on 2007-02-17
So 5 pages and every seems to agree that KDE is "more configurable" yet only **1** actual example, something about a middle button.

I said on page 2, if KDE is more configurable, I want to hear about it!  I'm interested in hearing what these things are, and I might switch if it sounds interesting to me.

Here are a couple of "deal breaker" questions I have about KDE:

1) Can you turn off the numbers in the workspaces applet?

2) Is it possible to have a normal looking clock?  The digital clock is simply unbearably ugly to me.

3) Going with the clock, does the clock integrate with whatever the KDE calendar program is, the same way that the gnome clock applet integrates with evolution?

---

### Post by GeneralZod on 2007-02-17
> **Mateo said:**
> S
1) Can you turn off the numbers in the workspaces applet?


You have numbers, names (of your choice), or no label at all.

> 
2) Is it possible to have a normal looking clock?  The digital clock is simply unbearably ugly to me.


You have the options of Plain (pick your own font), Digital, Analogue (i.e. with graphical hands) and the rather amusing Fuzzy (where the time is spelled out in text!).

> 
3) Going with the clock, does the clock integrate with whatever the KDE calendar program is, the same way that the gnome clock applet integrates with evolution?

Truly bizarrely, no - all the frameworks and stuff are in place, but no one appears to have done this, yet.  There's a calendar accessible from the clock, but it doesn't appear to have custom dates marked on it.

I say "bizarrely" as you can (optionally) have e.g. the online IM presence of your contacts displayed in your Contacts page, or even in the header of e-mails they've sent to you - heck, you can even send files to your IM contacts via a right-click menu in Konqueror! So given KDE's penchant for integration, the Clock/ Calendar thing is something I'd really expect them to have done - but, apparently not.

Edit: Aha - here's the bugzilla entry for it:

[http://bugs.kde.org/show_bug.cgi?id=24298](http://bugs.kde.org/show_bug.cgi?id=24298)

the answer to most of the above is "no". its worth bearing in mind the following:
-there is no written law in UI design that says that the user wants to customise every last drop of the desktop.  in most cases, they just want to use it to get thier work done. sensible defaults are all that is required. users who want to go that bit further and customise everything are often at the power user stage (or at least, aspire to be), so using gconf-editor and the terminal isn't exactly rocket science.

### Post by PatrickMay16 on 2007-02-17
> **ComplexNumber said:**
> the answer to most of the above is "no". its worth bearing in mind the following:
-there is no written law in UI design that says that the user wants to customise every last drop of the desktop.  in most cases, they just want to use it to get thier work done. sensible defaults are all that is required. users who want to go that bit further and customise everything are often at the power user stage (or at least, aspire to be), so using gconf-editor and the terminal isn't exactly rocket science.

The answer to most of my points is "no"? How so? I encountered and experienced all of these in my times with KDE and gnome.

And I think you'd be suprised at the amount of normal people who actually do want to do things like change the color scheme to their liking,  and many people will find the gnome desktop ugly if they happen to use applications which all use different GUI toolkits. gnome usually doesn't theme GTK1 applications unless the particular theme includes a gtkrc file for gtk1, and doesn't do anything to qt applications. But KDE applies the color scheme to everything.

And what if a visually impaired person wants to change the color scheme to be mostly dark, with white text on black backgrounds? With gnome, unless a suitable color scheme is included with the system, they have to look through gnome-look.org or art.gnome.org with their fingers crossed and hope to find something suitable. With KDE, they can just go into the KDE control center and specify the colors to be used.
Windows has had this capability since its very first version! It's as basic as being allowed to specify what fonts should be used, it should just be there. 

GNOME is good, but there are some features that just should be there, that aren't.

### Post by aysiu on 2007-02-17
This has headed away from Linus and more into the regular KDE v. Gnome realm. I've merged it with the megathread.

By the way, here are a few configuration options (immediately) available (via the GUI) in KDE that aren't available in Gnome:
- ability to choose folder for screensaver slideshow
- ability to change the view on open dialogues (to be able to see thumbnails of pictures to upload, for example)
- ability to make a webpage your wallpaper
- ability to view metadata (like ID3 tags) as part of the file manager
- ability to make the toolbar fully transparent (not just the middle part)
- ability to specify which applications start in full screen and which don't
- ability to have numlock on at startup
- ability to have different file extensions of the same mimetype open with different applications
- ability to have a different wallpaper for each virtual desktop

That's all I can think of off the top of my head. Does that make KDE "better"? Of course not. I personally don't care for KDE or Gnome right now. IceWM does everything I want and is a lot faster than either of those desktop environments.

I'm just giving some examples because someone asked for examples.

There are many things I like better in Gnome even though it's "less configurable." For example, the Removable Drives & Media interface is simple and configurable (check boxes and commands), unlike the one for KDE. And System > Administration > Login Window is built right into the default Gnome. I think for you to configure KDM themes graphically, you have to install some separate application.

Bottom line: to each her own. Maybe Linus' gripes have some validity, but who cares? If Linus likes KDE, he should use KDE. If other people like Gnome, they can use Gnome. Still others, like me, prefer XFCE, IceWM, Fluxbox, Enlightenment, or a host of other window managers.

---

### Post by Kindred on 2007-02-18
- ability to be able to switch desktops by scrolling on your desktop
- ability to be able to move windows across workspaces by dragging them off the screen
- ability to be able to configure all kinds of mouse clicks on the title bar so you can shade and unshade windows with a scroll up/down (for example)

This is all Metacity stuff alone. 

I don't use either KDE/Gnome but i'd like to see them get better so I can one day.

### Post by Rodneyck on 2007-02-18
**Linus fires latest shot in GNOME Wars**

Some bad blood between Linus Torvalds and GNOME developers is flaring up again. Previously, Torvalds has said that Linux users should switch to KDE instead of GNOME because of the GNOME team's "users are idiots" mentality. Now he has "put his money where his mouth is" by submitting patches to GNOME in order to have it behave as he likes.
 
This week, on the Linux Foundation's (formerly OSDL) Desktop Architects mailing list, the two sides are going mano a mano. On Monday, Marcos Pérez López replied in Spanish to an earlier message from Torvalds in which Torvalds lashed out at the GNOME crew, claiming GNOME developers believed their users were idiots. López's reply defending GNOME was in Spanish, and he closed it by saying: 

¿Quién es el NAZI?
Who is the Nazi? 

 Nada más, esto para LINUS para que piense. Nothing more, this is for Linus to think about. 
 LINUS, NO SABES LEER ESPAÑOL, ¿A VER SI VAS A SER IDIOTA TU TAMBIÉN? Linus, you don't know how to read Spanish, so are you an idiot too? 

Godwin's Law was quickly invoked by Fernando Herrera, but that didn't stop the debate. Torvalds took the Spanish in stride, and continued the argument by saying it was good for GNOME to be easy to use, but that "'ONLY being easy to use' is bad." He also noted that "GNOME people seem to think that once you 'got into it,' you never want to do anything more. Not true." 

Christian F.K. Schaller then threw down the gauntlet, urging Torvalds to action with: 
If you are up for a challenge, why don't you use GNOME for a month then come and do a talk about your experience at this years GUADEC in England? Could maybe be a good way to start a constructive dialog instead of this useless mudslinging?

 This morning, Torvalds responded to the challenge, though not in the manner Schaller suggested. He submitted patches to GNOME to make it behave as he wants, then told the mailing list: 

I've sent out patches. The code is actually _cleaner_ after my patches, and the end result is more capable. We'll see what happens.

 THAT is constructive. 

 What I find unconstructive is how the GNOME people always make *excuses*. It took me a few hours to actually do the patches. It wasn't that hard. So why didn't I do it years ago? 

 I'll tell you why: because GNOME apologists don't say "please send us patches". No. They basically make it clear that they aren't even *interested* in fixing things, because their dear old Mum isn't interested in the feature. 

 Do you think that's "constructive"? 

 So let's see what happens to my patches. I guarantee you that they actually improve the code (not just add a feature). I also guarantee that they actually make things *more* logical rather than less (with my patches, double-clicking on the title bar isn't a special event: it's configurable along with right- and middle-clicking, and with the exact same syntax for all). 

 But why, oh, why, have GNOME people not just said "please fix it then"? 

 Instead, I _still_ (now after I sent out the patch) hear more of your kvetching about how you actually do everything right, and it's somehow *my* fault that I find things limiting. 

 Here's a damn big clue: the reason I find GNOME limiting is BECAUSE IT IS. 

 Now the question is, will people take the patches, or will they keep their heads up their arses and claim that configurability is bad, even when it makes things more logical, and code more readable.

 Welcome to wonderful, wacky, never-dull world of free and open source software, where in spite of all the posturing and debate, the answer is always best couched in code.

[http://www.linux.com/article.pl?sid=07/02/16/1937237](http://www.linux.com/article.pl?sid=07/02/16/1937237)

### Post by FyreBrand on 2007-02-18
> **ComplexNumber said:**
> Linus should stick to something he's good at - developing the kernel.Why is that?  If he writes a graphic UI as good as his kernel then why should he stick to it?  There isn't any indication that he can't do that.  GNU on the other hand certainly can't say the same thing about being able to develop of working kernel.

Maybe the tiff between them is more than just a criticism about the UI and the apparent disinterest in GNU, GNOME, and Stallman in integrating the ideas of the community.  There is GPL v2/3.  There is GNU wanting their name at the front of the picture (GNU/Linux).  There is the rigid development mindset of GNOME.

KDE has faced a lot of criticism in the past (and present) and at least has given the impression that they are listening to and accepting input from the community.  GNOME has given nearly the opposite message and has a image of being critical to software that is developed outside their view.  I think it could especially be this last part that rubs Linus the wrong way.  Remember when they chose the mascot in the early days Linux wanted something he felt represented Linux.  Here is a quote from sjbaker.org/tux quoting an email from Linus:
[quote=Linus Torvalds]It's supposed to be kind of goofy and fun, that's the whole point. Linux is supposed to be goofy and fun (it's also the best operating system out there, but it's goofy and fun at the same time!).[/quote]

The GNU/GNOME developers don't have a lot of the fun goofy thing going on.  They kind of have the stuffy stodgy thing.  Even after all these years Stallman still seems to have a chip on his should about the UNIX thing.  That's not fun.

### Post by sicofante on 2007-02-18
> **ComplexNumber said:**
> Linus should stick to something he's good at - developing the kernel.

> **FyreBrand said:**
> Why is that?  If he writes a graphic UI as good as his kernel then why should he stick to it?  There isn't any indication that he can't do that.  GNU on the other hand certainly can't say the same thing about being able to develop of working kernel.
Has Linus Torvalds shown his abilities as a user interaction designer? If he did, I just missed it. If he doesn't his opinion on user interaction models is as good as anyone's. Moreover: if he's dedicated to system development his opinion is probably worse than anyone's. He's too close to the problem and in the wrong side at the same time. I'm inclined to think the opposite of what he thinks on this particular subject.

I suggest some readings on user interaction design and you'll see why a system developer is the last person you want to ask when it comes to user interaction. (Here's one of many lists on the subject: [http://www.eleganthack.com/reading/](http://www.eleganthack.com/reading/))

Unfortunately, too many Linux advocates, developers and even users still think user interaction design must stay in the hands of system developers. And they keep wondering why we've got such an insignificant market share...

### Post by sicofante on 2007-02-18
I know what Kindred was answering to, and my reply had to do with it. I would agree that UI is not the only force driving market share. Saying it has "nothing to do" with it is just too much, though. The discussion about the nature of Apple's and Linux's market share is really off topic so I'll leave it here.

Still, my point is a system developer has no special authority regarding user interaction design. On the contrary, he's handicapped by his very dedication: coding for system's internals. This does not mean everything Linus Torvalds seays about Gnome or KDE or any other user interaction approach is *necessarily *wrong, but it's *probably *wrong.

It is the unwillingness of GNOME to listen or accept criticism that is the real problem.

### Post by IYY on 2007-02-18
> I think there is a point in "clean and simple" and "reconfigurability" that they aren't mutually exclusive. The GNOME team seems to like designing for how they think the end user should interact with their system and doesn't like listening to criticism or feedback about that.

It has been shown that listening to feedback too much is not how you design a user interface. The main reason is that every user will request some sort of obscure feature that would be incredibly useful for him, but useless for other users. If you implement all of these features, the interface will become bloated and confusing. 

I find that KDE suffers from this bloat of features. I have been using computers since the days of DOS, and have a very strong intuition. I can figure out most user interfaces in minutes, without ever reading any documentation. Windows, Gnome, Photoshop, Gimp, 3D Studio Max, all office software... no problem. However, I've spent weeks trying to figure out KDE interfaces and they always confuse me. It's not that I can't find what I want; I find it eventually, but the search is frustrating. And with all of KDE's configurability, I still can't configure it to work just right. 

When I buy a cell phone, I don't buy the one that has a ton of features. In fact, I try to get as few features as possible, because I know that a phone is supposed to do one thing and do it well. I don't want it to have a built in camera, MP3 player, calendar... I want it to have the most basic features, easily accessible.

My favorite example of how UI can become bloated and ugly when you let users design it is the Simpsons episode when Homer's brother lets him be in charge of designing the ultimate car "for regular people". This is the result:

[IMG]http://girtby.net/images/homer-car.gif[/IMG]
 
Looks an awful lot like KDE to me.

### Post by GeneralZod on 2007-02-18
> **rai4shu2 said:**
> And my point that no one even noticed apparently is that Linus whines about how Gnome people never "fix" anything, then he proceeds to give a patch that I suppose is meant to add some configurability to mouse clicks, and *is not an actual fix* for Gnome. Lame.

It's also worth pointing out that not only did the patch add a feature, but it also *improved and simplified the code*.  It would be utterly trivial to separate out the cleanups from the added features.  Now, you can debate Linus's abilities as a UI designer all you want (for the record: if a kernel developer sent me a patch that added functionality to the UI, I would eye it skeptically), but when the guy responsible for coordinating one of the largest and most successful software projects in the world - when the guy who has architected a large codebase so well that 1000s of developers will give up their free time just to work on it - when the guy who has personally vetted countless patches with an eye to their long-term effect on the code based and their future maintainability - when this guy hands me a patch and says that this code will make your code cleaner and more maintenable, you can bet I'll merge that s**t so fast that bits will fly off my harddrive! :)

PS

Linus must be stubborn, but not in a petty, bureacratic kind of way: he is a meritocrat at heart; prove to him that your solution is correct and he'll usually take your side.

### Post by aysiu on 2007-02-18
> **GeneralZod said:**
> when this guy hands me a patch and says that this code will make your code cleaner and more maintenable, you can bet I'll merge that s**t so fast that bits will fly off my harddrive! :) Or at least look at it? Consider it?

As far as UIs go, people tend to talk about UIs in terms of extremes--either simple and not confusing or complex and confusing (the labels most frequently attributed in these forums to Gnome and KDE, respectively). It is possible to strike a happy medium. For example, I like the way Gnome and KDE handle the hiding of files. Many configuration files are hidden by default, but you can easily unhide them by going to the **view** option and unhiding them or pressing a simple key combination (Control-H or Alt-V, H). In Windows or Mac OS X, you have to do a lot more clicking or command-line work to unhide or hide directories, to the point where people using those OSes generally keep them hidden or unhidden--toggling is too annoying.

In both Gnome and KDE, the default (keep those configuration files hidden) behavior is sensible, but you're also making easily available a way to change that or modify it.

And, as I point out with this example--it is not KDE- or Gnome-specific. Sometimes there is good implementation in Gnome (Removable Drives & Medium) and sometimes there is good implementation in KDE (screensaver dialogue). I'm also a big fan of presenting simple options at first but then having an **Advanced Options** button placed somewhere so that those who care to have more configurability via point-and-click can have that, but those who don't care can easily avoid all those extraneous options.

Lastly, having more options in the GUI doesn't mean those options have to be confusing. That has more to do with design and implementation than the *number* of options offered. For example, KDE's taskbar configuration is pretty straightforward and offers a lot of options, but KDE's external devices dialogue makes absolutely no sense. Likewise, Gnome's keyboard shortcuts dialogue is straightforward, but the same deal for making custom shortcuts via *gconf-editor* > Apps > Metacity > Global Keybinding / Keybinding Commands isn't intuitive or easy to find at all.

While difficult (obviously), it is not impossible to create a UI experience that pleases **most** people (not the same as **everybody**) by making sensible defaults, a few immediate options, and some cleverly organized advanced options--all via point-and-click.

### Post by deanlinkous on 2007-02-18
quoting a famous person....
> Yeah. I'm probably going senile...

This is what a typical control panel experience looks like to me:

Okay. I want to change my screen resolution, cos everything is too small.
"Appearance & Themes"? Hmmm. No. "Desktop"? I'm resizing my desktop. Maybe it is there? No! Ahh It's under "Peripherals->Display" (I thought my monitor was part of my computer).

Okay. Now I want to change my desktop background.
"Desktop"! I has to be there! No... It is under "Appearance & Themes". I was being too clever.

My colours are all funny. (Let's say the colour depth is incorrect.) I try "Appearance and Themes"! No. "Desktop" No. It's under Peripherals->Display again.

I've seen people with a nice pattern on their 'Start Button'. I want it! "Appearance and Themes" It has to be there this time! No. "Peripherals->Display"? No. "Desktop"?.... Eventually I find it under "Desktop->Panel->Appearance"!

I want to change what my login screen looks like.. "Appearance and Themes"? Nope ... Administration->KDM Theme Manager.

The fonts are too small in the file manager... "Appearance and Themes->Fonts"? No. "KDE Components->File Manager->Fonts". Eh?!

Okay... let's forget appearances...

Now my keyboard isn't giving me the right characters. I've seen "Keyboard" on my travels in the Peripherals section. I'm learning where things are now. Oh yes.
No! It isn't there! Well where the hell is it, if it isn't under "Keyboard"?!
Eventually I find it under "Regional and Accessibility->Keyboard Layout"

My clock is showing the time in Japan, and I'm in the UK. Well... now it has to be under "Regional and Accessibility". I see countries listed... I'll change my country! Brilliant! I've done one thing right.

£%*&£% My clock still shows the wrong time.

At this point I set fire to my computer.

### Post by CaptSaltyJack on 2007-02-19
I'm having a tough time deciding which one I want to commit to and run permanently.  A few things I've noted (my opinions of course)

Where Ubuntu is stronger than Kubuntu:
- feels slightly more lightweight (Gnome is more spry than KDE, or am I imagining it?)
- Gnome session manager rocks, beats KDE's lame ~/.kde/Autostart directory (Gnome session manager can specify which order to run things, KDE cannot)
- Synaptic is slightly better than Adept, but Adept is pretty great
- I kinda generally prefer Gnome apps over K apps

Where Kubuntu is stronger than Ubuntu:
- looks nicer visually
- KDE's "System Services" runlevel manager blows Ubuntu's "bum" away (bum is really bad)
- SuperKaramba looks nicer than gDesklets
- System Settings section seems to have more comprehensive options than Gnome

I just really hate the "bum" runlevel manager for Gnome.. ugh.  And it's important enough because I'm into setting up services on this thing and configuring them where I like the interface and don't want to have to manually muck around in the /etc/rc?.d directories.

So.. I'm just blabbing here, but feel free to chime in.

PS: Does Beryl run particularly better on Ubuntu or Kubuntu?  Or is it plagued with problems equally on both? :D

### Post by Erik Trybom on 2007-02-19
I think Gnome has the better looks and a cleaner interface in most cases. Also the Ubuntu-specific apps (like Synaptic) are better than KDE:s counterparts. On the other hand, a lot of other KDE apps are excellent (like K3b, Ktorrent and amaroK).

I think pure Ubuntu is better right now (Edgy) because of the stability. From my experience KDE crashes more often and more severe than Gnome. 

But why choose? Hard disk space is cheap these days and I think it's worth having a few extra libraries installed so you can run both kind of apps. Just pick the WM of your choice and install any application you want. 

Speed is nice, but anything running at more than 1 GHz should be able to handle all these environments without lag.

### Post by brokencrystal on 2007-02-24
Gnome:

I like Gnome because it is simple. I feel it is missing some VERY important features though! 

I think it is somewhat difficult to change simple things like the splash screen. 

The applets on the panels can be ugly if they do not match the panel with a custom or transparent background. To fix this, the applets should have transparent backgrounds. The applet seperators are ugly and do not match the panel if it is somewhat transparent or a different color than white. 

Gnome is missing the right click, open terminal here and the right click open nautilus here options. I would like to go into nautilus settings and click a checkbox to turn this feature on. I do not want to find, download, and add scripts to a folder to get these features. A folder that the average user (Gnome's Targeted Demographic) does not even know exhists. 

They need 'Create New Document' templates in the right click menu that can be installed via software such as OpenOffice.org or Gimp. Example: Right Click -> New -> OpenOffice.org SpreadSheet... Right Click -> New -> Gimp Image... 

I also hate that little arrow that appears on the Main Menu applet. I wish they would leave that up to the distro to include in the Main Menu custom graphic rather than hard coded into the OS. It is impossible to remove without recompiling Gnome!  Very upsetting.

Gnome also needs the ability to change a theme by pointing to only one file to change everything. For example, if I want (Don't hate me for saying this, it's just an example that people will understand) a Vista theme installed... 

I have to select a splash screen, try to figure out how to install it, get a login screen, apply that and select it, get an icon set, apply that and select it, get window border themes, apply that and select it, find wallpaper, apply that and select it, find cursors, apply that and select it, find screensavers, apply that and select it, find a start button to replace the applet button, try to figure out how to install it... and so on. Can't I just make or find a theme that is in one .theme file and right click on it and select 'Use this theme'? Why do things have to be so difficult?


KDE:

The main thing I don't like about KDE is how cluttered it is. 

The bouncing cursor can sometimes (when the computer slows) create ugly artifacts on my desktop. 

The taskbar is 15' thick by default.  (but easily customized)

Mostly what I don't like about KDE is how cluttered and ununiform it is! That is enough to turn me off. That is the reason I still use Gnome even with all it's work-arounds...


Conclusion:

That said, I think there is room for improvement on both sides. ;- }

### Post by IYY on 2007-02-25
> Can you explain what makes you think KDE is cluttered? Is it having two toolbars? Or three menus?

Some people might find the following offensive, or disagree with me, but here is my opinion about KDE's clutter, selecting only a single application (konqueror):

[IMG]http://img137.imageshack.us/img137/1452/56540128nx4.gif[/IMG]

1. Every toolbar has a handle that allows you to move it. It might seem like a good feature, but it adds another entirely useless control, and an extra affordance to the control object (this means that now the user sees the toolbar as something that can be clicked *as well as* dragged, adding confusion and complexity). Now, is the handle a useful feature? I hold the opinion that the placement of controls like menus is up to the designer, not the user. In addition of adding this new affordance and useless degree of freedom, the freedom to move the menu around also makes every user's interface different, leading to problems of consistency (for example, teaching how to use this interface becomes more difficult, because every user's interface is potentially different).

2. To the left of every file manager window there is a toolbar with even more icons. This toolbar has all sorts of functionality: history, favorites, shortcuts to places, amarok... It's a useful thing, but does the average user need to see the history of his file management? Does he even use amarok? When the user sees this toolbar, the opinion formed is: "There are buttons here I need to learn how to use, because they may be important.". In reality, these buttons are not important at all.

3. The file manager has tabs. Why does it need them? The only reason to have two file manager windows open is to drag and drop files between them. This is possible, but inconvenient in tabbed view. A whole extra row of screen space, and a minimum of 3 extra buttons for the user to see and be confused about.

4. An address bar that has been replaced by a far more modern and useful mode of navigation in Gnome. 

5. The rotating KDE/Kubuntu logo to the right of every file manager. It's sole purpose is to emulate Windows Explorer. It has absolutely no other function, since your system will never actually take time loading a directory.

6. The sound icon, and a few other icons and graphics in the system are ancient, yet constantly visible. Remember: even a single outdated icon that's always being displayed is enough to destroy the look of the entire interface. That one icon alone makes all of KDE look amateurish to me.

7. The wallet system. I understand that it's supposed to be handy, but I usually just end up having to do twice the clicking and password entering.

8. Konqui. First of all, a desktop environment shouldn't have a mascot. The desktop environment should not even be something that you *notice* while using your computer. The new user asks: "Who is this dragon creature? Is it Ubuntu's mascot? Is it Linux's mascot? No... It's the desktop environment's mascot. What's a desktop environment?" And second, Konqui is a horribly designed creature. It's poorly rendered, poorly designed, and not even very imaginative. It looks like something that belongs in '97, along with the dancing baby gifs and tiling website backgrounds.

9. Gloss. Some users like it, but it's overdone and isn't very impressive anymore. Even Apple is getting rid of the glossy look.

10. Allowing the user to customize every little aspect of the desktop, and at the same time making installation of themes too complicated. The user doesn't want to change the colour of the radiobuttons, he wants to drag and drop a theme from the Internet!

Why these problems? Most of them are simply due to the fact that KDE insists on pushing Konqueror as a multipurpose swiss army knife. But why have one application do two entirely different things: browsing the Internet and browsing the filesystem. The two have absolutely nothing in common. 
 
Most people take Gnome's usability guidelines for granted, or see them as annoyances, but they truly are what makes Gnome great. They follow everything I have learned in all of my User Interfaces and Design classes. KDE, on the other hand, breaks every single rule. 

I am not going to say that KDE has no place. It certainly is not for the novice, or average home user (they just want an intuitive interface that works and looks good), and it is not for the true power user (we just use the command-line well enough not to require a GUI for every possible operation). The people in between may enjoy KDE. 

I'm sorry if this sounds like a rant... I am tired and not thinking very coherently. I don't actually hate KDE, and have used it on several occasions.

compared to
[http://img111.imageshack.us/img111/8729/etchgnome4bw2.png](http://img111.imageshack.us/img111/8729/etchgnome4bw2.png)
I mean it needs a search function to find what you want to use? Wouldn't that be a admission of clutter, complexity, etc.
To quote a famous person about the KDE control center...


---okay a test---
Can you tell me (from memory - not by looking) where exactly in KDE control center I go if my complaint is that the fonts are too small when I am browsing my folders?

this file manager
[http://img206.imageshack.us/img206/9861/screenshot3dc2.png](http://img206.imageshack.us/img206/9861/screenshot3dc2.png)
compared to this
[http://img180.imageshack.us/img180/9412/etchkde4ep6.png](http://img180.imageshack.us/img180/9412/etchkde4ep6.png)
What jumps out at me is nine menu choices on KDE compared to six on gnome, five seperator bars on KDE compared to two on gnome, foot graphic throbber compared to the a blue box containing a throbber,Personally I think both your top examples are cluttered and busy, but that's just my opinion.  This is how I like my desktop.  I normally have the kicker panel autohide.  The bottom panel might be busy for some, but it has what I want on it and it's how I like it.  So it works for me.  When I'm doing work it's not in my way.  The top panel has the pager, task bar, and clock on it.  I don't really consider my desktop cluttered at all.  Here are a couple screen shots.
[[IMG]http://img228.imageshack.us/img228/9074/desktopwithkickeryo9.th.jpg[/IMG]](http://img228.imageshack.us/my.php?image=desktopwithkickeryo9.jpg)
[[IMG]http://img81.imageshack.us/img81/8864/desktophiddenkickeryo5.th.jpg[/IMG]](http://img81.imageshack.us/my.php?image=desktophiddenkickeryo5.jpg)

I don't know where you got that screen shot of Konqueror and what distro that is, but it's not Kubuntu's default.  The Kubuntu default Konq only has 7 menu items.  I erased my personal directory name from the picture, but other than that I don't change anything.  Here is a screen shot opening Konqueror as the file browser.
[[IMG]http://img92.imageshack.us/img92/3474/kubunutkonqap4.th.png[/IMG]](http://img92.imageshack.us/my.php?image=kubunutkonqap4.png)
If the font size for my icons is too large or small I just go into settings and appearance and change it.  It's about 3 mouse clicks.  And no I didn't memorize I looked at the the Settings menu which is 1 click and it is *intuitively obvious* what entry I needed -- Appearance.  This isn't a highschool test.  It's real life and I don't memorize how to do everything.  If it isn't obvious then I look it up.  If it's complicated I bookmark it and make notes.  Maybe you have to memorize stuff in Gnome, but I never found that to be the case either on my Gnome desktops.

KDE definitely has things that irritate me and don't always work, but then again I have yet to use a piece of software that is perfect.  For example my KDM resolution is just whacked.  I can't get it to change no matter what I do and I can't find the config files to do it.  That's why the xorg.conf backups are in my home directory.  Does it irritate the hell out of me?  Yes!!!  Do I think KDE and Kubuntu is utter rubbish? No.  In fact Kubuntu, while criticized for being a tad sluggish, is much more stable and less prone to breakage than some other KDE distros I've tried.  I really appreciate the effort the KDE devs have put into stability and usefulness first and letting cutting edge eye candy take a back seat to that.  I've been testing out Sabayon some.  Even with KDE as default and Gnome, E16, and XFCE4 and beryl is still zippier than either Ubuntu or Kubuntu fresh installs.  However, I find it nowhere near as stable as either.  Also the community here (even with little Gnomes) is much friendlier and more helpful.

### Post by jbus on 2007-02-25

Linus also doesn't seem to be very impressed with ubuntu, based upon a video I saw posted on digg a couple weeks back. That doesn't change my opinion on ubuntu. He has the right to his opinions just like everyone else has the right to theirs.

It's no more his business what desktop environment I choose to use than it is my business which one he chooses to use. Linus is a great kernel developer, but is probably no better qualified to judge what a good desktop environment is than anyone else. This will hurt Linux more than help it.

### Post by IYY on 2007-02-25
> **Erunno said:**
> Could you provide some specific examples ? Thanks.

In my eyes, nearly all of the points I raised in my last points were examples of careless design. While the developers did appear to have thoughts like "This is a cool feature, I will add it" running through their heads, they certainly have never considered any usability principles and research, or even deeply considered how the program appears in the eyes of the user. Any usability expert will tell you that adding features should be done with *extreme* caution. The reason being that once a feature is there, removing it will greatly annoy users (actually, that's a mistake that Gnome sometimes makes, and I criticize them for it). 

Let's take a single example, and try to guess what the developer was thinking.

Konqueror being both a file manager and a browser. The developer thinks: "I have developed a web browser, but I can also make it manage files. This adheres to what I learned in my programming classes, since I'd be making the program more flexible, generic and reusable. It is a good feature." While it may be controversial or offensive to suggest this, I also believe that the programmer had the following thought: "Windows has one application for browsing the web and managing files; if they managed to do it, surely we can too!"

What the developer did not take into consideration is that Konqueror is not a programming project, but an *interface* project. What he should have been thinking was: "The mindset of the user while browsing the web is entirely different than the mindset of the user when managing files. This implies that there should be two different tools for the tasks. While the two do share some similarities (a notion of backwards and forwards, a home location, bookmarks), the differences in interaction are so vast that the apparent similarity would only *confuse* the user (the notion of up in addition to back and forward, the home location being the home folder rather than the home webpage, bookmarks being used for different purposes). Also, most features of the application in browser mode will be useless in manager mode, and vice versa, further confusing the user. The conclusion: while it would be a nice technical accomplishment, from a usability standpoint the two applications should remain separate."

The way things are right now, both Gnome and KDE have problems, and their problems are in fact one and the same: stubbornness and pride. KDE developers are too stubborn and proud to accept the criticism of a usability expert, whereas the Gnome developers are too stubborn to accept the criticism of the software engineer or common user. The problem is not that KDE developers don't value usability at all, but they just value it less than robustness, functionality and technical merits. So when a new feature is contemplated, they will consider where to place it, but they will never consider the fact that the feature may not be needed at all.

---

### Post by Erunno on 2007-02-25
> **IYY said:**
> Any usability expert will tell you that adding features should be done with *extreme* caution. The reason being that once a feature is there, removing it will greatly annoy users (actually, that's a mistake that Gnome sometimes makes, and I criticize them for it).

Actually, the only case I can think of where a user would be annoyed by the removal of a feature is when he actually used it prior to the removal. In my personal experience, which may or may not be a general phenomenon, the typical user just ignores features he does not understand. For example, my parents own a VCR which has a ton of additional functionality which goes beyond the basic function of recording and playing video cassettes. They don't need the extra functionality at all (I might add at this point that they bought this overprized monster without consulting me first) but they never had problem to access the basic functionality as the visual cues on the standard buttons are more or less the same on all VCRs.

On the other hand there's probably someone out there who really appreciates the additional functionality and needs it to be get work done / be happy. It's extremely difficult to pin down a subset of all features to put into a common desktop as there's no famous typical user and the ussage pattern varies from person to person.

I actually was always very uncomfortable about the reasoning that features are removed because the "casual user" (again, pretty undefined to my knowledge) doesn't need it. A more professional user is still a part of the user set and shouldn't be punished for that by making advanced features inaccessible or combined with more hurdles to overcome.

> Konqueror being both a file manager and a browser. The developer thinks: "I have developed a web browser, but I can also make it manage files. This adheres to what I learned in my programming classes, since I'd be making the program more flexible, generic and reusable. It is a good feature." While it may be controversial or offensive to suggest this, I also believe that the programmer had the following thought: "Windows has one application for browsing the web and managing files; if they managed to do it, surely we can too!"

Konqueror is acutally much more (and at the same time much less ;-) ) than just a file and web browser but that's a little beside the point. It is an extremely powerful tool for advanced users which actually adds to productivity and not detracts from it. For instance, I use the kparts technology in Konqueror to a great extent: When I'm working I have tabs open with PDF files, FTP shares, various directories, web sites and documents in the katepart. I use different instances of Konqueror for different subjects with their associated documents / data. Splitting Konqueror (ingnoring KParts here) will simply make people like me less productive and would add to to "clutter" on my desktop from dozens of open application instances.

Anyway, the KDE developers seem to share the sentiment that there should be a more focused program for file management hence Dolphin will probably become the default file manager of KDE (at least Dolphin will have a less crowded configuration ;-) ). That's fine with me as no functionality is removed and different users can decide which of the applications (friendly wink to the Krusader crowd) fits his personal usage pattern best.

> 
What the developer did not take into consideration is that Konqueror is not a programming project, but an *interface* project. What he should have been thinking was: "The mindset of the user while browsing the web is entirely different than the mindset of the user when managing files. This implies that there should be two different tools for the tasks. While the two do share some similarities (a notion of backwards and forwards, a home location, bookmarks), the differences in interaction are so vast that the apparent similarity would only *confuse* the user (the notion of up in addition to back and forward, the home location being the home folder rather than the home webpage, bookmarks being used for different purposes). Also, most features of the application in browser mode will be useless in manager mode, and vice versa, further confusing the user. The conclusion: while it would be a nice technical accomplishment, from a usability standpoint the two applications should remain separate."

I disagree partly as explained above. Different users, different usage patterns. And can somehow provide hard data (as in usability studies which explain the metholodgy of the test, background of the testers, aspects tested etc) what actually confuses a users. This whole confuse stuff gets thrown around quite a lot but so to my knowledge (maybe I just visit the wrong sites) lacks any backup in form of scientific data. Plus, can somebody give me specific outline of the "average user" persona ?

> 
The way things are right now, both Gnome and KDE have problems, and their problems are in fact one and the same: stubbornness and pride. KDE developers are too stubborn and proud to accept the criticism of a usability expert, whereas the Gnome developers are too stubborn to accept the criticism of the software engineer or common user. The problem is not that KDE developers don't value usability at all, but they just value it less than robustness, functionality and technical merits. So when a new feature is contemplated, they will consider where to place it, but they will never consider the fact that the feature may not be needed at all.

Incorrect insofar as the KDE developers are working with openusability for KDE 4 and are in the process of developing their own HIG which hopefully will not need to fall back on removing functionality from the UIs but find a comprehensible solution. So they did listen to the constant complains from KDE users (like me) about the default UI. As how fast and to what extent they will be adopted by the application developers remains to be seen but the feedback on some of the developer blogs has been positive so far and the KDE HIG people are doing a lot of lobbying work to convince the unbelievers :-P

### Post by IYY on 2007-02-25
> Actually, the only case I can think of where a user would be annoyed by the removal of a feature is when he actually used it prior to the removal. 

This is obviously true. However, when you look at users as a *group*, removal of features is extremely harmful. It only takes one feature that you used to be removed for you to lose a huge amount of trust for the developers. This is why all experts recommend to think very carefully before adding new features.

> In my personal experience, which may or may not be a general phenomenon, the typical user just ignores features he does not understand. For example, my parents own a VCR which has a ton of additional functionality which goes beyond the basic function of recording and playing video cassettes. They don't need the extra functionality at all (I might add at this point that they bought this overprized monster without consulting me first) but they never had problem to access the basic functionality as the visual cues on the standard buttons are more or less the same on all VCRs.

This is not exactly what the research shows. Of course, you are right to an extent: they will ignore the features and stick to the basics. But as a designer of a robust interface, your goal is not to make the users feel so confused by the features that they stick to a very narrow and limited range of functions and ignore the rest. You should design your interface in a way that makes the user feel like he understands the system, and is not afraid to learn new things. Your VCR is actually a good example. When talking to the salesperson in the store, your parents see the extra features as good and important things. However, once they bring the VCR home, they end up using in the same way as they use a $20 VCR. The hundreds of dollars have been wasted, and features lie there unused and intimidating. 

> On the other hand there's probably someone out there who really appreciates the additional functionality and needs it to be get work done / be happy. It's extremely difficult to pin down a subset of all features to put into a common desktop as there's no famous typical user and the ussage pattern varies from person to person.

I don't understand why people (and developers) keep repeating things that have been shown over and over by experts to be false. While designing a device, you *have to* choose what features to put in. You can't just skip this step and put *everything* in it.

> I actually was always very uncomfortable about the reasoning that features are removed because the "casual user" (again, pretty undefined to my knowledge) doesn't need it. A more professional user is still a part of the user set and shouldn't be punished for that by making advanced features inaccessible or combined with more hurdles to overcome.

I do agree with the statement that features should never be removed. Ideally, these features should not be there to begin with. 

To be perfectly honest, I don't think that either KDE *or* Gnome have the right idea. KDE has the power features all over the place, hoping that casual users will ignore them (this just goes against every possible rule of design). Gnome simply doesn't include the power features, making a good design, but less functionality.

The ideal design would have the advanced features completely disabled by default, and make it fairly difficult to enable them (buried in some obscure menu). The logic being that the power user will find the way to enable it anyway, but the feature won't be there to confuse the casual user, and that user won't feel pressured into constantly tweaking the exact feature set to his liking. 

I am not fully familiar with Gnome's future plan, but it is possible that they are going in this direction. If I was a Gnome team leader right now, here is what my plan would be:

1. Remove all power features and keep only the basics, designed elegantly. (this is where most users complain, and it is almost complete).
2. Start adding power features, but in a way that does not disrupt the overall design and doesn't get in the way. 

This way, the system gets redesigned from the ground up, adhering to design and usability principles. A mistake that Gnome could do is stop at step 1, but I don't think that's their plan. 

> 
I disagree partly as explained above. Different users, different usage patterns. And can somehow provide hard data (as in usability studies which explain the metholodgy of the test, background of the testers, aspects tested etc) what actually confuses a users. This whole confuse stuff gets thrown around quite a lot but so to my knowledge (maybe I just visit the wrong sites) lacks any backup in form of scientific data. Plus, can somebody give me specific outline of the "average user" persona ?

I don't know of such websites, but I have read several books on the subject. Don Norman's book, The Design of Everyday Things is a decent introduction (here are 3 chapters from it: [http://www.cs.wisc.edu/~konwinsk/ui.html)](http://www.cs.wisc.edu/~konwinsk/ui.html)). You could also try The Inmates are Running the Asylum by Alan Cooper. Note that most research in this field are done by psychologists and not computer scientists. I do not claim to be an expert on the subject (I'm a programmer myself), but so far, every article I have read goes against my programmer's intuition, and against KDE's style.

> 
Incorrect insofar as the KDE developers are working with openusability for KDE 4 and are in the process of developing their own HIG which hopefully will not need to fall back on removing functionality from the UIs but find a comprehensible solution. So they did listen to the constant complains from KDE users (like me) about the default UI. As how fast and to what extent they will be adopted by the application developers remains to be seen but the feedback on some of the developer blogs has been positive so far and the KDE HIG people are doing a lot of lobbying work to convince the unbelievers 

Until I see a working program, I do not consider this to truly be relevant. I don't believe they will have the guts to remove any features. If indeed the KDE boys are doing what you claim they are doing, KDE and Gnome should converge at some point in the near future and be approximately equal in robustness and usability. But so far, it's just promises from KDE.

### Post by Erunno on 2007-02-26
Quoting madness ! :) 

> **IYY said:**
> This is obviously true. However, when you look at users as a *group*, removal of features is extremely harmful. It only takes one feature that you used to be removed for you to lose a huge amount of trust for the developers. This is why all experts recommend to think very carefully before adding new features.

Sure, but that's the point which I fail to understand: There's obviously a market for this particular undefined feature so I don't see the reason not to add it and I find it hard to  accept the reason that not everybody will use it and therefore it should be omitted: Why should the usage pattern of people who don't need a particular feature be valued higher than those who do ? After all, the person who doesn't need it is free to ignore it while at the same time there's now way to conjure a missing feature if a user needs it. That's the whole problem about this feature mess: You'll never find a subset of features which fits all users and in the end you're making the desktop undesireable for certain gropus and as unbelievable as it might sound even some "advanced" users like to work with the GUI ;-)

There's always the alternative to split functionality into different programs with a basic version and an extended version with addiotional functionality but this probably will only ease, not solve, the problem that the basic version will probably not cover even all the needs of (for better lack of an alternative called) "average user" and you have now 2 applications to maintain (or more when you go for an even finer granulation) and the burden to explain to the user that the professional version exists in the first place and has to be installed. In the end you'll have 2 programs where one program is a true subset of the other which seems quite redundant to me.

> 
This is not exactly what the research shows. Of course, you are right to an extent: they will ignore the features and stick to the basics. But as a designer of a robust interface, your goal is not to make the users feel so confused by the features that they stick to a very narrow and limited range of functions and ignore the rest. You should design your interface in a way that makes the user feel like he understands the system, and is not afraid to learn new things. Your VCR is actually a good example. When talking to the salesperson in the store, your parents see the extra features as good and important things. However, once they bring the VCR home, they end up using in the same way as they use a $20 VCR. The hundreds of dollars have been wasted, and features lie there unused and intimidating. 

I doubt my parents want to learn how to cut videos on their VCR :-P VCRs and most other hardware has the advantage that it's available in a myriad of versions on a market ranging from versions for people who have problems using the light switch to people who'll remove the case and start replacing the ROM chips ;-) Sometimes I feel the GNOME way of handlings things would be to only allow the $20 VCR and banish all other modells from the market as their "average user" persona will never need them anyway. So, carrying over the VCR anology to desktops seems impractical as it would require different versions of the same software (see above).

> 
I don't understand why people (and developers) keep repeating things that have been shown over and over by experts to be false. While designing a device, you *have to* choose what features to put in. You can't just skip this step and put *everything* in it.

I do agree with the statement that features should never be removed. Ideally, these features should not be there to begin with.

It's just a repitition of the above but I'm a quote-whore ;-) 

No matter how careful you select the standard features you'll always find someone who'll be missing one particular feature, even from the camp of the so-called non-advanced. The crux of removing / not adding them. Design by the lowest common dominator seems a dead end to me as I'm sure you'll find persons who don't even use bookmarks.

> 
The ideal design would have the advanced features completely disabled by default, and make it fairly difficult to enable them (buried in some obscure menu). The logic being that the power user will find the way to enable it anyway, but the feature won't be there to confuse the casual user, and that user won't feel pressured into constantly tweaking the exact feature set to his liking.

Zing, again my problem: Don't punish the advanced users by making advanced features hidden away in an obscure place (eg gnome-conf). Plus, hiding them doesn't exactly invite the user to learn about them in the first place as suggested by you above. KDE has a nice feature (called "What's this?") that actually explains each widget/button/etc if you're interested in it. Too bad the documentation is rather incomplete but I like the direction as it supports the natural curiosity of most people and doesn't force the user to open a seperate documentation.


> 
I am not fully familiar with Gnome's future plan, but it is possible that they are going in this direction. If I was a Gnome team leader right now, here is what my plan would be:

1. Remove all power features and keep only the basics, designed elegantly. (this is where most users complain, and it is almost complete).
2. Start adding power features, but in a way that does not disrupt the overall design and doesn't get in the way. 


You'll have to do 1 and 2 simultaniously as I doubt most users will stick around until the features that once had been there are included again (which, as history shows, can take years). Just remember the outcry when a lot of features got removed in 2.x GNOME.

> 
I don't know of such websites, but I have read several books on the subject. Don Norman's book, The Design of Everyday Things is a decent introduction (here are 3 chapters from it: [http://www.cs.wisc.edu/~konwinsk/ui.html)](http://www.cs.wisc.edu/~konwinsk/ui.html)). You could also try The Inmates are Running the Asylum by Alan Cooper. Note that most research in this field are done by psychologists and not computer scientists. I do not claim to be an expert on the subject (I'm a programmer myself), but so far, every article I have read goes against my programmer's intuition, and against KDE's style.

Thanks for the recommendation, I'll try to read the book by Alan Cooper before the next ice age. :-)

> 
Until I see a working program, I do not consider this to truly be relevant. I don't believe they will have the guts to remove any features. If indeed the KDE boys are doing what you claim they are doing, KDE and Gnome should converge at some point in the near future and be approximately equal in robustness and usability. But so far, it's just promises from KDE.

My god, I surely hope they don't remove any feature. I doubt many people (including me) want a qt-based GNOME. Instead I'm hoping for a more consistent layout of each application (dialgoues, menues, toolbar layouts etc) so that you may apply your knowledge about the GUI to most programs. One of the changes (I think inherited from qt4) which is already visible are the new toolbars with bigger buttons with more spacing between them and text descriptions beneath each button. The biggest obstacle will be to organize the features so that the most-used are easier accessible than the less-used.

In the end we're talking about the default configuration. While streamlining it I hope they improve the handling of customizing applications in case somebody needs to do some changes to the UI (the toolbar configuration is a serious offender in my oppinion) as (sounds like a mantra, eh ?) you'll never be able to pin down the Perfect User Interface (tm) which fits all users.

### Post by Erunno on 2007-02-26
> **deanlinkous said:**
> But a advanced user IS already a user so no need to attract them, new users is what you should design for to attract them, no?

Not necessarily in terms of being a Linux user, as an new user who's already familiar with computers might be coming over from another OS. If you're always looking at the users you don't have and develop for them you're running the risk of driving away the users you have. I also fail to see how cutting features or hiding them away in obscure places is actually attracting new users in general, you're only reaching a subset of users and not necessarily all potential users out there. I haven't given up hope yet that it's possible to serve the needs of novice and advanced users in one desktop environment y that's why I'm anxiously waiting for the release of KDE4. I'm actually an advocate for basic defaults (especially in newbie-centric distributions like Ubuntu) but it should not exclude users who are in need of more functionality than the person who only browses a bit and reads emails (see Konqueror vs Dolphin) and put only as few obstacles as possible into their way to depart from the default.

That's the attitude I encounter repeatedly and that I dislike immensly. Advanced user probably CAN use command line and gconf-editor but the question remains whether they should be forced to do so just because they need additional functionality. They're also users who probably enjoy nice self-explanatory GUI options too. Advanced users and novice users needs are not mutually exclusive in my opinion.

---
