### Post by paperBag on 2007-08-09
I just wanted to see what people's opinions and past experiences are. First off, I don't want to install both, I don't see much point in that. I'm using Gnome right now, and like it, but engineer friends of mine just rave about KDE. Personally, I chose Gnome for the simple fact that I like their philosophy: Usability (which is a major field of interest of mine, hence Ubuntu). And while it may not be the most FOSS-friendly thing to say, I really like the fact they have a corporate backing (empowering enterprises through OSS is just totally rockin').

While it doesn't seem like it would be much of a hassle, it seems that most of the great software was built for KDE, so I slightly feel like I'm missing out a bit (and am too lazy ATM to make things work).

So, the question of the day, err...night rather, is which environment you find to be: more intuitive and usable, user friendly, better designed, but most importantly, a better fit for you, and WHY.

I'm looking for a more philosophical discussion, and less of the "Gnome/KDE is much prettier" unless you find that to be the reason you prefer it. So let me know, I'm very interested to hear people's thoughts on the subject. Oh, and please no radical flaming please ;)

### Post by GeneralZod on 2007-08-09
Huge KDE fan here, although this hasn't always been the case: it took me quite a while to get used to it, but once I discovered how well it all worked under the hood, and the ubiquity of technology such as kio_slaves amongst its apps, I was utterly hooked.  From a programming point of view, it's really quite a piece of work: gigantic, but for the most part unusually well-written and organised (when, in the Coverity scans it managed to get down to 0 defects, the next largest project to do this was *one tenth* the size), and far more efficient than people give it credit for.  It's devotion to sound software engineering principles like the One and Once Only rule is very impressive in a project of its scale.  Its base toolkit, Qt, is much the same: extremely comprehensive, well-designed and very developer friendly.  

I should probably point out that I'm more a fan of the KDE *project* than the Desktop Environment itself: it's strong points for me are the excellent foundations; the vigorous, committed, fun and free-spirited developer community; and the great apps that arise when these two are combined.  Watching such an old and huge project - which, according to conventional wisdom, should really have ossified into boring conservativeness by now - re-invent itself by tearing itself up from the roots for KDE4 is rather awe-inspiring.  The Desktop itself is much less impressive to me, although Plasma looks at least like a solid, if not radical, improvement.

The KDE project is not without its flaws: the defaults still suck to a mind-boggling degree and I think its going to be a while before they get the thorough going over that they so badly need, and the usability mailing list seems (rather disturbingly) to have become a ghost town over the last couple of months, but it still seems to me that they have gotten far more Right than they have Wrong, and more Right's than the competing projects.

Anyway, </gushing> :)

[PS: in a forum that prefers GNOME to KDE by a factor of 2 or 3 to 1, you're not likely to get very unbiased responses ;)]

### Post by igor54 on 2007-08-09
I started on a command prompt!
As a crusty old fart, I tried all the great desktop environments, like X. Then I got real hip, and tried out FVWM95. Nearly sucked my eyeballs out! All that glitter & glamour!
Anyhoo, 20 years later, I get back into *nix, and what do I find - the same danged arguments , over the same danged things! 
We had the shell wars (zsh still rocks!), but BASH won.
We had the X wars, and XFree86 won (yay!)
We had the window manager wars, and WE'RE STILL HAVING THEM!!!
What the hell, I use whatever is in front of me at the time. I don't really care for fancy trimmings - I want something I can work with.
The other day, I loaded Feisty on my laptop, standard GNOME interface(glad to see they dropped that horrible Ximian name, dickheads couldn't even spell the word for "crude & ape-like" properly!), but I didn't care! the first thing I did was find Eterm in the packet management system, and install that! there is where I spend most of my time - what it's running on is really irrelevant to me, as long as i have "my" terminal, I can do my thing :)

Personally, (as if it mattered), I actually preferred WindowMaker as an environment.
So, I've now contributed absolutely nothing to this particular debate, but I feel a whole lot better :)

Regards
Igor

### Post by Burbruee on 2007-08-09
When I first started using linux about 5 years backin time with what I think was Red Hat 7.1 it used KDE. Same with Mandrake8 which I started using shortly after.

But after that I have almost only used linux as a server, until recently when I found out about ubuntu and started using linux again on my desktop computer.
Ubuntu uses Gnome as default and it was the first time that I have used Gnome for a longer period of time.. 
I started using it, and I really liked it.

Last week, just for fun I tried out a 'sudo apt-get install kubuntu-desktop' just to check out what has happened with KDE since version 3.0.

Well, I fell in love with KDE again and now here I am.. Running KDE.
From the KDE website I found out about yakuake, feels really nice to bring up the terminal at any given time just by pushing my F12 key.
Lots of other stuff I like with KDE, such as the control panel and all the applications it includes, such as ktorrent, kopete and k3b mentioned above, which in general I seem to like more than the alternatives from Gnome.

Didn't mean to write this much, anyway.. My vote to KDE! :)

Other than that, I really like fluxbox. ;)

### Post by Kitsun on 2007-08-09
I'm currently stuck between GNOME and KDE, The main problem with KDE is that its interface is too different from the "average" feel of interfaces of OSes, so programs like Firefox and such don't "click" together as well as they do when used in GNOME.

The majority of my decision is made up of little things, for example, on KDE I can close windows by double clicking their icon in top-left corner of the window, but cant do that in GNOME, but in GNOME I can restore windows by dragging them from a maximized state, cant do that in KDE.

Another thing is that I don't like having features installed that I'm never going to use, I can't part with many of Firefox's extensions, and KDE has the browser built-in to its file manager.

I do like KDE's customisability, GNOME gets too restrictive (what?! cant change screensaver settings!).

Also GNOME seems better supported by Ubuntu, just compare ubuntu's website to kubuntu, also things like codec downloading in its media player is not there.

I also found KDE's dialog boxes to resize themselves to often, really annoying.

KDE4 looks very promising tho.

### Post by Matakoo on 2007-09-01
> **PmDematagoda said:**
> You are right about this, but if really compare both KDE and Gnome side by side you see that KDE is not as developed as Gnome

I beg to differ. I consider KDE to be much more developed in just about every aspect, and especially usability. To be honest, the file selector alone is enough for me to dislike gnome and/or gtk.  Unfortunately,  in my experience it is  impossible to have a fully working desktop  without having  components from both Gnome  and KDE installed (or gtk/qt when the programs use those frameworks even if not being dependent on the rest of the DE).

For example, I use KDE as my DE. But I have found nothing that beats Evolution for my e-mail needs. Or firefox for browsing. And when in a Gnome environment, I miss Konqueror (as a file manager), K3B, and Amarok as the most obvious ones.

I wouldn't mind mixing apps and frameworks like that if it wasn't for the fact that the apps sometimes show odd behaviour or just looks odd when in the "wrong" environment.  Again, the file selector sticks out. Then again, I'm a big fan of consistency.

And this may be just me, but I think KDE in general is better at integrating Gnome apps than the other way around.

How I would love it if more apps were separated into an engine part and a user interface part...for example, this would be great if divided into packages:

*gimp-common
*gimp-gtk-interface
*gimp-qt-interface

Every gimp user would need the first, and based on your DE preferences you would need one of the other two.

### Post by Matakoo on 2007-09-01
> **KoolPenguin said:**
> I also found that the user support forums and guides were better for gnome and in particular ubuntu. 

THAT is certainly true. A lot of the guides seem to take for granted that you use Gnome, which isn't so surprising considering that Ubuntu is primarily a gnome-centric distro. Still, I wouldn't say that is necessarily a problem. In some cases, it is only a matter of substituting kate for gedit. In yet others, the solution is DE agnostic if you read between the lines (granted, in those cases you need to be at least an intermediate user if you want to see beyond the gnome-centric approach described).

And in some cases, it shows that Ubuntu is gnome-centered. Case in point: the "This format is unsupported. Click here to download the codec". Maybe just me, but I never got that to work in Kubuntu. It's not hard to install such things (in my opinion, YMMV) but I still hope it will be fixed for Gutsy or Heron.

Still, it doesn't really show which DE is better. It just shows how the distro in question prioritize things.

But I still stick with Gnome because of it's graphics and conserving battery power is not a necessity on a desktop. 

But the way I see it Xfce will be used more on laptops in favour of KDE and Gnome and KDE provided it keeps developing itself will take over Gnome, but who know's Gnome may suddenly make themselves much better than KDE through some miracle, only time can say. KDE and Gnome are at a draw when it comes to the desktop but on a laptop, Gnome is the clear winner because on my laptop Compaq V3005TU, Gnome worked better than KDE did, Gnome detected the quick play buttons and knew how to use them with ease whereas KDE needs some work for that to occur.
So therefore my decision still lies with Gnome though it has been swayed towards KDE and XFCE as well.:)

### Post by mojoman on 2007-09-02
> **PmDematagoda said:**
> I never tried Xfce, what is it's emphasis on? Gnome is user-friendly, KDE is power and correct me if I am wrong but Xfce emphasises on lightness doesn't it, can you run powerful applications on it? By the way how is Debian, easy to use?

Can you run powerful applications on Xfce? Well, imagine that your computer has 1048 MB of ram and Gnome or KDE requires, say, 300 MB and Xfce requires, say, 150 MB. Which desktop would be best suited to run powerful applications on, or in the words of Ali G, "you do da math"? (On my desktop, a freshly booted system under Gnome used, according to 'free', around 350 MB and Xfce requires around 170.) So, Xfce is less resource-demanding and thus free up more resources for other applications. If one has the strange notion that a computer could and maybe even should be used for something more than to run a desktop environment, a light desktop environment is a pretty good idea (but try telling that to someone who's using Vista...). Of course, in the extra memory usage on Gnome and KDE there are libs that are used by other applications as well and if you use these applications under Xfce those libs will be loaded too (which they already are under Gnome and KDE) but (1) there are plenty of good alternatives to these applications so you could use other, and (2) even if you don't like the alternatives these libs doesn't require a staggering 100-150 MB. So, Xfce is lighter.

Compared to Gnome it is also sports more configurablility and it does not use metacity as a windowmanager (try using metacity with dual monitors under twinview and you'll see what I mean). As for KDE, I haven't used it that much (I can't get over the silly K- thing on the name of the applications). :wink: So, Xfce gets my vote.

ps: couldn't all of the 300 threads on this subject be merged? ds.

### Post by mojoman on 2007-09-02
> **PmDematagoda said:**
> Well this debate is for the major Desktops, but it will be worth bringing in the small ones as well, how are these desktops? Have you got any favorites?
How did you install them by the way?

Fluxbox and Enlightenment (and tons of others) are not desktops environments but windowmanagers. Fluxbox and Enlightenment are blindning fast and highly configurable. I use Fluxbox on my old laptop and it's just amazing how good it works. I sometimes use it on my desktop as well because it's so damned good. To install them both, do
```

sudo apt-get install fluxbox enlightenment
```

You will probably be recommended to install some other packages as well (menu, fluxconf and what more) but they are really tiny. I've hardly used Enlightenment at all but I can really recommend Fluxbox.

---

### Post by por100pre1 on 2007-09-02
I like Fluxbox, i use it with Rox-Filer for file browsing. **Needs some configuration job to be done.** [Fluxbox Documentation]("http://fluxbox.sourceforge.net/docbook/en/html/") is very helpful for that. Here's [how to configure Idesk]("http://idesk.sourceforge.net/wiki/index.php/Idesk-usage"). Here's my installation with Rox-Filer,  Idesk (for putting icons in the Desktop), and fbpager:

```
sudo aptitude install fluxbox rox-filer fbpager idesk menu fluxconf fbdesk
```

Then create the Fluxbox menu:

```
sudo update-menus
```

Finally look for Fluxbox at the gdm login menu. :)

### Post by miggols99 on 2007-09-02
I think that most people here will say Gnome because Ubuntu is very Gnome centric and has all the best features on it. Kubuntu will be getting a restricted drivers manager in Gutsy, so it seems it's behind. Some good KDE centric distros are:

PCLinuxOS - Nice looking, but RPM based. If you don't like RPMs, don't use it. Synaptic is included though! Control centre for setting up ATI/Nvidia drivers and Beryl.
Sabayon - Based on Gentoo, but a lot of packages are binary so you don't have to have a weekend of building.  A bit slow.
Arch Linux with KDEmod - KDEmod is the fastest, best looking KDE implementation I have ever seen. Hard to set up, but is really easy to maintain. What I'm using now :)

### Post by jamaisvu on 2007-09-12
> **Wardhog said:**
> Is there some kind of brief breakdown somewhere of the pros and cons of KDE and Gnome?

[http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/kdegnome](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/kdegnome) is a fairly nice relatively unbiased comparison.

There is also a fairly comprehensive article by Bruce Byfield at: [http://itmanagement.earthweb.com/article.php/3671906](http://itmanagement.earthweb.com/article.php/3671906)

Otherwise, I'd really suggest searching linuxtoday.com, or even just Googling likely terms. You'll probably stumble across several "I've tried my non-preferred DE for a month and..." articles, which I suppose are vaguely amusing in a way, although they tend to end up demonstrating that human taste is a funny thing.

If you really really want to see flamewar inducing stuff, here are a couple of favourites from the KDE side (I'm sure there are equally fun contributions from GNOMEophiles somewhere):
[Linus Torvalds' infamous "GNOME users are idiots" message]("http://mail.gnome.org/archives/usability/2005-December/msg00021.html")
[Desktop Memory Usage]("http://ktown.kde.org/%7Eseli/memory/desktop_benchmark.html") (sounds wonderful until you realize that in most cases it's utterly trivial)

### Post by RussianVodka on 2007-09-15
From my experience KDE runs quicker, looks nicer, and has better native applications (most of the time). Unfortunately unlike Gnome, it's very hard to do some simple things in KDE. Like if you plug in a USB hard drive in Gnome, it will load up automatically, in KDE you will have to mess around with settings and group permissions for a long time.

Well, at least that's what it's like in (K)ubuntu. Other KDE-native distros may provide a different experience.

### Post by aysiu on 2007-09-15
> **RussianVodka said:**
> Unfortunately unlike Gnome, it's very hard to do some simple things in KDE. Like if you plug in a USB hard drive in Gnome, it will load up automatically, in KDE you will have to mess around with settings and group permissions for a long time. I have a feeling you may be one of the few people who have had such an experience. The configuration dialogue for KDE's management of devices is certainly not as straightforward as Gnome's, but you shouldn't have to mess with group permissions. If it's NTFS, it should mount read-only in *both* Gnome and KDE. If it's FAT32, it should mount as read/write in *both* Gnome and KDE. And if it's Ext3, the permissions set on the drive will be read the same way in *both* Gnome and KDE.

Can anyone else confirm that RussianVodka's experience here is atypical?

---

### Post by x1a4 on 2007-09-15
> **Zdravko said:**
> Aaaaaaa, no! I am not going to read that many pages! I want a clean and correct answer! Tell me also, what is more developed and stabilized!

It all depends on your personal preference.  Personally I use Xfce as my primary GUI, though I sometimes, and some of my system's users as well, use AfterStep (augmented with WindowMaker features) which is very nice and stable.  I also like to use FVWM-Crystal and Sawfish (augmented with FluxBox features) is the default GUI I use for the root account.  

GNOME, KDE, Xfce and others are all well developed and stable.  Neither is better or worse.  They're simply different.
